Name: Joker
Review: Uneasy, disturbing, chilling. Phillip’s Joker is a well crafted exploration of how a marginalised person in society can crack under the constant oppression they face in the world. The entire movie is a showcase of Joaquin Phoenix’s incredible performance as the titular character, from the laugh alone Phoenix deserves to be accredited with some award. The emotion that is poured into every scene breaks your heart and you are found questioning how you should treat this character, is he deserving of sympathy? Aside from Joker himself, every single person in this movie is outstanding but especially Robert DeNiro, which every scene he’s in you’re enthralled. The score of the film is equally incredible, adding eryness to ordinary scenes, which leaves a constant uneasy feeling in you. In addition the music used throughout the film is poignant and somehow manages to make otherwise happy music insurmountably creepy. The story is complete and for the most part successfully shows the slow, downward spiral that leads to the breaking point of someone who lacks support from society yet this is where my small criticism lies with the film. Throughout the film there is a distinct focus on the mental health of Phoenix’s Arthur Fleck which ultimately I feel is fleshed out enough, there are subtleties to his past which explain the starting point of our main character’s mental state but never fully lands on a distinct message for the treatment of mental illness. Then there is the political message where the class system is examined and again the mistreatment of those marginalised by society is emphasised. The overall idea being that media puts a message behind events, they can’t just happen, they have to happen for a reason, such as a statement on the treatment of the working class, and on the whole the movie does a good job showing this. Yet my problem lies with where these two messages are supposed to combine, the whole message of Joker is not political nor is it to make a statement, it’s simply a deranged man getting back at his oppressors and while this is expressed explicitly in the film, I can’t help but feel the ending, no spoilers, turns this on it’s head and tries to add a message to his madness. Having said this, Joker is an exquisite piece of cinema from the performances and music to the incredible cinematography and aesthetic of Gotham, captured perfectly. Joker isn’t a film about the Joker, at it’s core it’s a film about Arthur Fleck and how he decended into madness, to compare this to the likes of Heath Ledger’s Joker would be unfair as they’re completely different characters but both incredible in their own ways. While I did have a small problem with the overall message of the film, it by no means overshadows the film, it simply doesn’t mesh cohesively despite the parts being there. In sum, Joker is a great addition to the superhero genre and hopefully allows for more creative ventures in this genre. And that laugh though.

Name: Proxima
Review:
GFF FILM #1

Proxima, while a beautiful tale of the relationship between mother and daughter and of a woman succeeding and achieving her dreams despite obstacles that were placed in front of her, suffers from some confusing characterisation and cinematography leaving the film to be less impactful that is desired.

The positives first off, Eva Green is delightful in this film and beautifully portrays a mother struggling to maintain a close relationship with her daughter while also trying to make advances as her job as an astronaut and is able to make the audience invest in both parts of her life as, she herself is so invested in them. Also the girl is great and helps deliver some fairly emotional moments in the film. The choice of the film to be in multiple languages as well was a beautiful choice which parallels the joint collaboration of the space mission they’re doing.

I have some gripes with the film though, Matt Dillon’s character is extremely poorly written. Slight spoilers here, when we’re first introduced to him he just appears to be your classic misogynist, with some disturbing comments throw at Green. Later in the film he begins to be kind to Green and she seems charmed and then out of nowhere we are to believe they’re close friends. All in all his character was just lost and whatever he was needed to be at the time in the film. A little issue I had was the use of GoPro and phone footage that was blown up to the big screen was unnecessary and didn’t do anything to progress the film, it simply felt jarring. Another aspect of the film that felt jarring was the nudity, in a way this film could’ve been a sweet family movie but there is explicit language and nudity that both do nothing for the film and feel out of place.

If you can get over some of these little gripes though underneath there a sweet tale of family that exists and while it’s not redefining cinema it’s a pleasant watch.


PROXIMA is out in UK and Irish cinemas from 8 May

Also the nerd in me freaked out to know a real life Star City exists.

Name: Les Misérables
Review:
GFF #2

A politically charged film centering around the morals of the police in France at the time of the riots, with some great acting and interesting cinematography all combining to add to this feel of intemacy and really making you feel as if you're a part of these tumultuous times.

Throughout the film character's actions are ambigously depicted, you're left to decide for yourself how to feel about a certain character's behaviour. This is to support the idea explicitly stated by the quote from Hugo Weaving at the end of the film, yet, while the characters are interesting and unique, they often lack depth. The main character, who is meant to be the best of the cops, acts as an observer and isn't as engaging.

Despite this there is some interesting ideas tackled thoughout the film dealing with morality of the police and of the kids who are growing up in the city and the discrimation of races in France which all build up to a good climatic final 30 minutes.

Name: The Critic
Review:
We are each are own worst critic.

Stella Velon's 'The Critic' is an intriguing and fascinating look at the inner struggles of an artist, how self-doubt and a feeling of imposter syndrome can manifest itself in self destructive manners.

The film tells the story of an inexperienced actress, played by Velon, who, after winning a big award, is being interviewed on her performance. Throughout the film the interviewer, played by Smyth, is trying to provoke a reaction out of the actress and as time goes on asks more deep and personal questions.

The acting in this film is fantastic, Velon plays her role with an emotional depth that is achieved even by the most subtle of looks, with most of film simply being shots of her face, and her reactions, she does an incredible job at pulling the audience into her life and the inner demons she is battling. Smyth also plays the role of the interviewer fantastically, not as someone curious about the actress but someone who has a preconceived notion of her and wishes to break her down. Using the most passive aggressive tone, he plays it more like a therapist trying to coerce the actress into saying what he wants her to hear.

The quality of both the production and the narrative is no small feat either, some indie shorts can feel low-budget or rushed but this film had beautiful cinematography and lighting, like the glorious opening shot of Velon playing her acceptance speech over in her head while gazing into the mirror, and it also allowed the actress time to sit in the moment and let us really see the emotions that are going through her head.

The use of lighting and extreme close-ups on the interviewer makes it overtly obvious that he is acting as a vessel for the actress' own insecurities and is an external manifestation of her self-worth. While heavy-handed in its execution, it is an intriguing narrative device and really allows us to explore the inner struggles the actress is facing.

This device really is an extension of what the film is about, self-worth and what we deem important to us but also the pressures we place upon ourselves and how we react to those pressures. We learn that the actress has a history of substance abuse, and even see some of this in a wonderfully acted and scored scene, where the actress breaks down due to the pressure of the interview, or more likely the pressures she has placed upon herself.

Not to spoil the ending but I think it was a nice, if strange, way to show the importance of film, how real it can feel and how it can reflect struggles faced in people's personal lives.

Name: Wonder Woman 1984
Review: What a mess. Disappointed is putting it lightly, this movie is horrendous. The central message was so confused the entire time and ends on something that is non-sensical. The entire plot is all over the place and weirdly in its attempts to stand up against sexism, is sexist itself. We see Wonder Woman in action for maybe 10% of the movie and Steve ends up saving her? Also love that we need to remind Diana that she will find another man that she can rely on and love cause that’s her purpose. The idea of a nerdy good guy wanting to be attractive and turn evil is also so played out and not great character development. But, the biggest sin of all? Why the fuck would you not use 80s music in an 80s film, the music is misplaced and tonally appalling, I liked the action music enough but Hans Zimmer missed the idea of the score here, the pieces didn’t fit. And who the hell puts classical music over an 80s montage, where’s the Blue Monday? I left this movie upset with DC cause they had the idea of something that could’ve been great. Gal Gadot was still amazing though and Kristen Wigg, for what she was given, was entertaining

Name: Call Me by Your Name
Review: Elio’s mother recites a German tale in the film and in it the question “Is it better to speak or die” is proposed and really it is this question that is the centre of the film and one that the film perfectly explores the difficulty of answering said question. The fear over not telling someone your love for them and the regret that would occur with it, always wondering what if? Vs the pain and sadness felt after experiencing some of that love but knowing that you’ll never feel that love again, it will always be there but never tangible, always out of reach. Michael Stuhlbarg’s monologue at the end was utterly beautiful and having read the book I thought the film had a far better ending. I just wish there was a little more time spent developing Oliver and Elio’s relationship, other than that this film was bellissimo. And damn Italy is beautiful

The thing that truly elevates this movie though is the score, hands down one of the most beautiful scores I’ve heard over the last few years it’s got such a sweetness and love to it while also conveying a deep sadness, Nicholas Britell’s score truly made some scenes for me, it’s just pure and raw emotions played through sound

Name: Malcolm & Marie
Review:
This movie is not without its faults but I think it captures the idea of an argument perfectly. The ebbs and flows, the sudden reconciliation only to be thrust back into argument, the lack of direction and tangents to then stumbling on the real point that you want to cause some damage. The pacing while a little repetitive was so natural, the breaths each of them take between arguments to help themselves take a moment and also to let feelings stew and arguments build up in them. The damage and hurt that can be caused in the heat of the moment, things that you never wanted to say or mean but deep down it was always there, brewing. I thought it all added to a simply perfect exploration of an argument and one so indicative of love and the pain it can cause.

JDW kills it, the raw anger he displays at points and his ability to hold an argument with himself for a prolonged period of time is simply masterful. But Zendaya, damn. I think Euphoria is a perfect show and was excited to hear Sam Levinson and Zendaya were working on a film together and it didn’t disappoint, Zendaya gives a nuanced and raw performance, at points even when she isn’t arguing there’s subtle things she does that let’s you know that her anger and hurt aren’t finished with. Levinson and Zendaya work so well together he clearly knows the best ways to direct her and after listening to her talk on the film it truly seems like a collaborative effort between the two with Zendaya having influence and say on the script and story too.


I still love Levinson’s directing and there was some beautiful long takes in the film, anytime Zendaya took a cigarette break outside I was grateful for the break and the beautiful shot. The in-world music in the film, while on the nose, was beautifully chosen and the lack of score over the arguments really added to the tension between the two. The smart quips and jabs they make at each other were fantastic and intricate monologues were written beautifully but the performances truly elevated them. I mean come on Zendaya giving a monologue on the state of art and the political nature of the Lego Movie *chef’s kiss*

Name: Cold War
Review: Sound design was absolutely beautiful in the film along with the cinematography, possibly one of my favourite shots I’ve seen in a film using a mirror to show a huge crowd and the main couple on the edge having an intimate conversation, absolutely beautiful. What this film does masterfully is that while on the surface it is a story of love, it’s subtly showing the degradation of authenticity within music. Starting with the natural opening where we see town folk signing songs in the country to the audition song, which slowly become less and less authentic by changing genres then language until at the end the singer is a husk of her natural self

Name: High School Musical
Review: Ok had to watch this so made a drinking game out of it with friends, here's some of my thoughts: - Chad has the most traject storyline, he's so engrained in toxic masculinety that he can't do what he loves and mocks Troy for it. Why?? Cause he's in love with Troy, the obvious looks, the constant mockery of his singing and of other people's passions, it's all cause of his represed sexuality - I swear to god that drama teacher says Musicale about a million times - Kelsey tripping over literally nothing and landing just sitting is absurdly funny - I'm sorry but why tf does a teacher have his office in the locker room with the mirrors facing the guys - Vanessa Anne Hudgens is horrific, the lines were delivered like she didn't know how to talk - The hacking in this movie is laughable, like...that's just not how things work - A weirdly good transition between the song and the game?? - Chad cockblocks Troy at the end further supporting the love he has for him - Kelsi has the best (and only good) character arc in the whole film, going from scared girl to standing up to Sharpay was vindication that was great - Speaking of Sharpay why the hell was she at the front of the dance at the end, her and Ryan were great though This was bad but good bad that's fun to watch with people, now onto 2

Name: (500) Days of Summer
Review:
Still in love with this film, it’s style, the comedy and its raw critic of modern love and pop culture. Despite her name being in the title, Summer is really an ancillary character to drive Tom’s journey throughout the film. While some may critic this, I believe it’s gives the opportunity for us to get a movie which is solely a intimate and complex character study, how Tom’s obsession with pop culture has influenced the ideas he’s instilled in himself about love. It’s these ideas juxtaposed with Summer’s beliefs about love that give such an interesting dynamic to the doomed couple.

I noticed this time watching it that in the early stages of there relationship most of the conversations revolve around pop culture; music, movies and celebrities. As times goes on the conversations become more imitate and personal highlighting the incompatibility of the two and ultimately leading to there breakup.

The style in this movie is perfection, nothing is used for the sake of it, everything making this movie hyper stylised is to serve a purpose. The expectations vs reality being such a perfect microcosm for the film.

The ending being sort of open ended I also love, it’s down to the viewer to believe if Tom really learned from Summer of his doomed to repeat the heartbreak again.

Name: Midsommar
Review:
Florence Pugh. Incredible cinematography. Stunning set design.

Aster manages to create an endless and building sense of anxiety throughout Midsommar that keeps you on edge despite the beautiful Swedish backdrop. Florence Pugh’s incredibly captivating performance is much the reason for the sense of dread along with the slow meticulous shots that help build up this fear.

All that being said I have an issue with the characterisation and to certain extent the plot of this film. Many character decisions feel forced or completely pointless, the thesis plot line for example, after what the characters witness that was what Christian took away? It did also feel very forced that the disappearance of the characters is just shrugged off each time with a Dani line like “Where’s blank?”. The overused symbolism and foreshadowing in the film didn’t amount to anything and I think the ending fell a little flat. There was other small gripes here and there with character decisions and plot points but it was still an enjoyable and stressful watch

Name: Billie Eilish: The World's a Little Blurry
Review:
Watched this to justify my Apple TV+ subscription and to my surprise found this documentary incredibly moving. This doc was a deeply personal look at Billie’s life, touching on the subject of mental health quite a bit which was handled with a lot of care. It also managed to capture the growth of this determined and self-critical person throughout their teenage years and truly managed to capture her feelings at times. It was able to relate the audience to the problems she was going through even when she might have seemed arrogant it always came from a good place. 

I’m a fan of Billie’s music but the music placements in this doc were perfect and the directing and cinematography were great with some incredibly cinematic shots especially the final shot with the imagery of an eye as Ocean Eyes played. God bless her family though they’re so down to Earth and understanding, especially her mum, she had some touching scenes and beautiful words about today’s society

Name: WandaVision
Review:
Wanda. Maximoff. Wow this show surpassed my expectations, in the MCU I feel like there’s been 3 distinct, fully realised character arcs: Iron Man, Captain America and Black Widow but I’m happy to add Scarlett Witch to that list. 

Wandavision is truly a tale of grief, loss and suffering and how we decide to deal with those emotions, looking at the idea that if we had the power to rid these feelings would we and should we? Wanda’s journey through the MCU is tragic and I think it took this show to really see how sad and traumatic her story has been. 

Setting the show with the backdrop of sitcoms that usually wrap up perfectly showcasing the “perfect family” was genius but to relate it to Wanda’s childhood was an ingenious touch. In these settings we get the comedy of the classic sitcoms but it also allows for us to be aware and look for the faults in Wanda’s world as she begins to break down the world does to.

The true power of this show is the details. The incredible production design perfectly recreating each sitcom style, watching some behind the scenes things you truly see the attention to detail in costumes, sets and the colouring. The beautiful cinematography and camera work, one thing I noticed in the early episodes is they were mainly shot with static camera like sitcoms from the 50s and 60s until Wanda broke and then the camera would become more dynamic and free to move, getting close-ups of the characters, just another smart small detail that added to the show massively.

The music and sound design in the show was fantastic, using a motif in Agatha’s theme song and hiding it in every theme song in the show was simply incredible. Plus not to mention a lot of those songs were actual tunes. As a project this is some of the smartest directing and writing there has been in the MCU, perfectly crafting a show that was fun to watch but had a true darkness to it, is a testament to the writing team. Episode 8 was especially well written with the line “What is grief if not love persevering?” being a perfect summation of the show and also a truly heartbreaking line.

The show would not be what is was though without the cast, Kathryn Hahn and Paul Bettany were delightful along with Teyonah Parris, Kat Dennings and Randall Park but the true standout of her own show was Elizabeth Olsen. The performance she gave in the show was nothing short of perfect, you could feel the grief and pain behind every persona of Wanda she took on. The longing for her to be happy and simply settle down with a family but deep down knowing that can’t happen, I was captivated by her performance. Not to mention the way she completely transformed into the different sitcom styles of acting, it was truly a tour de force and I can’t wait to see what’s next for Wanda.

I could go on and on about the show it’s oozing with style and substance, Fiege and all at Marvel know what they’re doing and I can’t wait for more of this type of content from them.

Even though it’s a show I’m reviewing Wandavision cause I want my MCU in Review list to be complete on Letterboxd and Fiege said the shows were like 6 hours movies so I’m counting it

Name: BPM (Beats per Minute)
Review:
So I didn’t have much knowledge of the AIDS crisis prior to watching 120 BPM but my god this film expertly shows the devastation and destruction AIDS caused not only only a macroscopic but also microscopic level. Framing the ACT UP movement as a continuation of the French Revolution is ingenious and it places the AIDS crisis in its right place as a tragic part of, not only Paris’ but the world’s history.

The most impressive aspect of the movie is how it manages to capture the community that arises between LGBT+ members. Even in the most awful situations, there is still a joy and comradery between the group, a true love and care for each other that was so beautiful to watch and only makes the ending more upsetting

Name: Booksmart
Review:
Booksmart is one of those films I can stick on when I’m down and it instantly brings me joy. The situation is not only relatable but crafted with such care, it sees a new wave for coming of age films, ones that are relevant to the diverse and multifaceted nature of youth now and casts characters as dimensional human beings instead of being a trope or a stereotype.

Olivia Wilde’s directorial debut is no small feat, it’s a beautiful film to look at and has a freshness to it which makes each watch enjoyable. What I love most about Booksmart is it’s use of smart comedy, I find myself audibly laughing out loud at the humour in the film and what’s so refreshing and fun to watch is that humour is never at the expense of others. The jokes used aren’t poking fun at someone due to some part of the character, such as sexuality or race, as most classic high school movies. It’s humorous in the situations the characters find themselves in and the characters themselves are funny. It’s a modern refresh on a classic story and is a perfect comforting movie

Name: Man of Steel
Review:
So I’m not a massive fan of the DCEU but with the Snyder cut coming, not that I have that high expectations, I’m watching the DC movies since I’ve not seen them in a while. I was looking forward to Man of Steel, a movie which I thought was ok and by no means the worst in the universe but my god this aged poorly.

It’s incredibly poorly directed, any action scene in the film, which unfortunately is most of it, is so badly shot, the spacial awareness for the scenes are completely all over the place. I would say any high energy scene in this film feels rushed and obstructed by the poor shots that it’s not only difficult to understand what’s going on but it’s not enjoyable to watch.

I enjoy Cavill and Adams and they do they’re best with a bad script, I actually think watching this again Lois is better written (marginally) than Sups until she turns into the damsel in distress. Despite the word Hope being mentioned every 10 minutes, Clark seems to go on a journey through anger which could’ve been interesting and an unexpected twist on a character that’s beloved but Sups is so devoid of character development, I mean he barely says anything in the film, that most of the time he comes off as angry for no reason. The moments that do shine are when Cavill and Adams have scenes together, while not stellar they still have a chemistry together and Superman has that charm that we are used to seeing on screen, this can also be seen whenever he shares the screen with MARTHA.

As mentioned, the script is bad... like real bad. I don’t enjoy the plot of this movie, there isn’t really a logical one. Zod could’ve picked any other planet to terraform so why didn’t he, he had to wait 33 years why? We first see Cavill in 3 random jobs until each time he gets discovered, why? Simply to give Lois a plot for discovering him? The plot as a whole is super thin and given much thought seems to break. The film is also thematically a mess, there’s some really interesting threads of themes: the battling ideologies of Jor-El and Zod, the choice to save lives over protecting your own and... hope, I guess. 

The point that gets me the most is his Dad’s death, his dad tells him sometimes you can’t save people’s lives. Ok weird but sure you wanna protect your son. Then dad proceeds to save dog instead of son who can survive the tornado cause why? He’s got to be a man I guess. And then tells his son don’t save me cause of that one thing he said to him that he should protect himself. I’m sorry but Kal-El would’ve saved him.

A part of a film that can make or break it for me is the score, it can make a bad film ok and a good film great. Hans Zimmer’s score made this worse. I like the score, it’s dark and mysterious and got a sinisterness to it, all those words shouldn’t describe a Superman film. They could if they’d earned it and build a story where he is a tragic hero that’s dark but they’re so confused as to who Clark’s character is. The scene that annoyed me the most with this is when Clark first flies, it’s an ok shot scene but Cavill makes it an enjoyable watch, the pure joy on his face with the suit on flying is great and what’s behind it? This sinister score that clashes with the tone of the scene so bad I didn’t know what to feel. It should’ve felt joyous and hopeful, while it did feel grand and big it completely missed the tone on what could’ve been an exciting part of the film.

Man of Steel isn’t awful but it’s not great. There’s some good shots that don’t revolve around action, the acting’s good and some of the action was ok. It makes me appreciate Marvel movies more and makes me sad that executives were desperate for this not to be like Marvel that it made it a tonal mess

Name: Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice
Review:
So this rating is for the Ultimate edition which actually adds Clark’s story instead of cutting it out. I was surprised rewatching this how enjoyable the first half is, it’s not mind blowing but it was fun and there was some great set-ups. And then there was another hour and a half of movie...

So Clark has a character now after Man of Steel where he was a wet blanket, we get to see the nerdy charm of Clark in his job as a reporter, while not over the top, it was a subtle optimism that was a breath of fresh air in the film. But Superman on the other hand was a mess. Sups in this film has the makings of two great characters, two halves that don’t mesh well enough to be a fleshed out character. We have the gritty dark side that continued from Man of Steel but there was more depth to it now in his hate for Batman which while not a traditional take on him could’ve played out well if they’d committed to it. Synder wanted to play both sides though, with more references to hope and being a symbol for the people and it didn’t quite work together for me, resulting in his death being void of emotion for me.

Batman on the other hand was... fine I guess. Him and Bruce were both ok, nothing amazing but nothing really wrong with them. The true MVPs in this film though are the side characters.

Gal Gadot is fantastic in this film, while not in the film a lot, any scene she had with Bruce was electric and had a sense of class to it. Also her entrance is still amazing, the music is fantastic and gives this movie a hype moment it needed. Now, while I love that scene, upon rewatch I noticed something, right after the theme starts to play and we get that get the shot of Wonder Woman we cut to Lois running back to get the staff falling all over the place while that punchy theme plays behind her and honestly it’s hilarious, it takes away all the build up from the scene, really a poor editing choice but it made me laugh.

Amy Adams still manages to act way beyond what the script gives her and in the first half has some interesting journalism story moments. And Jeremy Irons is a perfect Alfred, it’s a weird not typical take on him but his sarcasm and fun relationship with Bruce was so fresh and had a proper energy to it. And while many people don’t like him and there are certainly issues with the plot of his character, I think Jesse Eisenberg did a great job as Lex, he was eccentrically weird and it was enjoyable to watch.

Synder stepped it up with this film, the action is much better shot than in Man of Steel, if sometimes poorly choreographed, the scale was well captured and the power of the heroes and villains was well done. The story does fall flat though, we have a brilliant set up of opposing ideologies between the two titular characters which I wish came to a different climax than we got. Mentions MARTHA and suddenly they’re best friends? I think as soon as we get the scene at the Senate the movie starts to spiral, it doesn’t know what it wants to be. What motivated Lex to put these two against one another? Cause he’s insane? It all felt very thin which is disappoint since they’d set themselves up for a movie that could’ve really explored the internal beliefs of the two characters and put them against each other for a satisfying climatic battle


Also this is the most nitpicky thing but if PBS had a Senator on for an interview they would capitalise their bloody name, drop in respect for Synder due to bad grammar

Name: Suicide Squad
Review: I’m not reviewing this, I spent £3.49 on this since it’s not on streaming services and I’m angry. I didn’t think it was as bad as it was when I saw it in cinemas but this is probably the worst editing I’ve seen in a film

Name: Ingrid Goes West
Review: A real interesting take on social media and the dangers of it, coupled seamlessly with a depiction of mental health that is rarely showcased in media. Everyone is fantastic in the film but Audrey Plaza is truly amazing as she is in everything, Ice Cube Junior was great too and delivers one of the smartest, funniest lines about Batman that I adored. There’s also a hilarious scene between the two of them that’s worth watching the film just for that. Only real gripe is that it was sometimes a bit on the head with the social media stuff and was sometimes tonally confused but otherwise a fun enjoyable watch

Name: Wonder Woman
Review:
Thank god for Wonder Woman, it was a nice break in the DCEU rewatch to get a good film as a palate cleanser. By no means is Wonder Woman incredible but I like to think of it as a phase 1 marvel movie: there’s a great set up, an incredibly likeable main character and weird funny side characters, a predictable love interest, some well directed action and a 3rd act that’s really disappointing.

Gal Gadot makes this movie, she’s magnetic every time she’s on screen and manages to deliver the sometimes poorly written lines with such passion and love it makes them not stand out. I really love the 2nd act of this film, the whole london sequence is hilarious and has some interesting moments surrounding sexism, also Etta Candy is great.

I wish this movie had committed to Diana realising that Ares wasn’t causing the war, it was men and that not all men are good. The movie could’ve still came to the same conclusion that everyone chooses if they’re good or bad and would’ve thematically worked better and rid the movie of the weird twist and CGI battle. Then Ares could’ve been saved for the sequel and maybe made WW84 a watchable movie

Name: Faces Places
Review: The relationship between the two was lovely and the idea of what they were doing was nice, preserving normal people as these works of art. But the film lacked direction and relied on good transitions and forced conversations passed off as natural to make a cohesive through line. The editing in places was very clumsy and some of the people they visited, the stories and sentiment behind it was confused and added no emotional resonance to the film. But the biggest detriment to this film was that it felt very staged, the dialogue in places was not natural at all, it was over dramatised and tried to act as if it was completely organic and for a documentary trying to show a slice of life was a complete distraction

Name: Justice League
Review:
This is a mess. It’s so clear the mix of directors and both had incredibly different views of the project, there’s scenes which carry the consistent darker tone of the universe up till now and then there’s also half a really bad comedic marvel movie in there as well and it ends up being a tonal nightmare. I had to spend money on the DCEU again and while there are some fun moments none of it feels earned or like it should’ve head in the direction it did to give us these moments.

I’m such a big Flash nerd that to see him and Sups face on screen should’ve been a mind blowing moment not a small smile at the end of a film. Sups character in this is completely unrecognisable to the one Synder built up and despite me not enjoying that I would’ve preferred to have a consistency than trying to force Sups to act different to get some jokes.

The editing in the film is so bad along with colouring and the visual effects, for one of the most expensive films ever made this looks awful, and not to mention Cavill’s lips which are even worse watching this again. The writing and directing was a confused mismatch, I didn’t enjoy most sequences, the part where they go build the team was ok I suppose but overall this was far worse than when I first saw it. And the Russian family, really? What a cheap emotional beat that’s as so out of place. I’m looking forward to see Synder’s story and it continue his idea whether it’s good or not it’ll probably be better than this

Name: Shazam!
Review:
It’s not the greatest superhero movie of all time and yes the 3rd act is another generic fight with a villain that’s under developed and is has elements of a cookie cutter origin story but this movie is super endearing and sweet. It’s the first of the DCEU movies to have a consistent theme, of family and appreciating the people who love and support you. The comedy is fun, the action is ok and the whole cast of kids are great, Darla is a standout while Zachery Levi is super charming and great as Shazam.

One nitpick, if you have a big superhero film with a huge budget maybe don’t use the same B-roll footage of the school 3 times in the movie

Name: Aquaman
Review:
This movie was passable at best yet it allowed for some great visuals, the whole underwater aesthetic is crazy and was pulled off which I didn’t expect after seeing how Aquaman and Atlantis were handled in Justice League. The directing is probably the best in the DCEU with the action being fluid, dynamic and fun to watch. But the plot is so generic and at times nonsensical, the war could’ve been interesting but I wasn’t investing in the characters or the world to get the payoff that this movie tries to emulate. The characters are fine, Jason Mamoa is great but everyone else was very plain that it was boring at times to watch. And Oceanmaster’s helmet... how did they get that so wrong and Black Manta’s so right


Also Pitbull... no

Name: Birds of Prey (and the Fantabulous Emancipation of One Harley Quinn)
Review:
It’s nice to have a DCEU film that start to finish was enjoyable all the way through. The stylised aspects of the film work so well with Harley Quinn as a character, the costumes, the set design, especially the amusement park, the exaggerated characters, it all worked in the context of the film.

This has the best cast of all the DCEU films, everyone on screen was magnetic and oozed style and each had some level of dimension to them. Margot Robbie was fantastic she owns the screen every time she’s on it, her jokes are well placed and the action she pulls of is nothing short of amazing. In fact the action in the whole film was brilliant and the last 20 minutes might have some of the best fluid and dynamic action in a superhero film it was really violent and creative and engaging throughout all the fight scenes.

Ewan McGregor is a campy, over-exaggerated brilliance, Black Mask, while having generic motivations, was so fun to watch on screen and didn’t make me dread the villain scenes like most DCEU films. All the birds of prey were great, Black Canary being a standout and all the Huntress jokes hit very well. Cassandra Kane was a great addition too.

Some great directing, visuals, writing and action if the DCEU heads in this direction I’ll be happy, but first bring on the Synder cut...

Name: Zack Snyder's Justice League
Review:
Well... I’m shocked. Rewatching Justice League was a pain, the pacing, the tone, the characters, the action I didn’t enjoy it, it’s an overall mess. I was interesting in seeing a Snyder cut purely just to see Zack’s vision of this darker universe and not the tonal mess that we were giving. But going into this I had low expectations, how different can the movie really be? Oh how wrong I was.

This film is amazing...for what it is. It’s not really a film. It’s not a TV show. It’s a weird piece of art made by Snyder for Snyder. Right from the get go, the cheesy misplaced tone is gone and replaced with the more serious, darker tone we’re used to from DCEU Snyder films. This tone is consistent throughout the film and is the first of Zack’s films where I think it truly works. To the executives at WB who tried to pivot from this tone to be a copy of Marvel, that was a mistake. This film while dark still has fun and light moments but this comes from the characters themselves instead of the components of the film. The Flash being the “comic relief” really works in this film as every other character isn’t trying to be a comic relief, they’re all well defined individuals in this world.

Speaking of The Flash, my boy was given justice in this film. I love The Flash but left Justice League not really loving Ezra’s take on him. In ZSJL we get time to be with Barry, find out who he really is, he’s not used as a weak part of the group but another incredibly important member. His quirks being related to his speed was more apparent in the film and I enjoyed the quips as it was fresh and fun. I got quite emotional in the final battle as well with him reversing time which I never expected one bit to get emotional during this. The whole scene was beautiful and Ezra’s smashed it out of the park.

Another standout for me was Ray Fisher as Cyborg. My god he gets far more screen time in this and it’s earned. The whole story with his father emotional resonated with me and I loved getting more of his backstory. It was tragic and upsetting but Snyder allowed his character to breathe and that payed off in the end. The final battle managed to build tension (which Justice League lacked) through The Flash and Cyborg and adding a touch of emotion to the climax was powerful and well earned.

The other league members were great. I would go as far as saying Affleck might be my favourite Bruce Wayne in this film, I think he played the cocky billionaire incredibly well and enjoyed his dynamic with the rest of the heroes especially Diana. Gal Gadot is still great as usual, what surprised me was how much more I enjoyed the London bank scene with Snyder’s tone. Momoa and Adams are great too and Jeremy Irons is fucking brilliant, I really love his take on Alfred and his quips usually land.

The Superman story beats were well earned and I enjoyed Cavill’s performance in this. I wish I enjoyed the prior movies and the arc of Superman’s character within the DCEU but I think it falls flat. This movie however redeemed him a bit for me, the scene’s with Lois and his Mum were touching and I really enjoyed both his Dad’s VO when he dawned the black suit. But if his character had been built up to be a symbol of hope instead of the confused mess of a character we got then all his scenes would’ve meant a lot more.

What’s incredible is how dramatically different the movie is and I think that’s due to the characterisation and also a few technical things.

1. The music. The score works way better in this film, the ethereal voices for WW and the Amazons and the fusing with the WW theme and the more grand score making it feel like a momentous event. I’ve had my problems with the Man of Steel score, not that I didn’t like it, I love it as a score on its own but felt like it was out of place for that film, but it works perfectly here. It had an emotional charge to it that I didn’t expect but absolutely loved.

2. The colouring. I can’t believe the difference the colouring can make. Snyder made the film with a clear colour palette in mind and here we get to see it in action, the weird colouring of suits and background are gone and while it may not be a colouring expected for a JL movie it’s a colouring that matches the Snyder tone and the film he’s made.

3. The shots. The cinematography in this film at times is beautiful, the WW shot throwing the briefcase, the Superman shot above the Earth, The Flash running through the speed force, it was unexpected how good parts of the film looked. The visuals for the most part were good too, some clunky CGI in places but for the most part it was great. And, I’ve been critical of Snyder’s directing of action before but I loved it in this film, it was kinetic and smooth and the action was smart which is better than most of the DCEU films.

Some of the shots however ran far too long. This film didn’t have to be as long as it did, there’s some fat that could’ve been trimmed. The runtime is egregious, some shots linger on people or events that add nothing to the larger story.  I think the film would still have been long even if some of it was cut but it could’ve been a bit cleaner. In saying this I don’t think Snyder would’ve been able to tell the story he did without the runtime, it wouldn’t have met the two hour limit set by WB, so for that I’m glad we got this weird mini-series/film to flesh out the characters.

Along with the insane runtime there was a lot of clunky, awkward dialogue, at some points it was noticeably bad but at others it was normal superhero cheesy lines at some points that were bad but not jarring. Some story beats as well weren’t incredible, while I enjoyed the added (and necessary) background to Steppenwolf I still think he’s a basic villain. Although I did enjoy the Darkseid aspects of the film.

While the nerd in me loved the weird nightmare epilogue, it was completely unnecessary and didn’t add to the film in any way. However, and this surprises me, I would love to see that film if it gets made. I actually was interested in Jared Leto’s joker?!? For that Snyder... congratulations.

Overall I think it was great to see a director’s vision play out and surprisingly enjoyed this take. Was it a brilliant film? No, there’s a plethora of things that are wrong with but there’s some great (earned) superhero moments, some good character beats, great action and a clear through line. It’s incredible this is as good as it is. It’s a love letter from Snyder to a world and characters he loves and a film his daughter would be proud of

Name: Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse
Review:
“Anyone can wear the mask”

So this is part of my course films and I’m glad that this masterpiece is being studied by people as it should

Name: La La Land
Review: This is my happy place. I never payed much attention to the lighting the first few times I watched this (I have no idea how) but the lighting in this film is stunning every scene is lit to perfection. The final 10 minutes might be my favourite part of a movie ever

Name: Blade Runner
Review: Conceptually and thematically interesting but I think the story and plot they chose for this film are weak, if there at all. While the world building and the style are amazing, the set design is probably the best part of this film, unfortunately it’s not supported by the strongest story. There are some parts which are interesting but others that become basic and bland story beats or completely non-sensical, for example the rape of a replicant turning into a love story. The dialogue in places is very jarring too, I watched the theatrical cut this time and Harrison Ford’s narration is awful was much better without it

Name: Blade Runner 2049
Review:
So... this is probably an unpopular opinion but I think this is far and away a better film than the original. The strongest elements of the original are the world building and the themes it tries to tackle, both of which are exponentially utilised more in this film. This world is fascinating, from all the technological developments to the scale and scope of the different places, I was captivated at every scene. The philosophy of this film and the thematic elements are equally as strong from trying to question what is love and if the love K feels is real to the larger questions of humanity and the consciousness of technology. I think most interestingly this film places us in a comfortable position for most of the film, we follow someone who he and the audience believe to be “the one”, as common in a lot of Hollywood films. Yet this pivot to someone who isn’t special, who is there to facilitate another purpose is interesting and to see what that character does with this information is unique and exciting to watch.

This might be one of the prettiest films ever, the cinematography, set design and colouring are all perfect and help build out this fascinating neo-noir future. The characters are also far more compelling in this film and there’s a plot which makes use of this incredible world that’s been built. I wasn’t in love with the army plot point as it seemed out of place and more like a dystopian cheesy film but that is a minor gripe. This film is stunning and I think people should be looking at the jackets that have built in masks, could be handy right now. Also I’m glad we got a great version of Jared Leto’s Joker, sad it wasn’t in a DCEU film

Name: Love, Simon
Review:
Didn’t expect to enjoy this as much as I did. This film is so heartwarming and sweet, it has this nice aura to it that I can’t really explain. Yes some parts are cheesy and a little cliche but in the world that the film builds it’s not distracting and is kinda endearing. Greg Berlanti has always championed diverse casts and characters in the shows he’s produced so it’s nice to see him be handed a mainstream platform to herald those voices who have often been squashed in history. 

The film successfully placed us in Simon’s shoes with all the anxiety and pressure he feels and while it is a run of the mill story it’s fun to see it framed from a perspective that’s not been done before, this in itself adds depth and dimension to the film. Nick Robinson’s performance is beautiful and his character is handled with such care. 
The film didn’t have to be as funny as it is, Tony Hale is effortlessly hilarious as usual and Natasha Rothwell cracks me up every time, she also gets a badass moment standing up for Simon that was sweet. Jennifer Garner though... man her speech got me, it easily elevates this film for me, it’s a short loving speech from a mother who truly cares for her son, and it’s just perfect. 

There are some character beats I don’t love, specifically the complete abandoning of Simon by his friends after he’s outed and some scenes that are a little cringy like the crowd around the Ferris wheel but it’s sweet and cute so who cares. Also fuck Martin, what a completely unlikeable character.

While it may not be as artistically nuanced as CMBYN, this film has its own place as a really funny, heartfelt coming of age comedy that just happens to centre around someone who’s gay

Name: Inglourious Basterds
Review: Tarantino is just a master of directing, not saying anything new there, but his film effortlessly manages to tell a compelling and captivating story and every aspect of it has been polished and thought through. Christoph Waltz is delightful as the campy “Jew Hunter” and any time he was on screen it was brilliant to watch. Mélanie Laurent was the highlight of the film, her storyline was compelling, heartfelt and badass and British 1940s Michael Fassbender is something I didn’t know I needed. The issues with the film surround the titular characters, “The Bastards” aren’t given enough development or characterisation despite the lengthy runtime which left me invested in every other character’s story more than I was there’s. Yes Brad Pitt was great but it would’ve been nice to know anything more about him other than the fact he’s American and a Jew. The ending tho was großartig

Name: Birdman or (The Unexpected Virtue of Ignorance)
Review: This was insane. The camera movement, shots and cinematography, with the bare, stripped back, jazz score, creates an endless barrage of anxiety and stress that places us directly in Keaton’s world. Every single performance in this is Acting with a capital A. I was engulfed in the performances and the film’s beauty throughout and the message it has to say on the value of art and what audiences, all audiences no matter the class or brow, crave is purely just spectacle and larger than life outcomes was wildly fascinating. Emma stone also always amazing

Name: Snowpiercer
Review:
Bong Joon-Ho is unreal. After loving Parasite I was excited to dive deeper into his films and this one didn’t disappoint. He has cornered these niche films that toe the lie between comedy and ultra-violence and seamlessly blends these two opposing genres together. The film has so much heart and Joon-Ho manages to make us care for every single character, which makes it all the more heartbreaking when a character passes. He also continues his themes from Parasite of class and social structure and the comradery that is forged through hardship, which are so relevant to our society right now. While also places these themes in a world that has been destroyed through climate change.

The cast are all sublime, Tilda Swindon is fantastic as always and Chris Evans gives a sweet, heartbreaking, tortured and badass performance that elevated the film. The concept was masterfully executed and was thrilling to watch from tail to head.

Name: Kajillionaire
Review:
My whole life has been dictated by someone else. Someone who’s been saying, “You will.” And now, now I feel like I’ve discovered my own voice. And it says, “I may.”
The words of Delores ring true for Evan Rachel Wood’s Old Dolie, a woman who was starved of compassion and human contact so much that she became a host to facilitate her twisted parent’s motives. This quirky, unique film takes a methodical and steadily paced plot and uses all its characters to great effect allowing the film to be foaming from the roof with heart, charm and sincerity. Evan Rachel Wood delivers a standout performance in this, given sparse dialogue she still manages to give an emotional core to the film, every time we see her crave human contact it broke my heart. Her parents are so slimy and cold to her it was hard to watch at some points, you just want to give Old Dolie a hug. Not to mention the creepiest scene where the parents approach Melanie in the hot tub, they’re characterised perfectly to make us hate them. Gina Rodriguez is fantastic and plays such a great counterpoint to Wood’s character, with the bubbly, inviting personality and being an incredibly compassionate person, I was screaming at the TV for her to grab Old Dolie’s hand.  July directs this film effortlessly and there’s some nice visuals throughout. The score however elevated the film to new heights, the ethereal and mellow beats blend a romance film with the heist elements perfectly and add to the sweetness of the film. The film’s power to show intimacy on screen was beautiful, from the smallest touch you could feel the emotional impact each thing had on Old Dolie. The parental role and the role of intimacy and love in our lives was masterfully conveyed, and while these themes are incredibly apparent in Old Dolie’s story, I think the film is trying to show, through the family but also through Melanie, how we often substitute these emotions for things and material goods, how we take for granted the intimacy and love people try to give to us and I found that beautiful.

Name: The Lego Movie 2: The Second Part
Review: Better than it has any right being but feels less special than the first, thematically the same yet it’s handled sloppily and leads to less of an emotional impact. The constant cutting between the “real” world and the Lego story I think was unnecessary. However there’s still heaps of creative storytelling and animation in this film that is makes for a fun watch

Name: Whiplash
Review: Master Tenzin went off with his Avatar training in this one

Name: (500) Days of Summer
Review: Watched for research, still a practically perfect look at postmodern love

Name: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
Review: This is a fun, quirky and unconventional story that pulls off the romantic and depressed tone perfectly through Kaufman’s masterful writing. This is only enhanced by the film’s visuals, the use of lighting and set design while we travel through the mind was great and the transitions between scenes were so smartly done. There’s some story beats that aren’t fully expanded on which was a little annoying but the incredible performances elevate the story from being a cliche romance to something deeper and more interesting.

Name: Annie Hall
Review: Mansplain: The Movie This felt so pretentious and self-indulgent it became painful at some points to get through. It feels like a movie purely just for Woody Allen to process his feelings on love, which could’ve been fascinating but it’s clear Allen thinks he’s a genius and the best and funniest writer to exist but the film just lacks heart and an entertaining story. This film bet Star Wars for best picture and that makes me mad. Diane Keaton for what she was given was fantastic to watch but this film is truly mental masterbation

Name: The Graduate
Review: So like 10 year old Tom in (500) Days of Summer watched this, no wonder he’s got a fucked up idea of love

Name: Chicago
Review: Fun take on the stage show with some amazing performances and some smart transitions and imagery. However the direction and editing of the film feels a little sloppy in places, the big musical numbers are oversaturated with cuts that it takes away from the spectacle that is happening. The film I also feel doesn’t use the cinematic medium to its fullest, setting the musical numbers mainly on stages, while it works for the most part, could’ve been so much more dynamic and interesting. The Cell Black Tango was great tho

Name: Palm Springs
Review: Better than Groundhog Day

Name: High School Musical 2
Review:
It’s taken me a month to work up the will to watch the sequel to the disaster that was the first but I found this movie way more enjoyable, here’s some of my thoughts: The good: - Sharpay having the self awareness to make fun of Gabriella cause of Vanessa Hudgens acting - Sharpay in general - Kelsi (my god her fashion), Ryan and Chad are still great - The themes of class and being a poor student - Most of the songs in this movie are proper bangers - My theory about Chad continues in this film:
- He doesn’t want to dance until Troy asks him to - His face lit up when Troy asked him to perform in the show- He’s angry at Sharpay not cause she’s taking him away from Gabriella but him- Gabriella and Chad both believe Troy can’t be taken by Sharpay cause they’re too in love with him - Chad doesn’t want to do the show without Troy - The queer readings of “I don’t Dance” and the fact him and Ryan definitely fucked since they changed clothes, Chad doing it cause he’s annoyed at Troy - When Troy apologised to Chad he lit up
The bad: - Troy gives Gabriella a necklace of the letter T like she’s his property and the group think it’s the most romantic thing ever - Vanessa Hudgens acting - The fashion...it’s real bad - The dialogue between Troy and Gabriella, people don’t talk like that - Zac Efron’s dancing, it’s awful - The reflection of Troy in the water, might some of the worst cgi ever The wtf: - The cult like chanting of Summer at the start - Why are all the drama kids treated like celebrities in the school - Why is the whole school hired at the club, cause Troy asked?? - Chad’s weird slogan T-shirts - The obvious stunt double for Gabriella literally just diving into a pool - Troy - “My singing career ended in the last movie”   Last 30 minutes of the film: Troy singing constantly - Troy complains that he’s poor but look at his house, how is he poor?!? - Troy’s tantrum singing on the golf course (song slaps tho) - Miley Cyrus?!?

Name: High School Musical 3: Senior Year
Review: So rounding out the trilogy that’s up there with the Synder trilogy, here’s my thoughts on the 3rd movie: The Good: - The opening was kinda cool - Feels like there was a bare minimum thought out into cinematography which was surprising - Lighting and editing were competent?!? - “I Want it All” is the best song in all 3 movies - Sharpay and Ryan still great - “I think he stopped breathing” “Well then give him the heimlich” great line - Conceptually having the music from throughout the film as the musical was very smart - Kelsi still the sweetest person in this - My Chad theory continues: - He immediately says he wants to play basketball to look cool in front of Troy but denies that he likes musicals- His initial proposal to Taylor is so small cause he doesn’t want to then he gets so nervous asking in front of the school but does it to keep his appearance of not loving Troy - The entire “Boys are Back” song - Taylor doesn’t value Gabriella’s relationship because she’s not satisfied with Chad - Chad picked out Troy’s tux - Chad says that they leave the girls behind in high school, isn’t upset about having to leave Taylor and says it’s going to be a different “ball” game with him and Troy in college - He gets upset that Troy won’t be going to the same college as him but then is happy knowing they’ll still see each other when competing against each other The Bad: - Gabriella screaming Troy after he falls, I was howling - Kelsi didn’t learn any fashion sense :( - Worst way of asking someone to prom - “In every language, oui, si, ya, yes” AWFUL dialogue - Too many slow boring generic songs - The cgi basketballs were awful - “I love you Wildcat”, “You’re crazy Wildcat” who wrote these lines - How did no one notice Chad wasn’t there until they called his name? - Why is Sharpay redeemed at the end of all the movies? The WTF: - Still why tf does the Dad have his office in the locker room - Juilliard and Troy?!? Yeah so believable with Zac Efron’s acting and singing - Troy and Chad are literal bullies in this - Ryan’s watch, it’s HUGE - Ryan and Kelsi hahahahhahahhahahahhahahaha ok sureeeee - WHY ARE THEY ALL DRINKING PINTS OF MILK AT DINNER?!? - What boring ass person’s favourite pizza is margarita... oh Gabriella ofc - The whole ending with the Juilliard people getting up and singing yeah that’s not believable - The British girl singing in British

Name: Promising Young Woman
Review:
Ok so first off CAREY MULLIGAN, yes. She gives an incredible performance, every scene she’s in I was completely captivated and engaged, she just eats up the screen. Bo Burnam is effortlessly funny as usual and a delight to watch despite his “twist” being a touch obvious. The film as a whole visually is a delight to look at, the framing of shots, the incredibly smart use of colours especially blue and pink and the set design.

The film falters in my opinion in a few ways however. It takes about 30 minutes to get into the plot and while it’s an enjoyable 30 minutes it feels a little disjointed and not cohesive enough to the film as a whole. I think there were some odd music choices too, despite the use of toxic played on a violin being PERFECT, a few felt on the nose or out of place.

The ending too which I found really enjoyable, the fact that the patriarchy allows men to get away with these things and face no consequences, for example when the detective goes to Ryan to speak to him it’s all about what the Dad had said to him and not the concerned mother. He believes the girl is crazy and gets Ryan to confirm this to dismiss his case which was great because it highlights the sexism in our society. But I felt like this was undercut slightly with the last sequence when the police arrive and we are to be happy as Cassandra got her revenge but it’s putting trust in other people, the police, to carry out justice for her and we’ve seen that they haven’t previously. So I think the ending was a little counterintuitive yet still overall a needed and important watch

Name: The Big Lebowski
Review: Effortless acting, effortless writing, effortless dialogue, effortless direction and a weird plot

Name: The Incredibles
Review: Film is front to back flawless, thematically the film shines  balancing the superhero aspects perfectly with a family drama. This is a testament to the incredible voice acting and realistic dialogue alongside the beautiful animation and clever scenarios the characters are thrust into. Edna Mode is a delight and the score is so well utilised blending the 60s spy feel with superhero action, 100 mile dash is a perfect piece of film music.

Name: Incredibles 2
Review: Watching the original and then the sequel one after another really shows the insane improvements Pixar has made in animation. This film is BEAUTIFUL, which is saying something as the orginal didn't look bad but here, the shading, the textures, the backgrounds are all incredible. Jack-Jack and Edna Mode need a spin-off

Name: One Night in Miami...
Review: Regina King’s directional debut is filled to the brim with extraordinary performances and some great visuals however lacks a soul and a purpose. The film was engaging enough and the dialogue strong throughout but it falls flat. The movie fails to amount to anything, it’s simply conversations with 4 powerful people which showcased the incredible acting of all the main characters. Props to Regina King though on making what’s essentially 4 people talking interesting and engaging but this really feels like a play on screen

Name: The Falcon and the Winter Soldier
Review: The Falcon and the Winter Soldier makes for one of marvel’s most impactful stories yet, the rise of Sam Wilson as Captain America. The beautiful and poignant way they handled racism in America and the story of Isaiah Bradley was not expected but incredibly appreciated. They handled race so delicately and with such care in the show it was great to watch and so relevant with what’s going on in the world. Full thoughts on a video essay I did on here They also handled Bucky’s story of coming to grips with the mantle of the Winter Soldier well and had some nice emotional beats with him. The real strength in this show lies in its two leads, Mackie and Stan make for a perfect duo and there chemistry is electric. Often the best parts of this show are moments you can tell they were improvising. They have such a fun playful banter between them that’s always engaging to watch and towards the later half of the series are given some emotional moments that help cement them as one of the MCU’s best duos. The inclusion of John Walker was brilliant as well, Russell plays this anti-hero/villain with the perfect amount of charm and desire to be a hero that makes for a fun watch. The other characters were fun inclusions too, namely Zemo, who’s grander role into the plot seems pointless and unnecessary but was a brilliant addition to the duo when he was involved. Sharon Carter manages to get an ounce of character development too which is more than can be said for her in the films. While her “twist” was overly obvious and doesn’t add a lot to the show it gives Marvel a brilliant starting point for what could be one of the better villains in the MCU. Speaking of villains, this is where the show falters for me, the “Flag Smashers”. Carli is an interesting character throughout the show yet isn’t given a lot of character development to justify her motives, which in the show are incredibly unclear for a lot of it. The people she surrounds herself with are completely superfluous, with every one of them either given a few lines of are there to die. This storyline felt extremely tagged on and was an after thought until the finale. This was obviously impacted on by Covid as they had to change the plot but still it felt out of place. The general writing of the show is also very hit or miss. There are some extremely well written scenes, for example the scenes with Isaiah or anything with Sam and Bucky talking but there’s some really bad cheesy writing at some points. Quips made by some characters that were atrocious or some of the most obvious and unnecessary dialogue I’ve seen in a show, that felt like a CW show. Granted this is few and far between but still not what I expected from marvel. Luckily though there is the incredible action in the show to make up for this. Like DAMN. This is some of the best action the MCU has produced. From the flying scene that kicks off the show to the badass action scene Sharon Carter gets to the beautiful fight scene in the last episode, it was masterfully done in this show. The way they utilise Sam in the final episode combining the shield and the wings for some amazing choreography has me excited for what they will do with him next. The show as a whole was shot beautifully too with some stunning cinematography in the fight scenes and especially the last shot of episode 4.  The Disney + shows have allowed us to explore characters on a deeper level and get really interesting looks into who these people are, especially focusing on characters who’ve not had a lot of development in the MCU. If the shows continue to head in this direction then I’m excited Also Julia Louis Dreyfus playing a Marvel villian. FUCK YEAH. MCU IN REVIEW

Name: Sound of Metal
Review:
Incredible. Riz Ahmed delivers one of the best performances I’ve seen in a long time, he perfectly channels the innate desire for things to stay normal. The range of emotions on display is something actors should be in envy of, every moment is so captivating due to his performance. The film is simultaneously sweet and heartwarming but also so melancholic and heartbreaking and it displays its themes masterfully. The scene with Ahmed and the kid on the slide, cry


AND the sound design. LIKE FUCK. The sound in this film is a masterpiece

Name: Miss Americana
Review:
So. This isn’t something I would normally watch but it was on the tv and I watched it with the family. I was convinced by the sundance Laurel on the picture :/ It was surprisingly better than I expected, not knowing much about Taylor Swift going into this was interesting as she’s not who I thought she was. The heart and humour she has is in abundance and the amount of abuse she’s been given by the media is outrageous and sickening. It was nice to see the natural evolution of someone who’s become strong and more mature in speaking her mind while still keeping her moral values.

There’s some weird editing though that I didn’t love and the pacing and flow of the documentary was clunky and jarring. The overall message of the documentary, of becoming secure in your beliefs and standing up for what’s right is somewhat undercut by the backdrop of fame and some things Taylor says. But it was an enjoyable watch.


Publicist: “The President could come after you”
Taylor: “Fuck that, I don’t care”
This was perfect comedic timing

Name: Ma Rainey's Black Bottom
Review:
Ok so I’ve decided I’m not a fan of the play to film genre. Between Ma Rainey’s and One Night in Miami I find myself annoyed at the waste of potential in adapting plays to a cinematic form, both are prime examples of a “play on a screen”. The staging and blocking feels like a play, the set design is bland and there’s a lack of creative visuals. However much like with One Night in Miami, this film shines in the acting and performances that are displayed, which is the prime benefit of these sorts of films.

This film doesn’t disappoint and gives two incredible lead performances by Viola Davis and, in his last film, Chadwick Boseman. Davis is able to channel the bitter, vile role of Ma Rainey perfectly while still maintaining the reasons behind her aggression and resentment of the world she’s in. In her moments of quiet is where her performance truly shines, the complexities of the emotions she feels are written on her face. Boseman delivers a heart wrenching and emotionally charged performance that beautifully showcases his acting range.

However while the performances by the leads and the supporting characters are incredible it doesn’t help the complete mess of a story that they are acting in. There’s plot points that don’t add anything to the film, like the door and what’s more is the character arc of Levee is nonsensical and comes out of nowhere. There’s aspects that make sense and a clear rise in tension but it’s resolution seems so weird and that’s due to the writing not being able to build up to this climax.

This film seems to be sweeping up the makeup and costume awards which it deserves as that’s another place the movie is strong too but there’s a lot of minor things that don’t work well with the film too, some weird editing choices and some bad cgi. But for what this film is there to showcase, which is the acting, it is a perfect example of the two leads with an beautiful final performance from Boseman

Name: The Mitchells vs. the Machines
Review:
First movie in a while, needed a break from studying for exams and this was the perfect film for it. As a unabashed lover of animation, there is so much in this film to gush over. Sony animation studios really has one of the best redemption stories, with this and Spiderverse under their belts. The stylisation of the film is delightful, the use of 2D animation to emote Katie’s feelings, the beautiful colouring of the film, and the absolutely stunning cinematography at points (the cover page on Letterboxd) made it so easy to look at. Specifically the set design was insane, every set piece was fully detailed and so precise, the world felt lived in, for example even their family home felt like they had been there for years and had truly been using their surroundings instead of being passive in the environment. 

The animation of the characters as well was so natural and realistic, from the little details like chewing on hoodie strings to the characters motions when getting emotional, like rubbing their arms, you could clearly see the emotions being conveyed through body language which is a feat in animation. Then going to the hyper scenes the characters animate and move is unique and fun ways.

The characterisation itself in the film was beautiful, every character in the family was given a chance to shine and given strong emotional and comedic moments. Speaking of the comedy, the film hit my humour so well, it’s the most I’ve laughed in a film in a while. Basing the humour in emotion and distinct characteristic traits was a smart move, each time there was a call back it added upon the humour. Both the mum and the brother had incredibly funny moments, when the mum gets mad and beats up the robots going full Mad Max, fantastic. Also the emotional beats really hit hard, it’s such a beautiful representation of a normal dysfunctional family.

The themes surrounding technology and it’s impact on our lives were interesting, all be it heavy handed, but I enjoyed the issues it decided to tackle and the way in which they told it. There was some small minor gripes I had with the film, a few jokes that didn’t land and an awful music choice at the end but the work and love that was put into animating this film is a feat itself.

Lord and Miller produce incredible animation, with this, Spiderverse, Cloudy with a Chance of Meatballs and The Lego Movie I trust them to produce great animation. Also directed by the writer of Gravity Falls, this film is full of great creators in animation and it shows

Review:
“I love lying”
“Ugh. As if earth needs another politician.”


Richard Ayeode calling politicians liars, chefs kiss

Name: Hercules
Review: The Muses and Megara are still some of Disney’s best characters, and hands down “I won’t say I’m in love” is one of the best Disney songs

Name: Amélie
Review: If I was to sum up this film in a phase it would be inquisitive intimacy. Amelie manages to use its fast pace and specific descriptions to create a sense of intimacy that other films strive to achieve. The abundance of close up shots and warm colours adds to this conforming atmosphere, along with the beautiful score that oozes “Frenchness”. The film manages to toe the lie between wholesomeness and melancholia perfectly through Amelie herself, the idea that her kindness and love, while true, comes from a place of fear of intimacy and isolation was a delight to watch. Some editing choices I didn’t love but overall this was a pure pleasure to watch

Name: Frances Ha
Review:
This was a delight to watch. The film perfectly captures the struggles of wealth that happens as you meet new people who have came from privileged backgrounds. The ease of how money is dismissed in conversation and how disingenuous people with money are to those who are struggling was masterfully crafted. The scene where Frances is having a conversation with “friends” at a meal who keep pressuring her into conversation was handled perfectly, the idea that Frances slowly becomes defensive and then says stuff that she doesn’t mean was such a nicely captured piece of the real world. 

Not to mention this film is doused in humour, specifically humour that I like, the funny mannerisms of a person amazingly performed by Greta Gurwig. The dialogue at some points felt contrived and stilted but overall this was a film based in realism and a lovely story of a kind hearted person who was living their life

Name: Nomadland
Review:
Beautiful. This film is summed up in that one word, it’s absolutely stunning visually and the score by Einaudi is just magnificent. This film truly shines in its themes and specifically its themes can be encapsulated in one word: people. The communities built between people and how cherished such bonds are, the isolation people face alone, both in loneliness and the choice to be alone and the compassion of people, out of kindness and out of pity. This film is multilayered in all its pockets of conversations and is eye opening to a community of people that aren’t represented in media that much.

The Nomads give insight into this community while also each being so interpersonal, managing to tell heartbreaking and tear inducing stories in minutes. It helps that this film is masterfully directed by Chloe Zhao, so excited for Eternals now, who creates these intimate moments so easily. She also has Frances McDormand to thank for the effortless realism and emotionally charged conversations.

McDormand delivers an incredible performance throughout, specifically how she manages to convey this theme of age and ageing out of society, it’s something I’ve never seen in a movie before and it was so incredibly done and handled with such care, it was a stunning look on how society treats the elderly and how money and capitalism affects people who are elderly

Name: Bo Burnham: Inside
Review: This is incredible. Bo’s work while classified as comedy (and is great comedy at that) always has had an element of critic and observations on the world. This special evokes these same elements while also managing to expertly toe the lie between comedy and sadness with it being an insight to the deteriorating nature of some people’s mental health in the last year. The special is a must watch for anyone that has had a hard time the last year as Bo masterfully creates a fun and poignant special that tackles with the feelings many have felt in lockdown. Bo is multitalented and this special proves the extent of his talents, the fact he did this practically on his own is unreal. Also it doesn’t hurt that there are some incredible songs in it, White Woman’s Instagram is one of my favourites

Name: Little Miss Sunshine
Review: This is a gem of a film. It’s endearing and weird with some brilliant twists that play off perfectly both comedically and dramatically. The performances are fantastic, especially Abigail Breslin, how the hell was she that amazing at that age. Also I adored the message throughout the film, it’s such a warm and sweet one that was pulled off perfectly in a movie that manages to also be depressing, just an all round sweet, unique indie movie

Name: Scott Pilgrim vs. the World
Review: 3rd time watching it this year and I just love this movie, the stylisation, the editing, the writing, bless Mr Wright. It’s also weird watching this after Succession with Kieran Culkin. Great movie to watch on my bday tho since the 20 year old is called young

Name: Us Again
Review: This goes to show the power Damien Shazelle has had on society

Name: Raya and the Last Dragon
Review:
Ok so the film itself isn’t incredible, there’s some weird editing choices, a beyond predictable plot and while there’s some nice situational humour, there’s also a abundance of sloppy childish humour… cough cough all the fart stuff. Which if Disney had steered away from that humour, the film would’ve had more stakes while still not losing any real value in humour.

But the film is also a powerhouse in a few aspects, the world building, while yes very Avatar-esque, was done very well and the representation of Asian culture in the world building was a great refreshing take. I particularly loved the use of holes in the film as in China the architecture has holes in it sometimes to let dragons fly through.
