Set against the backdrop of the countryside of America, Guadagnino interjects a tendness and beauty to these long winding roads and large sprawling areas, doing for America what Call Me by Your Name did for the Italian countryside. The characters feel more isolated in these spaces, when no one is around acceptance isn’t found in others but only yourself. Having these spaces often shared between the two lovers really adds this intimacy to their relationship, as they’ve found the one other person who understands them and willing to accept them flaws and all.

The film seems to prioritise the blossoming love between Maren and Lee as its main driving force, not the violent acts they commit. Now while that could’ve created this tonal dissonance that would have left the film leaving disjointed, it’s precisely this exchanging of tones throughout the film that makes it feel fresh and adds to the tragedy of their love. There are some ingenious edits interspersed in the film that directly juxtaposes these two tones that heightens the horror elememts as you become aware how gory and excessive, they are when it’s contrasted with a shot of the beauty of America. The score also perfectly captures this unique blending of tones, it evokes a lot of the same feelings as The Last of Us score, using a more stripped back guitar underscore that at times is loving but hides something darker underneath it that crops up in these more graphic scenes.

Chalamet as expected delivers another fantastic performance, playing his character the entire time with a hidden hurt that only becomes exposed once finding a connection in Maren. Rylance is also magnetic is this devilishly creepy role but far and and away Taylor Russell is the standout of the film. The film centres around her so she has a lot to play with, the palpable confliction you’re able to feel emenating of her as she stays on the run, both looking for acceptance but also running from her own self-acceptance.

The film’s culmination is a tragic deconstruction of this theme of acceptance, and a visceral and tense conclusion. The way that the scene plays out tragically explores what happens when one tries to force themselves into a mould instead of accepting who they are. And yes, I’m aware that this is in regards to cannibals, but I ultimately believe that the film is trying to use this as a vessel to explore self-acceptance. The final shot too, just tragically beautiful

Name: Athena
Review:
Venice Film Festival #9 – PalaBiennale

The opening scene to this film is one of the most insane experiences I’ve had in a cinema. Long one-shot, or false one-shot, take scenes are all the rage now, but I’ve yet to see one as extravagant and bursting with energy like this one. The sheer coordination and choreography needed to pull of a scene of this scale is simply a feat in directing. After one of his brother’s is killed due to supposed police brutality, Karim stages an attack on the police that starts with the throw of a Molotov cocktail to the podium where a speech is taking place, despite that speech being given by his brother. What ensures is a cacophony of violence and chaos, that at once feels precisely coordinated but simultaneously spontaneous and natural. The sheer brutality and masculinity on display as the scene culminates in a floury of violence and fireworks is pure euphoria on screen.

This employment of long one-takes is used throughout the entire film and it adds this sense of tangibility to the events that are playing out, it’s shot in a way that you become a part of the scene allowing you to be a part of the violence and bloodshed. The brilliance of the directing keeps each scene filled with so much raw energy that forms this tense thriller. Reminded me a lot of Lady Ly’s Les Misérables yet on steroids, a pure extravagant display of brutality that results in a stunning spectacle.

The film does feel a little muddled in its social commentary however, and I’m unsure what to take away from it. It feels so strongly heading in one direction but abandons this idea and leaves you confused as to what the film is trying to say. The screenplay sadly doesn’t match the masterful technical components on offer, which is disappointing as it had such an interesting premise, but it gets lost in the chaos and ends with a fizzle not a bang. However, as a cinematic experience, this is a suffocating display of raw masculinity that is an achievement in filmmaking

Name: All the Beauty and the Bloodshed
Review:
Venice Film Festival #10 – Sala Grande

Had absolutely no knowledge on what this would be about going into it, but to my surprise I was moved to tears. Chronicling the story of Nan Goldin’s attempt to take down the Sackler family and her battle against Big Pharma, while interspersed with excerpts documenting her life, this powerful documentary is an empowering display of the value of art and one’s drive to enact change in the world based on their own personal experiences.

The film really showcases its emotional power in the slices on Nan Goldin’s life. They play out by and large in the same format, a collection of photos taken by Goldin, and others, while Goldin voiceovers said images with stories of her past and the events that led her to where she is. These include the tragic death of her sister, issues with sexuality, the AIDS crisis, her relationship to drugs and the importance of photography to tell the truth. The depth to which these sections explore is astonishing, it fully paints a picture of who Goldin is and is a wonderful way to document a person’s life.

In the present, Nan’s battle against Big Pharma is just as fascinating, but given the context of who she is and how big corporations and drugs have had a significant impact on her life only enhances this section further as you understand where the fight is coming from and how important it is to her. Seeing someone try so hard to make the smallest difference against these, often impenetrable, figures is inspiring, let alone it being told in such a beautiful and cinematic way. There’s a scene where people are allowed to tell the Sackler family the affect the drugs they distribute have had on them that is so emotionally charged, it was heart-breaking to watch.

There’s something so powerful about someone telling stories unfiltered over images of how they viewed their life, it’s raw and a deeply unique experience. The number of topics spoken to with such depth and compassion was a profound thing to watch. Nan Goldin received a 5-minute standing ovation in the screening and there’s no doubt she deserved it

Name: Argentina 1985
Review:
Venice Film Festival #11 – PalaBiennale

Despite its heavy topic, this ended up being a total crowd pleaser. Strange to have such comedy interjected into this deep subject matter but it blew over the crowd, with it getting more laughs that the other more comedic ventures here at the festival like White Noise and Bardo. Yet, even tough what’s presented is an extremely palatable presentation of the story, it feels as if it’s lacking the nuance that should have accompanied such an important topic.

The film decides to opt for a simplicity in most of its scenes, the court case isn’t really explored in detail, it’s more the people behind it. While that certainly works to get you invested in these people and the fight they’re struggling against, it ends up making the situation feel a little empty. This is best exemplified in the ending where, like a lot of based on a true story films, it ends with title cards explaining what happened. The way this film ends makes it feel more like a sitcom where it’s showing the characters after they’ve finished the court case. It just feels tonally strange and was lighter than the event deserved. The speech that’s delivered near the end got a huge reaction, but it would’ve been so much more powerful had the film been more dramatic up until that point.

It does what it sets out to do well though, it’s an easy-to-follow recount of this historical event in Argentina is a pleasant watch. In its simplicity though it becomes very repetitive once you understand what’s going and since there’s no attempt at depth it becomes redundant after a while. The light-hearted tone which makes it so strong is also the thing that stops it from being amazing as it lacks the weight this topic should have

Name: Master Gardener
Review:
Venice Film Festival #12 – PalaBiennale

This film hinges on one of my biggest gripes in cinema, having a relationship at its core that has virtually no development. The film asks you to believe in this odd relationship that’s built, but it doesn’t seem interested in showing why it exists more just informing you that it does. This is a problem as it makes no sense why this relationship occurs, as it’s between a former reformed Nazi and a young woman. The film screams that it was written by a man since all of a sudden the woman doesn’t matter who he was and just wants to have sex with him. It was so jarring I ended up laughing in the cinema. Having this relationship be the basis of the film just makes it collapse in on itself.

The writing as a whole is not only disappointing but genuinely atrocious at some points. The dialogue between the two seems forced and contrived, and when it comes to the woman she’s written with virtually no autonomy or agency, and not in an interesting storytelling way where the film explains why she is like this, this is in a way that uses woman as plot devices. The film is about a man’s redemption arc and finding happiness despite his dark past, but for that to come at the expense of giving the woman any semblance of decision making is laughable.

The film is missing a driving force too, why does this man suddenly fall in love with this woman? What motivated him to reforming? The film opts to avoid these questions in the hopes that you won’t notice. Yet when the core of the story is so false and unbelievable and the film doesn’t want to explore any of these other narrative threads, it creates an air of indifference to the film.

What’s trying to come across on screen is this symbiotic relationship between the beauty of nature and violence but sadly this idea isn’t as striking as the film wants it to be. For being a film centred around gardening there’s never a real sense of how beautiful the main character finds the art. In contrast the violence just feels completely out of place and as the film does a bad job setting up its characters, it doesn’t feel as impactful as the film doesn’t convey how much of a change this is for the reformed man.

Some good performances though, despite some atrocious dialogue, and a mesmerising score. But a lacklustre screenplay and flat and lifeless technical elements leave this feeling like a garden that hasn’t been tended to with love and more like a few flowers that Schrader says is one

Name: Monica
Review:
Venice Film Festival #13 – PalaBiennale

A weird aspect ratio, excruciating long shots and an emotional story. You guessed it, it’s an arthouse film. The film takes a while to set up what its story is, and the plot covered prior, well there isn’t any. They’re nice setting scenes to get an idea of who Monica is, but other than that it feels very superfluous once the film actually gets into the main plot. This happens throughout, as this film is more of a character study than anything else, but sometimes these scenes added virtually nothing to our understanding of her.

The film shines when it focuses on her relationship to her mother and her dealing with the situation she’s been placed in. Any scene between Trace Lysette and Patricia Clarkson is emotionally charged and gripping. However, the film just feels a bit hollow, it’s not until deep into the film that the plot becomes more focused and it becomes more emotional. As a character study though it isn’t as compelling as it wants to be, partly due to the amount of time spent in these scenes that don’t have a lot of substance to them.

Performances all around were great, Trace Lysette is insane and despite their being no reason for it being shot the way it is, it’s intense and divine. However, the drawn out first half, that is meditative to its detriment, spoils a meaningful second half. Only though reading about the film did I know it was attempting to highlight trans issues which didn’t come through in the film at all. Sadly, in its subtlety it loses any impact, which is a disappointing as there is some strong parts hidden in the film

Name: L'immensità
Review:
Venice Film Festival #14 – PalaBiennale

L’immensita is a movie that’s trying to juggle to two plots simultaneously and while the connection between the two is clear is doesn’t make for the most satisfying story. A mother dealing with an abusive partner and escaping her relationship by entering the world of a child and her son who is accepting his trans identity despite the criticisms of his family. What really shines is the unbreakable bond between the two and how they find solace each other, how even though they have their concerns and aren’t entirely supportive of each other’s situation, they will always have a love for each other.

For the most part this is a standard family drama, mixed with a coming-of-age story but the film sometimes breaks the confines of reality and bursts into these more fantastical sequences, even at some points having these energic scenes seeping into reality. Everyone of these is really fun and gives the film a different flavour, stopping it from being another undistinguishable entry into the genre.

It's a crime that I haven’t seen Penelope Cruz in much, except for her pivotal role in Pirates of the Caribbean: On Stranger Tides, as she’s magnanimous in this film. As a performer she’s able to completely capture your attention and embodies this complex woman struggling to stay strong while under abuse from her husband. She’s able to play these heavy dramatic moments just as well as the scenes where she escapes into child-like behaviour. Sadly though the story doesn’t have the depth her performance has and even though it tries to tackle heavy topics it’s surface level exploration of them and uneventful ending leaves this being charming and not much else

Name: The Whale
Review:
Venice Film Festival #15 - PalaBiennale

On the whole I don’t find play to film adaptations particularly interesting, often they lack the cinematic qualities that makes cinema such a thrilling art form. Yet, this is Daren Aronofsky, so it’s no surprise that he somehow manages to make one of the greatest stage to screen translations ever. Often when a film is confined to one location it can become stagnant, the film is at odds with the setting as it lacks the ability to keep the film fresh for its runtime. To my surprise this is never the case in The Whale, there’s a delicacy and flow to the camera that allows a two hour runtime to never feel dry despite it only taking place in one location.

Like other Aronofsky masterpieces, The Whale facilitates a standout lead performance in Brendan Fraser. Not enough can be said about how insanely talented Fraser is in this role. What he brings to this character is a rawness that feels so real it’s hard not to be emotional. He’s so magnetic to watch that you become transfixed as the film peels back the layers that make the character who he is. An unabashedly positive person pushing through the storm of his daily tasks as a result of his obesity. An unflinching love for those he has wronged in his life despite this not being reciprocated is heartbreaking to watch, it’s in this contrast that Aronofsky finds your heart strings and begins to pull. This is portrait of a complicated character, or rather a real person, and what Brendan is able to do unearth who the character is and why he’s in the situation he is, is nothing short of incredible, it’s a career defining performance for him.

Alongside him there’s an incredible supporting cast. Sadie Sink, playing his estranged daughter, is simply perfect, this will be the thing that catapulted her career more than Stranger Things. She plays a character so filled with hatred and teen angst that manages to never come off as hyperbolic and feels based in realism. The effervescent dynamic that is created in scenes between her and Fraser is lightning in a bottle. The way that they’re able to bounce off each other and fully give each other the space to express themselves is simply beautiful. Hong Chau and Ty Simpkins also deliver emotionally powerful performances that equally have their moments that shine in the film.

The genius of Aronofsky is how he’s able to paint a picture of different people and their perspectives but never casting judgement on them. Each other then has made mistakes, each of them has their own motivations for the emotions that drive them but the film takes care in how it expresses these emotions manifesting themselves. Often the emotions that enter Fraser’s house are ones of rage, deceit and hurt but the endless optimism that pours out of Fraser as he attempts to do one good thing in his life contrasted with these hateful emotions will endure tears.

As Fraser begins to unpack the emotions he’s bottled up, the film postulates the intersection between religion and love, and the place these take in the life of someone isolated from everyone else. He has a troubled relationship with both and how that’s got him to the place he is really is heartbreaking to learn. Aronofsky has done it again, something tonally different from his other films, but just as poignant. It also fades to white, and Aronofsky films that fade to white always are masterpieces

Name: Other People's Children
Review:
Venice Film Festival #16 – PalaBiennale

The film isn’t doing an awful lot that hasn’t been done before, this feels like an AI generated version of a French film, events happen because they’re supposed to not because it serves the story. Yet within this generic structure of a film, there’s a compelling story about the value of maternal love, placed on oneself and by others in their life. A generation that isn’t often explored in cinema, women as they enter the stage of their life where if they want a baby they must do so quickly, it was interesting to see how both the character and the people around her react to this information and how often it is disregarded by others.

A stunning lead performance from Virginie Efira, who captures this struggle of the longing to want children and the distress that comes from finding connections for them only to be stripped away from you without you having any ability to change this. The exploration of a woman’s role when she doesn’t fit the societal model that’s expected of her was an interesting concept, but it was a little surface level. Not a bad film, one with a great ending, but sadly the film just feels a bit too unfocused and generic for it really pack a punch

Name: Love Life
Review:
Venice Film Festival #17 - Sala Grande

A methodical and melancholic look into the life of a couple affected by a tragedy and how they go about trying to process their grief. The opening of this film is so wholesome and just such a beautiful look into a family in Japan. At times it’s a little slow and it seems to be dragging on but this is purposeful, as when the event happens I was so taken aback, as I hadn’t any knowledge of the plot, that it genuinely shocked me and left me stunned for far past when the event happens.

What follows is an attempt at deconstructing grief, it’s very subtle and far less showy that most films would opt for, which works both to its strength and its detriment. At times the characters seem emotionless in how they’ve decided to act despite what happened, and furthermore the film ends up following a different story entirely and never really tying back as strongly to the instigating event as much as it should.

As it goes on the film loses its tightness and starts unravelling as it focuses on different plots, which given the context of what happened is hard to believe at some points. However, there’s some pockets of genius hidden within about how tragedy brings people together or breaks them apart that are where the film is the best

Name: Don't Worry Darling
Review:
Venice Film Festival #18 - PalaBiennale

“Y’know, my favorite thing about the movie is, like, it feels like a movie. It feels like a real, like, you know, go to the theatre film, movie”

The amount of hate this film is getting is absurd, it has really shocked me how many people are able to get swept up into drama and let that influence their opinions of a film that’s presented to them. If we had truly watched the same thing there’s no way this film would be getting the abysmal scores it’s getting from some people.

For one, Florence Pugh delivers what might be her best performance of all time. Yes it’s true she does carry the film on her back but that’s simply because of how incredible she is here. The weakest part of the film, the writing, has absolutely no effect on her ability to fill the screen with this effervescent present that she’s been able to do in every project she’s been in. The fact that even in a film that isn’t the most impressive she still shines through, much like her work in Black Widow, cements her as one of the best working actresses at the moment.

To my surprise as well, after that infamous clip was released, Harry Styles delivers a decent performance. The fact that he can hold himself even just a little against the force that is Pugh is impressive. And shockingly his wavy accent is a choice? Well the film at least presents it that way, but wether or not this was something done retrospectively is unclear, however if this was a conscious choice then it’s kind of amazing.

All the other cast members turn in good performances, I particularly enjoyed Olivia Wilde acting, however none of the tertiary characters are developed enough meaning the actors don’t have a lot to chew on. This is a Florence Pugh film though and the film is well aware of that, and by focusing on her it makes for a far more engaging film.

The cinematography from Matthew Libatique is stunning too. It does a lot for creating this idyllic paradise that they’re supposed to be living in but also is able to convey the idea that there’s something deeper happening. Also an incredible score from John Powell that does wonders for building the anxiety throughout the film.

Sure the writing isn’t anything mind-blowing, it retreads a lot of the same threads explored in these kinds of films. The whole plot feels a little half baked, it meanders for a long time before getting into the meat and the antagonist isn’t explored nearly enough, but there’s some hidden gems in here. This idea of society’s romanticisation of the past, and especially from a male perspective really worked quite well until it becomes a bit too on the nose near the end. There’s good stuff within this film though, it’s no Booksmart, but it doesn’t deserve the abhorrent amount of hate it’s getting

Name: Lord of the Ants
Review:
Venice Film Festival #19 - Sala Grande

Despite this being a tale that needed to be told, it never really captures you attention as much as it wants you to. The first moments are so guteral and heart-breaking that when the film progresses as decides to focus on a drawn-out court case it loses the hold it has on you in the beginning.

Stuffed full of incredible performances, beautiful cinematography and a delicate score, the film is good but it's just not doing anything that hasn't been done before, and more so I think it's been done better. The film opts to sort of skip over the relationship that the film is built on which is to its detriment as you don't have as personal a connection to the characters as it expects you do. Not bad but sadly blander than you would wish to accompany such an important story

Name: The Banshees of Inisherin
Review:
Venice Film Festival #20 – PalaBiennale

One of the best reactions in a cinema I’ve ever experienced for a film. Period. The ingenious writing of McDonagh is able to take this relatively small-scale story, about two friends whose relationship changes suddenly when one decides to stop their friendship and elevate it to a comedic masterpiece that at the same time is extremely poignant and nuanced. The crowd reaction to this was simply sublime, laughter at all the right times and this palpable joy at watching this story play out. It’s hard to come by a comedy now that provokes such a strong reaction from everyone in the audience, but McDonagh writes with such a smart wit and situational humour that manages to land practically every time. Not all comedy has to be so explicit and, in your face, here it’s simply dialogue that does most of the work, and it does it to hilarious results.

Obviously, this humour wouldn’t work as well as it does were it not for the exceptional performances delivering such great writing. Colin Farrell, what a year he’s having between this, The Batman and After Yang, is sensational here, possibly my favourite performance he’s ever given, which is saying something. The layers he’s able to explore in what is a “simple” character is truly something incredible. He’s able to bring such a charismatic humour to the role that is never not completely delightful, but hidden underneath his charm, there’s such a human heart and a profanity in what he says, that once he’s lowered your guard with his humour, is even more striking than the already powerful dialogue.

The opposition to Farrell’s charming and endearing character is Brendan Gleeson’s sulking and rageful character. While Farrell’s outlook on life is glass half full, Gleeson is half empty, a man who’s contending with the idea that no one will remember him once’s he’s gone, and how this manifests itself as frustrations to the small island he lives on, and the bland friendship he’s invested in. Gleeson is able to unearth something really powerful with this, he’s stoic in his performance but there’s a constant bubbling of fear underneath. Also, a wonderful performance from Kerry Condon that’s just as witty as Farrell and another addition to the weird, quirky roles of Barry Keoghan.

It’s the dichotomy between these two opposing forces of the main characters that creates such a compelling story. Through them it’s explored whether or not one must cast aside the people they love in pursuit of something greater. The idea that one has to be surrounded by interesting people lest their life also become dull. There’s a particular poignant scene that debates the value of kindness in people that I found particularly compelling. Lots of meditative stuff here with a visionary voice behind the camera

Name: Saint Omer
Review:
Venice Film Festival #21 – Sala Perla

There’s been a lot of films at the festival like this, a dull and slow methodical take on a court case that thinks it’s more poignant than it is. Perhaps this is because this is the latest one I’ve seen, but this angered me. It starts quite strong, the basis of the case is laid out in detail and then the rest of the runtime proceeds to expand on the details of the case, yet you never really learn anything that important that wasn’t given in the summary at the start. It becomes frustratingly dull at points as the film just rehashes the same story over again. With the ridiculously long takes too, which showcase phenomenal acting, but are just flat and unengaging to watch. And colour me surprised when I saw Claire Mathon’s name pop up at the end, as far as her work goes this was a disappointment.

As mentioned, some amazing acting in here but particularly Guslagie Malanga who is able to take these long shots and dominate them. The film feels at odds with itself though, I really have no clue how the two stories were supposed to tie together, other than on a surface-level. One feels under baked and the other overdone, with neither having much to say, which leaves you feeling detached from the film as a whole

Name: The Eternal Daughter
Review:
Venice Film Festival #22 - PalaBiennale

Adding another addition to her catalogue of deeply personal and reflective films, in a way a spiritual successor to the Souvenir duology, or just a straight up sequel, The Eternal Daughter is a chilling and moving story that Joanne Hogg uses as a vessel to explore the relationship she has as a filmmaker to preserving stories of her life. What does it mean to construct a story about a person from your point of view? And is that ethical?

In the film Hogg wants to preserve the memories of her mother even if these memories aren’t entirely accurate as they’re fabricated from the protagonist’s perspective. What’s so fascinating about Hogg’s films are how she’s able to weave and intertwine her personal experiences with a fictional story that leaves the product being a unique blend of fiction and reality. The film is compelling for an audience but also it must be for her as she’s able to process her feelings on screen, and in doing that it finds a connection with people who resonant with her own story.

Set amidst the setting of a gothic mansion, Tilda Swinton in a double role of both mother and daughter is magnificent. This is no surprise, Swinton is one of the most powerful performers in Hollywood and this film just adds to that idea. Seeing her play opposite herself you understand why she’s such a great actress, as she’s able to bring something different to each role yet have similarities that clearly show they’re related. The narrative choice to have her play both is ingenious as well as it speaks to how we find ourselves in others. Not as strong as the Souvenir films but still a chilling and heartbreaking watch

Name: Beyond The Wall
Review:
Venice Film Festival #23 - Sala Darsena

One of the most awful experiences I’ve had in a cinema. There was a point where I had to cover my ears as the sound design genuinely began to hurt. For the most part the overbearing nature of the sound works to the film’s benefit. There’s an exquisite scene early on where the sound is used to join two locations, paired with some brilliant editing, the scene is able to convey the anxiety and stress it wants you to feel perfectly.

However, the film doesn’t let up after this point, it keeps using the sound design to provoke these reactions in you and at a point it becomes too much. It feels very much pornographic in how it revels in causing you this discomfort. Yet, the story was still interesting enough that the sound didn’t bother me until it really drove home the excessive nature of it at the end.

The film was enjoyable up until this bizarre plot twist that had me rethinking the whole movie, and not in the way you’d want. It ended up distracting me as I tried to understand what had happened, due to the film’s poor job at explaining how it made sense. It didn’t really contribute anything else to the film and actually ended flat for a film that had done a great job building up its story. A good film but a horrible experience

Name: Dreamin' Wild
Review:
Venice Film Festival #24 - Sala Grande

Saw this at the premiere and Zooey Deschanel looked at me, waved and blew me a kiss. So yeah.

The film itself is super sweet, it’s a slow methodical approach to a music film that plays more like something between a film and a visual album. It had this lovely vibe throughout that just exuded warmth and security. The story is nothing groundbreaking but it was a beautiful recount of a true story that seems fictional. It’s a lovely story about the sacrifices family makes for each other and the weight that can bear on other people. It dissects the relationship between fame and passion, and recognition and talent but all encapsulated within a family drama.

The cinematography is so understated yet so wonderfully elegant. The warm tones and subtle lighting in indoor environments contrasted with the natural splendour of the outdoor scenes maintains this beautiful atmosphere that is simultaneously enhanced by the songs accompanying it. This subtle way of filming brings a sense of authenticity to the film. Helmed by a fantastic performance from Casey Affleck this was a charming watch but Zooey Deschanel was wasted

Name: The Son
Review:
Venice Film Festival #25 – PalaBiennale

Zeller has fumbled his 2nd feature adapted from one of his plays with this hollow and unrealistic screenplay. As a portrayal of mental illness and how that affects not only the person suffering from it, but also the people around them, this was incredibly clumsy and misguided. The film plays out exactly how you expect, once the Chekov’s gun is revealed it’s simply you just waiting for that moment to happen. The film is more interested in emotional manipulation than telling a complex story that actual grapples with the complexities that come with depression. Moreover, ending is a microcosm of this fact. It’s played for gasps, despite being entirely predictable, but that in itself is a gross misuse of mental illness and playing it for shock value feels disingenuous to the topic it’s trying to show sympathy towards.

What really surprised me were the performances. Across the board the performances were a disappointment. Sadly, Hugh Jackman doesn’t shine as bright in the lead role, he’s not awful but it feels very preachy and not as authentic as a role like this requires. Vanessa Kirby is fine, but she’s given practically nothing to work with. Much the same can be said about Laura Dern, who despite her important role on the surface, she’s really wasted here, and in the scenes, she gets she feels wooden and less emotional than the dialogue would suggest. The biggest problem sadly is Zen McGrath. He doesn’t work in this film, the huge overacting he does feels like it belongs on the stage and not alongside the more cinematic performances in the film. The result is this disconnect between all the actors that makes a lot of the scenes feel incredibly forced and staged. When Anthony Hopkins shows up, he blows everyone away and weakens the other performances.

Despite it being about depression, the film lacks an understanding or sympathy towards it and uses it as a plot device. It feels like a pale imitation of other films that tackle this subject matter, like it tried to hit all the points a film like this is meant to hit but didn’t try to inject any emotion or connection to it. It will surely garner some emotional reactions, simply due to the end, but on a cinematic level this was verging on bad. The writing is shocking throughout, it doesn’t feel realistic at all, and it’s flat out bad in these emotional scenes. The scene in the hospital is written completely for plot and isn’t how it would play out in reality. The son is also written is such a bad way as he’s given no characteristics, he is his depression, which makes it hard to connect emotionally to him. From writing to directing to the overall production value, this felt staged and bland

Name: Blonde
Review:
Venice Film Festival #26 - Sala Grande

This movie could’ve been incredible.

I don’t think I’ve ever left a film feeling so conflicted after watching it, and with some time removed from the screening those feelings have become a bit clearer, and sadly they’re not positive.

Part of me thinks this deserves 5 stars, namely the performance from Ana De Armas. I mean she’s magnificent in the film. The film doesn’t ever lift its foot of the acceleration when it comes to the graphic and horrific experiences Monroe went through throughout her life and De Armas is able to channel the pain and torment Monroe went through with such compassion and realism that it’s at times hard to watch. It feels very much like the direction for her was “Ana, just keep crying” and she executes this perfectly. Often crying in film can feel forced or unnatural but somehow she’s balling her eyes out for most of the film and you feel every inch of sadness exuding from her.

As the film documents Monroe’s life she’s never really surrounded by the same people for that long, meaning that the supporting cast doesn’t really get a moment to shine but everyone else is brilliant. Adrien Brody and Xavier Samuel especially give wonderful performances.

There’s so much filmmaking on offer here. What I mean by that is Dominik throws everything he can into the movie. There’s changes in aspect ratio, blank and white portions, all different sorts of editing, weird framing, montages, Snorricam shots and odd pov shots. This results in some of the most gorgeous cinematography, many scenes are just beautiful to watch but it does beg the question why? It’s maximalist filmmaking but for what reason? It doesn’t really have any meaning or deepen the story on screen, it feels flashy and substance less.

Accompanying the beautiful visuals is a perfect suffocating score that manages to capture Marylin’s emotional journey perfectly. The production design and costumes are also wonderful. So what’s wrong with the film?

Contextually, I think this film is dangerous and offensive. First off, I suppose this is more of critique of the book the film is based off of, but the decision to make a film based on someone’s life that isn’t accurate to their life at all feels violating. The film makes no attempt to display that some of this story is twisted into a fictional retelling, which I thought was going to be the purpose of the change between colour and black and white, but alas that wasn’t what it was used for. Sure, you could argue that any biopic is a fictionalised version of someone’s life but this film goes out of its way to tell false events in Marilyn’s life.

Secondly, it feels odd to have a film that is so demeaning to Marilyn, someone who was already degrading enough during her time. This isn’t the feminist take on her life from her point of view that I expected going in, this is very “male gazey”. There’s an excessive amount of nudity, like De Armas spends 1/3 of the film nude, and is this ever really purposeful? There’s multiple sexual assault and rape scenes, that are trying to highlight the traumatic events Marilyn went through, but the grotesque and explicit ways in which these are filmed feel completely antithetical to what the film is trying to achieve. You have a woman who throughout her career was constantly sexualised and not valued as a person, so what does this film do to shine a light on her perspective? Have scenes where she’s treated as the object the media deemed she was. Numerous scenes where the film follows her panties, instead of her body, all the pointless topless scenes, a blowjob scene that is so perversely shot and lasts a while for no reason and a pov abortion scene.

The film doesn’t cast Marilyn as a person, it doesn’t give us information about how she felt during all this time, it doesn’t really feel interesting in exploring who she really was at all. The one thing we learn about Marilyn throughout the film is that she had daddy issues, and even this gets hammered home too much and takes a perverse turn with Marilyn calling everyone Daddy in the last half of the film. The film feels like a betrayal to who Marilyn was, there was a story there but the film avoiding it and decided to tell a misogynistic and perverse story that is all too family in Hollywood

Name: No Bears
Review:
Venice Film Festival #27 - Sala Grande

An odd film that requires some knowledge of the director going into it to fully appreciate the story going on. Admittedly I didn’t know of Panahi going into the film but after learning about him after I find his story so inspiring. The film itself however is just lacking something. It’s told in two parts that don’t fully connect, a glaringly obvious thread that’s been running throughout the whole festival. One story is of a film he’s directing within the film but he’s not able to direct it in person so does so over video calls. The other is the story of a relationship issue he gets himself involuntary involved with in the village he’s staying at to direct the film.

The connection between these two stories is very thin, this idea of love and the challenges that oppose it. Neither story was that compelling to me, the film is more focused on the director and how he gets pulled into this story, not the stories going on around him which takes away from them as they aren’t developed at all. Was a pleasant watch though

Name: Copenhagen Cowboy
Review:
Venice Film Festival #28 - Sala Darsena

NWR started the premiere by saying “Fuck the establishment” and ended it by calling Hideo Kojima. Yeah might be the coolest experience I’ve had in a cinema. What a brilliant idea it is to watch a TV show in a room full of people who’re just as excited to watch it. It gives the show a completely different feeling and was so fun to share this experience with so many people.

The show is a methodical and meticulously mastered piece of filmmaking. It’s neo-western, something I didn’t know was a thing but man was it enjoyable. The entire show is just one woman going from place to place and exacting revenge on those who do bad. It’s transfixing as the story is paired with the staple neon visuals and electronic score that usually accompany his work. What impresses me here is how he’s able to set the TV show in locations where the neon lights are not just for stylistic flare but also feel natural. A wonderful, perfectly paced, and surprisingly funny story of a woman on a quest to do good, if only it had ended before going into the weird ending which confused me so much and felt stapled onto the show

Name: All the Beauty and the Bloodshed
Review:
Venice Film Festival #29 - Sala Darsena

So glad this won The Golden Lion, it wasn’t my favourite of the festival but it’s one the best documentaries I’ve seen and was such a unique way to tell a story. There’s a line in the film where Nan talks about real life being dirty and memories don’t ever have the dirt in them. This is a thesis statement for why she loves photography, to show the dirt in the moments but also show how beautiful the dirt is, it’s a gorgeous way to sum up her work and this film

Name: Welcome to the Club
Review: This was a little better than the rest of them, but why are they still being made, they would work as shorts in front of Disney films

Name: Starter for 10
Review: It’s on me for thinking that in 2006 filmmakers actually wrote women with some semblance of personality that isn’t dependent on a man

Name: Thor: Love and Thunder
Review: Gets worse with every rewatch. This screams lazy direction and the reliance on visual effects is more noticeable on a small screen. Love some stuff but there’s too much boring or unfunny scenes that it makes the film feel like a chore

Name: Do Revenge
Review: So, I wasn’t expecting much from this and then the rating here gave me higher expectations, but what is everyone smoking? This film has a lot of fun once it gets through the predictable set up that takes up the majority of the plot. But prior to this moment when the film turns, it’s a really boring by the books film. I really enjoyed the twist and wish it happened a lot earlier as that plot would’ve been more interesting to explore. And surprisingly this film has made me think Camila Mendes, who I only know as being on Riverdale, is a great actress

Name: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
Review: First time this film really worked for me, the music is amazing and when the fellowship was founded might be one of the coolest moments in film

Name: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
Review: Takes a bit to get going but Helm’s Deep is unbelievable

Name: Everything Everywhere All at Once
Review: I cried again

Name: See How They Run
Review: Exactly what you expect going into it, takes a while to get going but was really fun when it got into the plot. It’s like if Wes Anderson directed a whodunnit. Saorise Ronin was a wholesome treat in this, easily the reason to see it

Name: The Woman King
Review:
Despite its new setting for blockbusters, The Woman King ends up being a pretty generic action flick that doesn’t make use of its unique historical backdrop. Opting to tell a story more focused on the warrior aspect rather than the deep history, that as it’s set in West Africa during the Slave Trade, is ripe for the taking seems like a wildly missed opportunity. The film is by no means bad but when there’s a clear aspect that makes the film different, and would allow it to go a little deeper, it’s disappointing.

Yet, there’s a lot that works. The cast is phenomenal. Oddly enough this isn’t really Viola Davis’ film, despite all the marketing suggesting otherwise. Thuso Mbedu is the lead of the film and does a wonderful job bringing a powerful performance to the screen. Despite her motivations being a little thin, it’s never not enjoyable to watch her rise as one of the warriors led by Viola Davis. Even though Viola Davis isn’t the lead that doesn’t mean she isn’t as flawless as usual. What’s really powerful is how much she’s able to just act with her face. There’s a deep, tragic history behind each of her facial expressions that doesn’t need explaining to understand what she’s went through. Also there’s another incredible performance from Lashana Lynch that steals the scenes she’s in.

Baring the great ensemble though, the film is very thin, hence why the avoidance of diving deeper on the history feels like a wasted opportunity. The story meanders and doesn’t really have a lot of substance that it could’ve been a quicker film and nothing would change. Also the score really annoyed me, much like the rest of the film it was generic and could’ve been something better using instruments that suited the setting instead of being a normal action score

Name: Rogue One: A Star Wars Story
Review: Greig Fraser is a god

Name: Crimes of the Future
Review: This is a bad film and simply having the name attached to it is making people think it’s better. It genuinely might be one of the worst examples of dialogue I’ve heard in a film, every line is either expository or simply unnatural. The performances feel like a parody of themselves and the takeaway from the film is exactly what I thought going into it. It goes as far as to jeopardise the thematic idea it built in the film. Vapid and substance less and weirdly not that gory

Name: Uncharted
Review: Not a good movie but weirdly a comfort one. Holy shit is Tom Holland good looking in this

Name: Cha Cha Real Smooth
Review: Something so special about seeing emotionally vulnerable men in film

Name: Mean Girls
Review: If the Academy would’ve nominated comedies and more female led projects back then, then this would’ve been nominated for writing

Name: Werewolf by Night
Review: Michael Giacchino, the acclaimed composer and now the talented director. Seriously though for this to be a directorial debut is unbelievable. There’s a clear vision and through line for what Giacchino was attempting to recreate, but what’s even better is how he iterated on this to create something suitable for modern audiences. Never did I think there’d be a project in the MCU that harkens back to gothic horror but strangely it really worked. This style allowed for Marvel to push the boundaries on the amount of violence and gore than normally accompanies an MCU film. You’re able to tell that this was directed with sound as a clear motivator. The way that the score effortlessly pairs with the visuals and the stunning sound design that allows for these moments of horror to leave an impact is truly a feat of filmmaking. How he’s able to weave the sound design into diegetic sounds that make sense in the world he’s created was so impressive. This is truly one of the few Marvel projects that really feels like a departure from the MCU formula in a good way. If anything this project was just lacking in its story. None of it is bad, but it’s just a little thin. The film does a wonderful job at setting up the main characters, who both of which are so good they need to be given more projects, but doesn’t give them a lot to do, this is due to the runtime being so short, this doesn’t allow us to get to know them. But it’s a refreshing entry into the MCU and as Marvel puts more trust in their creatives, projects will become new and exciting, if they now make unique, quality projects, the MCU is in good hands

Name: Emily
Review:
So normally I’m very clued into what’s releasing but to my surprise I had no idea this was coming out until it was in cinemas. It was only because I got an advanced screening that I went to this, it seemed like the standard period piece/ biopics that are made that have no unique aspects and al feel like the same film. However, this blew past my expectations.

This film had style that much of the other entries in the genre lack. It felt like an art house film, not in the typical sense where it’s very explicit you’re watching one, but in a more subdued and nuanced way that worked for the film. The cinematography surpassed what’s normally done for films in the genre and these visuals did a lot for giving the film a distinctive look and style that sets it apart from the barrage of period dramas.

The reason I saw the film was curiosity over Emma Mackey, as I love her in Sex Education, and my word was she phenomenal. Playing a character over a number of years is he’d but she’s able to let you understand how Emily changed over time and how her previous experiences led to where she is. The screenplay is also just so sharp that there was numerous times I sat and had to pause over the audacity and profoundness of some of the lines. Sadly the marketing is non existent for this film along with it being in a genre where there’s too many films, I don’t think it will perform that well

Name: She-Hulk: Attorney at Law
Review:
Let’s get this out of the way. This show is nowhere near as bad as it’s been made out to be. It’s fine. As far as Marvel outings into TV have went, this is actually quite good, it’s only when you compare this to other comedies, that it’s not as cohesive nor as funny as sitcoms it’s trying to imitate.

Again though, I really enjoyed this. It has its peaks and also it’s bad moments but I think the overall show was a glowing success. This is mainly thanks to Tatiana Maslany. She is unbelievable. Instantly a classic Marvel character, which is a feat as there’s so many now. She is effortlessly charming and so effervescent on screen that no matter what’s going on you’re instantly captivated by how she commands the screen. It’s hard to have such a humorous performance with good writing, yet with writing that doesn’t always hit, it’s even harder and yet she manages to always be funny, to make the bad writing work.

The writing isn’t always that bad, in the episode I would say that the comedy is more hit than miss. The problem occurs with the pacing. The show is written like most of the other MCU shows and not like the sitcoms it’s imitating. There’s a flow to comedy writing and the show doesn’t always nail it. If opts to sit in moments that would otherwise be fast in a comedy show, it’s missing the snappiness that often accompanies such shows. It means that when episodes end you sometimes feel unsatisfied as not much happened in an episode.

This wasn’t always a problem, it mainly happened in the middle of the show, but towards the end, especially the last two episodes, the pacing really worked. Also, the writing culminating in the way that it did was ingenious. How the show was essentially a critique of the Marvel formula and the meta commentary surrounding how women are treated by fandoms was amazing. Possibly the best finale of a Marvel TV show.

It’s not perfect but I really enjoyed this, with a fun cast and some good writing, it was just a blast to watch and Daredevil weirdly worked

Name: Amsterdam
Review:
This is possibly one of the biggest disappointments I’ve ever had in a cinema. What the fuck is this? Honestly not that mad cause David O. Russell has done some bad shit, but I went in trying not to view this with that in mind yet it ended up being awful anyway. How do you have a cast like this and have every single one of them deliver their worst performance, except Anya Taylor-Joy who is the only person able to make this enjoyable.

The three leads in this film feel like they’re acting in different films which is the fault of the director. If you’re unable to bring a cohesiveness to the set which the actors understand what tone the film is going for then not matter how good the actors are, it’s not going to come together. The film is so tonally inconsistent it’s hard to find out how you should react. Was the film meant to be funny? I found myself laughing at the film not with it. There’s a moment at the end that I laughed at loud at how bad it was, but most people in the cinema didn’t even though it’s possibly one of the worst storylines I’ve ever watched

Name: Black Panther: Wakanda Forever
Review:
“My heart was buried with her”

The undertaking of this film by Coogler is no small feat. Faced with the tragedy of Boseman and having to incorporate that into the story in a way that was both respectful and continued the legacy of his character in the right way was a difficult thing to do, but to Coogler’s credit he seemed to do it seamlessly. The whole film felt like this emotional tribute to Chadwick and a collective processing of this tragedy both in our world and in the world of the MCU.

The delicacy of how they chose to write the characters we’ve come to know was beautiful. It’s a study on how grief moulds and shapes people, how it changes them over time, yet what I think was most poignant is how the base of everyone was still the same. Shuri, who has undergone a massive amount of grief and guilt, is clearly a changed person, yet there’s glimpses we get of her more upbeat personality, but instead of this being her personality it’s more a relief from the guilt she feels, a glimpse to a future that could’ve been.

The latest Marvel projects have been hit or miss, but what can’t be denied is the massive step up in performances that these projects have featured, and Wakanda Forever not only continues this trend, but might feature some of the best performances within the MCU. This is an ensemble cast, and by that I mean that as a collective they’re an acting force. There’s not a single performance that doesn’t feel wrapped up in emotion and is so palpable it’s able to felt through the screen. 

Letitia Wright specifically is an exemplary lead for the film. Strangely, upon rewatching Black Panther before this I didn’t realise how little of a character she is in that film. She’s essentially there for comic relief and to be the tech person for Wakanda, but other than that she doesn’t do that much. In contrast, to have her helm an entire film that already had so many eyes on it and to do it so beautifully, I was simply blown away by her performance. She is the emotional lynchpin that ties the film together. I found myself teary eyed for the majority of the film just due to her so delicately struggling with the grief she feels. Her entire character arc is so expertly written and she grounds it with such care that it ends up being a stunning, cathartic release for Shuri but also the audience.

Alongside her, possibly the standout of the film, is Angela Bassett. My Lord, what a performance. In the ways which Wright’s performance is more subdued and nuanced, Bassett is maximalist in how she conveys her emotions, but to no less effect. She’s simply magnetic on screen, every time she appeared she demanded your attention and didn’t let go of you until she left the screen. I loved how much time they took with her character in the beginning, she basically felt like the main character for the first half. It felt like watching royalty, but it also felt just as much like watching a grieving mother having to deal with her hurt while maintaining her position as a global figure.

Danai Gurira and Lupita Nyong’o return and also deliver outstanding performances. You’re also experiencing their hurt just as much as you are the main characters. There’s something so beautiful watching every character deal with grief in a different way. Newcomer Dominique Thorne was a pleasant comic relief in the film, but also fell to some of the same Marvel trappings we’ve come to know. Another standout performance was from Tenoch Heurta. The way in which Namor was written as an antagonist is some of the best writing for a villain Marvel has to offer.  How they managed to humanise him and get you to sympathise with his cause, yet also allowing a hate to fester inside you against him was brilliant. This was only enhanced further by the powerful performance from Heurta. He makes long scenes of explanatory dialogue feel rhythmic, and makes it such a pleasure to watch. He was simply fantastic at toeing the line between love and hate that it’s hard not to leave the film enamoured by his portrayal.

Much like the first film, this sequel boasts a delectable score from Ludwig Goransson. The way in which he’s crafted a world in his music is something to be admired. The sounds of Wakanda are just as tangible as the people. In this film he also gets to create the sounds of Talocan, which to no one’s surprise are amazing. It’s just refreshing to have blockbuster scores that don’t feel generic and actually use the film and the culture in it to inform how it should sound.

Not only is the score just as high quality, but also the production design. The costuming, on the whole, is phenomenal. The more natural clothing is pitch perfect, the superhero costumes might leave a bit to be desired however. Okoye’s suit in particular at the end looks very haphazardly put together when compared to the rest of the cast. But the world of Wakanda, and Talocan, is simply stunning. The use of locations we’ve seen previously, in particular the Throne Room, and also new locations that felt right contextually were just beautiful.

The beauty of this film is only enhanced by the wonderful cinematography that the film has to offer. Often Marvel films may have sequences that looks visually stunning, but it’s rare to say that about the entire film. This film delivers in that front. Much like how the performances and writing takes a slower pace, it feels like the camera takes it’s time, as if it too is mourning the loss of Boseman. What transpires is truly magnificent scenes that help you feel the weight of grief.

It does have its problems. Ironheart as a character is heavily underbaked. She’s essentially a plot device for the film and while her moments of levity are good in the moment, I don’t think they were necessary. However, the way they’ve set up her character and the burden she’s going to feel I really hope is explored as they’ve set themselves up to have a rich and complex character. There’s been complaints I’ve read of Ross’s story also being unnecessary, but in all honesty his story is so inoffensive and hardly explored that I didn’t mind the necessary detour for the story to progress.

I’ve also read criticisms that the film isn’t action oriented, and I have to say I don’t understand this at all. I thought the film was well balanced in terms of handling the action Marvel fans expect and the nuanced dialogue we got, if anything I would’ve loved more dialogue scenes as they were the best parts of the film. The film does run long however, but I’m honestly not sure what you’d cut. For all it’s shortcomings this film did what it needed to do and did that well. I was a mess for 3 hours and teared up multiple times. The care and love that was put into this film by everyone is felt emanating from the screen. Those final few minutes as well are just perfect

Name: Weird: The Al Yankovic Story
Review: Clearly trying to be a parody of the absurd amount of musical biopics that keep being released and does it to middling effects. The story itself is fun, it’s obviously not trying to be realistic account of what happened but it’s honestly not that absurd as it thinks it is. It ends up falling into the same trappings that makes these films just feel like carbon copies of each other. I think this comes down to presentation, the writing isn’t as sharp or quippy in these less absurd scenes, and it just ends up feeling like a tonally off biopic. Some funny scenes, decent performances and Madonna as a drug Lord, it’s a fun watch

Name: Taylor Swift: Reputation Stadium Tour
Review: How tf does someone do this with a cold?!?

Name: Middleditch & Schwartz
Review:
“There’s a male gazelle behind you”

Still the greatest comedy special

Name: Glass Onion
Review:
Rian Johnson is back again to prove he’s the master of the whodunnit. The sequel boasts the same impeccable witty dialogue between a kaleidoscope of colourful characters set amidst beautiful modern and contemporary sets, it’s hard not to love. 

The film struggles to find its footing in the first act, it really takes a while to set up the story, the dialogue isn’t as punchy and it seems to meander on its way to the main plot. However, in a way it feels necessary to allow for a sensational second act that allows the film’s main star, Janelle Monet, to truly shine. She gives a wonderful performance that really grounds the film amongst the chaos of everyone else. Kate Hudson and Daniel Craig also shine in their comedic roles.

I think the film falls a little flat at the end but this is still a blast to watch. The mystery is really compelling and ends up being overtly obvious yet still surprising which is such a feat to pull off. It lives up to its allegorical title but sadly doesn’t surpass the genius of the original

Name: Solo: A Star Wars Story
Review: Y’know what this movie could’ve been something. Donald Glover is phenomenal, as usual, and I honestly think the third act twist is really well done. How often do we see the hero just straight up kill the villain without giving them a chance to lay out their plan? It’s refreshing to have smart storytelling. There’s just some great character choices in the final third that really would’ve made this something had this not been some of the most annoying and boring filmmaking up until this moment. NOT EVERYTHING NEEDS TO BE EXPLAINED. It happens so much in this film that it becomes funny but it’s insane how someone read this script and said this is good. Han isn’t a character in this, he’s a sum of the things we know about him which means he ends up being a husk of the character people love

Name: The Guardians of the Galaxy Holiday Special
Review: FUN! James Gunn knows how to write comedy and mix it with the write amount of heart. "This isn't a Christmas gift; this is human trafficking."

Name: Looking: The Movie
Review: A fitting ending for the show, Patrick’s character continues to get worse as the show went on and this follows from that. I think that’s cause the show, and so the film, decides to paint the relationships and the reasons that lead them to not working as very black and white, it doesn’t really explore the nuances and it either blames Patrick for a relationship failing or exonerates him of any blame, when the reality is more complex than how it’s shown

Name: The Devil Wears Prada
Review: Iconic. That’s all

Name: Causeway
Review: Was so good to have Jennifer Lawrence back in a dramatic role, she's truly underrated as far as modern actresses go. She was phenomenal in this, a more subdued performance but nonetheless it was still so rich and authentic, it's the reason to watch the film. Also really loved the subdued cinematography and how it reflected her mental state. Despite it's restrait and more surface level look at trauma, this is still a pleasurable watch anchored by two great performances

Name: Entergalactic
Review: Some good animation, the blend of animation styles was absurdly beautiful. Also the rom com story really worked as an animated film, I just wish there was more meat on the bone. The entire film is beyond basic, there’s no real depth to the plot at all. More so, some plot points are introduced and have no real resolve or reason to be included. The songs didn’t feel ceremonious enough for it to be a visual album either which results in the film being a pleasant but uneventful watch

Name: Avatar
Review: Gets more hate than it warrents but there's some clear issues. The dialogue is attrocious in areas, and the whole opening sequence is so poorly handled in setting up the world. It's so exposition heavy and it's delivered in such a forced way. Jake Sully lacks the charisma or depth to lead the film, luckily Zoe Salanda is there, who truly makes this film, despite only ever shown in her Navi form she delivers an incredible performance filled with emotion. Also I love how Michelle Rodriguez plays the same character in everything, she's able to get away with the cheesy dialogue so well

Name: Avatar: The Way of Water
Review: No, every other single review wasn't lying, this really is a visually stunning film. As expected, this film is mind blowing visually. No matter how much I was told this before going in, it still managed to surpass my expectations. I saw this in IMAX 3D which is the way to watch this film. I've never really been a fan of 3D; I just don't think it particularly enhanced any of the films I saw - I was wrong. This allowed me to be transported to the magical world of Pandora. The depth of field effects that they're able to achieve with this technology is phenomenal, and it doesn't feel like an afterthought, it's clear that the intention of the film being made for this technology was always there. Much like its predecessor there's issues with the story. Strangely, this film follows the exact same beats as the first one. It is the exact same story just with slightly different characters and locations. For such an interesting world filled with interesting characters, it's so strange that this is the story they decided to tell. However, credit where credits due, this is a better version of that story. It was such a wise choice to focus on the children throughout the film instead of Jake Sully, as they were instantly more complex than him with far more interesting dynamics. Many people are critiquing this middle section that meanders and doesn't focus on the main plot but instead explores characters and their stories. That to me was far more interesting that the basic plot surrounding it. Cameron has built such an intricate world that I honestly found it more interesting watching the characters breathe and explore these new areas of Pandora. It is unfocused and takes away from the main story, but I don't think it detracts from it. There's so much going on in this sequel that it isn't able to balance it all. There are so many characters to set up, so many. What ends up happening is some main characters just completely disappear for long periods and then suddenly appear later. This is excusable for some of the insane amount of children in the film, but for Zoe Saldana to just be wasted feels so strange. She just isn't really in the film but is simultaneously always there when she's needed. This becomes the main crutch of Avatar. There are complex things going on in the characters’ lives, but the film makes no attempt to actually explore what they're feeling in any depth. Having just joined a new tribe, feeling out of place and unable to keep a grip on her children, why isn't how she's feeling explored at all. The same thing happens all over the film, things happen to the characters but there's no real reactions to what happens. Also, just a weird waste of Kate Winslet too, she did nothing. There are just too many characters that they're trying to establish for them to form any meaningful emotional attachment to them. It's just very strange, the visuals are there, not only the visual effects but also an improvement in how it's shot - I still hate those digital zooms though - the action is a big step up, there's a more cinematic quality all around, but the writing really lets this down. The dialogue is still really clunky in areas, granted it's a vast improvement over what it was, but still it felt ripped out of a cheesy 2000s film. What these films are great at is the world-building, the best character in the film is Pandora, the more it's explored the more you become invested. All the set-up of exploring this new land really pays off in a phenomenal third act, that makes use of all the set up prior. The entire scene was just beyond visually beautiful and the choreography was insane. I also loved how it was basically a greatest hits of Cameron's films. It doesn't even really matter what the story is for this film, the fact that it exists is unbelievable. Visuals alone make this a feat of filmmaking. I need a documentary of how this film was made as the whole time I was watching this I had no idea

Name: The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent

The visuals hold up, and in 2D you really get to experience the beautiful cinematography on offer, but the dialogue is unbelievably bad in places, it sometimes gets away with it but I doubt the intention is to have people laughing at how awful some of the lines are. Still this is a cinematic achievement in itself, if simply becuase I have no idea how this was made and made so well

However, this sadly means the two leads are given virtually no character development in exchange for focusing on the investigation. Just a decent investigative reporter film chalk full of some great performances

The songs get tiresome after the first act, why is there 3 versions of the same Octavia Spenser song?!? Also wtf is the plot, who pitched it in a writers room “What if we made A Christmas Carol but with Ryan Reynolds, oh and yeah why don’t we make the plot that his decisions lead to a child committing suicide”. The only thing going for this is the production value but that’s to be expected it being an Apple TV+ film

Name: Dear Evan Hansen
Review: Wow. I knew this was bad but this isn’t what I expected. I thought the film was going to be bad based on the merits of the film, I didn’t expect to hate this based on the story. The musical is on Broadway, this led me to believe that the plot the film was based on was at least some what good considering IT’S ON BROADWAY. But wtf is this. The story is disgusting, I kinda see what they’re doing but I also really don’t. I get that it’s trying to help a family but the self serving nature of what he does mixed with how deep it goes feels so bad. I’ve never been more uncomfortable with a character’s decisions in a film ever. The music also is just really weird. It opens with a great song, then proceeds to have another good song that’s upbeat right after the event which tonally doesn’t work. Then every song after just feels the same. I really don’t understand why this has had so much love. Also every time Ben Platt was on screen it took me out of it cause I was like why is a full ass grown man hanging around high schoolers
