Name: You're Cordially Invited
Review: Had fun, I guess, but be fucking real, the last stinger at the end of this film just wouldn’t happen

Name: The Seed of the Sacred Fig
Review:
Heavy, profound and important filmmaking. Kind of blown away by the sheer level of guts it takes to make something this defiant and uncompromising for the world.

I was completely unaware of the situation in Iran prior to this movie and really appreciated the way this story approached educating its viewer without feeling hamfisted. They managed to integrate a way of getting you aware of the situation in the country also work narratively and I think that’s incredibly impressive.

As a result of this, the first third is disconnected to the other two thirds of the movie. This is not a bad thing whatsoever, it instead allows the first third to set up the ideologies which are integral to understanding the characters as the film proceeds. 

The other two thirds of the movie play into tension and stress like many anxiety inducing films before it. It centres around a missing gun and that manages to make the rest of the movie feel so unbelievably stressful. It purposefully builds and builds to a thrilling conclusion which serves as a metaphor for the political unrest that has plagued Iran.

In doing so the ending does feel a little comical in how they choose to portray a character to serve as this metaphor. It didn’t completely take me out of the movie, but it does a disservice to how well the characters were built up prior.

Another slight issue which is hard to get past is that the back half of the film hinges on a decision a character makes and I just don’t think the film does a good enough job, if any, at exemplifying why this decision is made. It’s easy to dismiss this thought and get enthralled in the tension but it is still a lingering issue I had. When so much of the film relies on character ideologies and understanding motivations it’s sad that the most pivotal decision lacks reasoning.

Still a thoroughly entertaining and important watch which has at the very least provoked a lot of thoughts surrounding the generational perspectives on progressive values and how bearing the weight of violence results in only more violence

Name: The Last Showgirl
Review: I liked this. I wanted to love this. The Last Showgirl would be nothing without its magnetic centre woman, Pamela Anderson. She does so much here in giving depth to this woman which perhaps was not found in the writing. The joy, the pain, the sorrow, the regrets, the love is written across her face and you are immediately enamoured by her performance. She does so much to expand upon the themes which were teed up in the film, the ideas of nostalgia and complacency, chasing your dreams and achieving them, even if to an outsider they seem small and pointless, the exchange of achieving goals and the weight that holds on other aspects of your life and the nuances of motherhood. I think the film skims the surface with a lot of these themes and I desperately wished it dived deeper and had more to say. The exploration of these themes simply comes from Anderson’s wonderful performance but if only there was writing to match this. I actually did like the creative decision to sometimes meander and have Pamela explore the city of Vegas via montage. It said a lot without explicitly having to say it. This crumbling city around her, something that’s increasingly being replaced with exploitation she still manages to find the beauty in it. Like these scenes were glorious, we just also needed more scenes to chew on. Halfway through the movie we do get more meat and exploration of said themes but it just never amounts to anything more. I ended up projecting themes I think onto the movie as a way of hoping it had explored those themes. What is maybe lacking in substance, it makes up for in style. This film is glorious to look at and immediately establishes a vibe and maintains this throughout. The way it was shot, the transcendent score, it all made for this dreamlike vibe which I fell in love with. Except for the odd choice for a quarter of this to be out of focus. I just thought it looked stunning. The scene of Pamela looking over the Vegas buildings was… no notes. Really wanted to love this and did walk away loving the vibes but just not in love with the overall result. Pamela Anderson gives a bloody incredible performance however she really is the star of the show

Name: Bridget Jones: Mad About the Boy
Review:
Colour me shocked! I…loved this. Hands down the best Bridget Jones has ever been and it’s not even close.

Who would’ve thought that clever situational comedy instead of hammering on about how somebody is “fat” works better as a comedy. And what’s more this movie just has an abundance of heart to it. I did not expect to walk out saying I cried twice but I did. Sure, I’m an overly emotional person when it comes to films, but I really think this film struck a really lovely level of depth vs comedy.

The biggest compliment I can give this film is that it felt real. This was a real woman grieving and navigating her life, in that messy way we all do. Nothing is ever always sad or always laughable and this film taps into that.

Placing the character of Bridget Jones, who’s always been a delight, into a more grounded story and just letting her be funny was a fantastic decision and I thoroughly enjoyed this. It’s like genuinely a great screenplay.

Name: Captain America: Brave New World
Review:
Oh. Oh no. Look, I really am not one of those dismissive film fans who believes the MCU is incapable of making anything good, if anything I think I get shit some times for d*ck riding the MCU from friends and being forgiving of some of the issues in their movies, cause by and large I'm invested in the larger story of the MCU. So, when I say this is dogshit, know that it truly is. 

  

I seriously don't understand how this was even put forward to audiences. Sure, if you go in to switch your brain off, it's a whatever action movie. If you decide to activate your brain watching this, the plot falls apart almost immediately. I won't spoil who the main villain is but their so called "plan", which is supposed to be this hyper intelligent plan, where all these moves are planned out probabilistically to get the result of the movie, makes NO SENSE. Quite frankly I felt insulted as a viewer. Hey, let's just distract you enough to make you not question anything cause the minute you do it falls to pieces.  

  

I've seen many people say they can feel the reshoots, which is a glaringly problematic issue when so many people are saying this, and you do certainly feel reshoots, but it's not as if this is the first movie to have reshoots. I think the issue of these reshoots lies in the direction given to string together a story and the god-awful editing. 

  

The editing in this film is all over the place, there is absolutely no rhythm to this movie whatsoever. Scenes sometimes just end, others drag. Something that truly pissed me off though is not a single action scene - bar maybe the last scene with the Red Hulk - felt tension filled, or like it had any weight as it rapidly goes between shots allowing for no sense of positional awareness. I have no idea if the fight scenes were good cause I don't know what was happening half the time, and when the lead of your movie is not a super solider and relies on a lot of hand-to-hand combat, this is a real shame. However, even the in-air stuff was super rough. 

  

I do think sadly the editing started from a low point already as when scene to scene the film looks completely different, trying to find a way to seamlessly edit becomes impossible. 

  

I did have low expectations going into this, I knew of these reshoots and I just felt like this isn't necessarily something Marvel wanted to release. But that doesn't excuse the fact that this went into production. WHY would anybody choose to make the CAPTAIN AMERICA movie essentially a The Incredible Hulk sequel? 

  

In a way I feel like it's commendable to start to pull together your universe. The Incredible Hulk, Eternals and The Falcon and The Winter Soldier being put in a blender under the allure of a political thriller? Sure, why not that sounds really creative, if pulled off well. Well, it wasn't. 

  

If you're going to tell this story, you need to have something to say. This felt so empty and void of anything. It's titled Captain America: Brave New World, so why did I leave feeling like nothing really happened to Captain America at all. And weirdly I think the film knows this. When the film starts, well firstly there's no Marvel Studios fanfare which is telling, but when it goes to splash the title of the movie it's simply Brave New World. The film knows this is not a good Captain America movie. 

  

If anything, I weirdly think this is a Thunderbolt Ross movie. Which I guess brings me to my first positive, Harrison Ford. Sure, he's not quite the same Thunderbolt Ross as before, he's written differently, but Ford really gives a lot to this performance and I enjoyed him. It's Ross who gets the only real arc of the movie and it was clearly the most interesting part but barely. 

  

The film shines (rare) in its quiet moments, of which I only think of two. Sam and Joaquin have a scene at the end, and Sam also gets a moment with another person (no spoilers) which is great. Why are these moments great? Oh, because Sam finally becomes the character, we know him to be. 

  

A major flaw with this film is that Sam Wilson, a character who has been in the MCU for ages, and has an established personality, becomes a vessel for expository dialogue for the majority of this film. He is just a boring lead, Mackie does what he is able to but there's just nothing going on with his character. 

  

It's actually frustrating cause even in bad MCU films this is normally still a strength, the film might be bad but at least I'm enjoying watching the main character, and this film fails at even doing that. So, when you get these quiet moments where Wilson shows his personality, they stick out so much. Oh, and everything with him and Issiah Bradley is good, but that's cause Carl Lumbly is so wonderful. 

  

I think the film suffers cause, well it's bad, but also cause of how it was marketed. Firstly, Red Hulk is all over the promo and, well he's hardly in it, and furthermore is the plot point which you build up to in the third act. The trailers also lean into this political thriller vibe, like The Winter Solider, and I'm sorry but saying that something is a thriller, and like putting a thriller skin over a movie doesn't make it thrilling. 

  

There's no mystery here, and perhaps that got lost in the reshoots, but it's very obvious who's pulling the strings and there's no real meat to the politics. It's still good vs bad. What makes Civil War effective is there are sides to the story, you as the viewer get to decide who is right. What makes Winter Soldier effective is that it's actually a political thriller and you don't know who to trust, NONE of that exists here. The main political thing of the movie, the fight over the Eternal in the Ocean, is settled via a news broadcast shown on a TV?!? 

  

It's not surprising, the writing is fucking dreadful throughout and the reliance on using news broadcasts to update the plot began to feel so lazy. It's cool if you want to commit to pulling together different MCU stories together, like as a fan I appreciate that, but please find a better way of doing this than force feeding viewers multiple times to remind people of stuff. DID YOU KNOW ROSS HAS A DAUGHTER CALLED BETTY? 

  

Yeah, this pissed me off. It's cool they're starting to bring together stuff from the MCU but is this really supposed to get me excited for Avengers, and more over you didn't set up Sam Wilson as a leader. Oh, also it looked fine but also some of the worst green screen ever in an MCU movie

Name: Lady Gaga & the Muppets Holiday Spectacular
Review: I have no other words for this than Artpop. Would like to thank Mikes Mic for letting me know of its existence

Name: Wallace & Gromit: The Curse of the Were-Rabbit
Review: Literally how was this made

Name: I'm Still Here
Review:
Really adored this. Loved how we just get to sit with the family for quite an extended period of time at the start of the film, some may say this is unnecessary or indulgent, but I think it necessary to immerse you in the family. You truly feel a part of the family and by the time the father is ripped from them, you feel the anguish the family is put through so profoundly. It’s harrowing but beautiful.

The rest of the film plays out as a gorgeous character study on the matriarch of this family, helmed by a powerhouse performance from Fernanda Torres. She’s absolutely breathtaking in this film. Such a subtle and nuanced performance. It’s like she is playing two characters in her own life, the performative brave strong mother for her children and the grief and angry riddled woman who knows what’s happened to her husband.

It’s truly a wonderful and impressive performance especially considering how little of this is played out through dialogue, it’s all written on her face. There’s a scene in an ice cream shop where the camera floats around different things she’s taking in, and it cuts back to her and you just feel exactly what she’s feeling. A single tear stranded in her eye, begging to be released but holding itself in place as she must remain strong for her children. It’s truly a wonderful performance deserving of the acclaim.

Perhaps the only thing really holding this film back is its reliance on keeping this true to life. It becomes apparent that this is a real story quite early on, and in keeping the story true to life it ends up dragging towards the end. This has more endings than the Lord of the rings. It’s strange there’s some really lovely endings it could have and yet it goes further and where it ends up is less powerful. Yet as a whole still a gorgeous story that is harrowing and all the more relevant with how our world is going

Name: A Matter of Loaf and Death
Review: Wallace is a man of the people - kinda smart but lacks any common sense

Name: Wallace & Gromit: Vengeance Most Fowl
Review: Sorry Wallace and Gromit who?!? Wallace he’s a nobody. This is Gromit’s film and Wallace is lucky to be lifted up by him

Name: Kneecap
Review: Just insane that none of these guys are actors. Really had a lot of fun with this, feels immediately so fresh, really nice blend of telling their own story and telling a wider story about Ireland

Name: Shithouse
Review: Finding your way is hard. This movie stings in the best way

Name: Theater Camp
Review: Sometimes films are just made for you, how is the musical in this better than Emilia Pérez

Name: Twiggy
Review:
Really interesting human who I had no idea about - turns out I recognise her from M&S ads and a David Bowie cover - but as a film, this is the equivalent of a Wikipedia page. It’s tonally all over the place, as is the filmmaking.

We go from plot point to plot point with no real substance, and the film just ends with the most generic statement about her, which makes the rest of the film feel even more substance less. I have no idea why this was released in a cinema, and this is coming from somebody who thinks most movies should be seen on the biggest screen.

It looked kinda bad for the most part, when it wasn’t archival footage, like the talking head parts were abysmally lit, on flat coloured backgrounds and had the most forced staged look to them - simply adding a plant doesn’t make it interesting.

Sadly very little to like as a film but interesting nonetheless

Name: One of Them Days
Review:
I was sold on this on the chemistry these two seemed to have off screen, and what do you know, it's the best part of this film.

A really explicit R-rated slapstick comedy with likeable leads is rare to come by but this delivered on that premise. It's fun to get a film like this getting a wide release. The leads are excellent. I feel like I don't even need to express how good Keke Palmer is, since she's so captivating and gravitating in every thing she's in, but this film semmed to match her comedic chops in the best way. I was shocked at how good SZA was as well, she manages to stand toe to toe with Keke in both comedy and more quiet moments which is impressive.

It's nothing incredible, the story is very thin and is just a facilitator for the comedy we get but it's fun, it's very easy to watch and it's got great vibes. Some sloppy filmmaking but I spent most of this with a smile on my face

Name: Mickey 17
Review:
Perhaps one of the most hotly anticipated movies of the year, Mickey 17, Bong Joon Ho's follow-up to the acclaimed masterpiece Parasite, is... weird. Its undoubtably fits in with the rest of his filmography, the thematic elements running through the film are a continuation of themes he's been exploring his entire career and it's almost genre less as it bounces from social commentary to slapstick comedy. Yet, something holds this film back and I sadly left feeling quite underwhelmed. 

What did work was the comedy. This thing is balls to the wall hilarious. It has elements of Chaplin era comedy, along with modern sensibilities and some really brilliant characters who are clearly satirising our world. The comedy is brought to life by the magnificent performances from the entire cast. Robert Pattinson in particular is outstanding as Mickey Barnes, in every single version of him. 

The way his performance is able to morph completely depending on the version of Mickey he is playing is so impressive. It's a masterclass in vocal and physical comedy. It's the reason this film works as well as it does. 

There's a number of other good performances, really enjoyed Naomi Ackie's performance and her opposition to Mickey 17 as a person, the way they contrasted themselves was great and how that changed with Mickey 18 was super intriguing. 

Mark Ruffalo is doing his best Trump x Musk hybrid and it oddly feels tame - a sign that the times we're living in are comedically absurd that a performance this outlandish feels tame. His wife, played by the ever-wonderful Toni Collette, was equally as comedic. 

However, aside from the hilarious comedy, I felt very little towards this. Thematically, sadly, it's painfully obtuse. If you walk out of this not knowing the commentary that is being made about Western society, capitalism and class I seriously worry. It was so heavy handed in its exploration of these themes and didn't feel like it was cutting below the surface. Given that this is coming from a director who does normally have such sharp social commentary I couldn't help but feel a little disappointed. 

This is not to say that the film had to go deeper and end up with something profound that none of us had been thinking of, but I just feel like it thematically runs quite thin towards the end. It's a shame cause I think a level of interest in this film requires you to be interested in what it has to say about our world, but I mean we're all living through these insane times and if you have engaged with any sort of critical thinking about the way our society is right now, I sadly think there's very little here that you will get from it. 

The film feels bitty and not cohesive, like the ingredients are there but it just doesn’t all come together to make a satiating meal. There's some interesting ideas which do crop up but they're never really pushed in any interesting direction, if often feels quite samey like we end on the same point like there's nothing more to say. 

The premise immediately lends itself to quite relevant social commentary, I mean Mickey is referred to as an "expendable" I wonder what that's commentary for. I just needed a little more depth when it came to exploring ideas. 

Sadly, it's also really poorly paced. The initial opening is so exposition heavy, I enjoyed it's editing but it is sadly messy and the rest of the film never really finds it's pacing. There's also a bunch of questions which crop up that are just never answered or explored which was annoying when it feels like it was pulling on the same idea and yet there were ideas in here which were interesting. 

This certainly feels like a film made by Bong Joon Ho. The themes are very similar to his previous work, he's clearly interested in exploring these ideas. There is a level of social commentary, and real characters who exist at the same time as characters. Genre less filmmaking which is always thrilling and some gorgeous visuals, but it's just missing something... some sauce some might say. Myabe the best Wilhelm Scream

Name: Opus
Review:
My favourite genre of film! EAT THE RICH! CULTS! SOCIETY! CLASS! AMIRIGHT! Kidding. I'm getting a little tired of these very similar stories which play out almost exactly as every other story, it's a tale we have all become accustomed to, and unless the film is doing something more, these often fall flat for me. Sadly this was another example of this.

I will say I really enjoyed Ayo Edibiri in this, which really isn't surprising, she's so entracing in everything she's in. Bringing so much humanity to her characters and this was no different. I also just loved how she was characterised as often I end up feeling frustrated by the protagonists in these satirical horrors, as they end up being stupid in service of the plot. This flipped that on its head and made her extremely headstrong and aware of the bullshit going on.

The start of the film was promising, but it just loses itself in the middle with some really poor pacing, and really on the nose metaphorical symbols for wealth, class and celebrity. The meandering sense of the film felt void of text and the sole purpose was to bring subtext to the film which wasn't interesting. The ending plays out how you'd expect, but also with the added layer of the ending which I really don't think is that smart and undercuts some of the ideas it was exploring.

Really wanted more from this but keep Ayo getting roles, loved her on the big screen

Name: Her
Review:
Wow. Many feelings and thoughts:

- Kinda been scared shittless recently with AI and the advancements (and I mean that purely in the technological sense, if we're meaning society-wise I think it's quite a setback) it's making to society. Honestly as well it's shocking I haven't seen this considering the main career pillars of my life are CompSci and Film but I think I timed this so well.

- Genius of this script is I think depending on where you feel you are in your own relationships, this could either be seen as a frightning dystopian reality or a soothing balm for the beauty of human realtions. I weirdly found this quite comforting.

- The more I sit with the final moments of this film, the more it really works on me. I think society has placed this value on romantic relationships and brainwashed us all that this is the pinnacle of success when it comes to love. Love is so many things though and boiling it down to romantic love and making this so valued I think has had repricussions on human relations that will forever be damaged sadly. Maybe a dark as fuck thought but the shots of him wandering among a place of endless possibilities and people, and yet he was so dialed into an earpiece, really had me thinking about all the small human relations we miss out on because of both our obsession with screens but also romantic love.

- The flashbacks in this film are maybe the best I've seen flashbacks used.

- The rise of LLMs is genuinely so scary when it comes to connection and every time we saw his job it made me feel so icky, as it feels like in this world AI writing is fully possible so much so that they'd seen a rise in AI, then they replaced that with humans writing other humans work as other humans had become complacent with AI doing stuff like that for them. Genuinely made me so scared.

Gorgeous movie, I mean visually and spiritually. I think there's something so beautiful about a film which is simultaneously a wake-up sign and a warning call but also a lamentation on love and the power it has.

Name: Black Bag
Review:
Has being a spy ever been this sexy. My god. A word which encapsulates this movie is “sexy”. From the gorgeously slick costumes, the lighting - which from the trailers I had major reservations about but it really worked in line with the vibes that were being set - and the just sexy af cast. Cate Blanchett walking down the streets of London in her brown leather jacket just was delightful.

This is a tight ninety, the way tight nineties should be made. It all felt purposeful but never rushed. I really adored the way this moved from scene to scene. We get these prolonged, extended dialogue heavy scenes, where the viewer just feels like a fly on the wall and I adored it. It’s putting the onus on the viewer to make decisions about how they feel.

It’s oozing with personality as well, not afraid to have humour injected in scenes which are serious, and the dialogue felt stylised but never so far removed from the real world that it comes off inauthentic. Really loved this, perhaps a little convoluted in how it wrapped up, but also simultaneously loved the genius of the final scene

Name: Novocaine
Review:
I popped more for the Chappell Roan needle drop, which is CRAZY.

It’s fun. Idk wasn’t in love with this to be honest. Jack Quaid is a totally compelling lead, charming, and definitely the right person for this role. The fight scenes are SUPER creative, and they fully use the shtick to its fullest extent but the story is so poorly conceived. The dialogue is real shaky and it leans into every single trope you would imagine. It’s got some fun jokes but it’s cringy a lot of the time and the performances don’t exactly move past this. It’s just another version of this type of movie nothing really all that different.

Name: The Electric State
Review:
Yikes. What a complete load of garbage, only made worse when you learn this is one of the most expensive films ever made. It was made for a clickable piece of content on a streaming service and for people to half ass pay attention while bad jokes are being said amongst a boring plot.

Performances across the board are horrible, the story feels overly written like we end up on plot points because we have to, lacks any sort of emotional attachment and the characters are so uninteresting. The CGI is cool and that's the single thing saving this film. It's crazy how much of a fall off the Russo's are on, it's insane to me that people who are foundational to the success of blockbusters have made such boring and bad movies since

Name: Win or Lose
Review:
This show feels like the magic of Pixar movies as a show. It's filled with heart and charisma and stories which toe the line between being suitable for kids and adults. The alternating perspectives between people of different ages and showing the same event from different sides was really interesting and I loved the foray into surrealism. It really enhances the emotional aspects of this story.

There are some issues mainly surrounding the obvious trans story which was cut, it's sad because it dampens the end of the series, but overall a stunning show with gorgeous animation you've come to expect from the studio and stories which are hard and important packaged in a fun way

Name: Theater Camp
Review: Really fun short, kinda nice to see it unpolished

Name: Snow White
Review:
So I happened to only be available to watch this in an IMAX screen and not only that but I was the only person in that IMAX screen, it took all of 10 minutes for me to start regretting my life choices.

Yeah, this isn’t good. It’s yet another piece of slog made by Disney in the hopes to prey on the nostalgia of people’s love for the classic animated film. It just fails to really expand on the original in a way which makes sense, and there in is the issue, is it feels at odds with itself.

The writing is clearly wanting to expand Snow White as a character, which isn’t hard given she’s really not much of a character. In doing so, the sense of autonomy and independence they give her contradicts with the ways she acts with the bandit, it feels very unlikely she would fall immediately for him.

The bandit as well has a song which sings about her privilege, which is an interesting idea and a pretty good song, but it makes no sense. She’s been kept prisoner for years and made a slave, so I don’t think the song really ends up hitting.

There’s plenty of contradictions like this which come from expanding the story but also feeling tied to honouring the old story. It ends up just being incredibly messy.

It also just feels cheap and hollow, the costumes are jarring and feel like they could’ve been Halloween costumes, the cgi is really quite horrible and uncanny when it comes to the dwarfs and the acting… Gal Gadot is truly giving a horrible performance here and it’s not even horrible in a fun way.

The only saving grace of this movie is Rachel Zegler, she brings to life this messy character so well you do feel some level of endearment towards her. She has an insane ability to act opposite CGI and make it feel so real and her voice is unreal. It’s sadly the only part of this movie which makes it worth seeing

Name: Flow
Review:
I mean this movie is beyond simple but I adored it. It washes over you like an ocean, letting you feel so much without telling you how to feel. I got a lot out of this actually. I read this as a lamentation on the strength of community and sometimes community is found in the people you don’t expect.

Trying to shape yourself to fit a certain mould is never right, especially when the groups you change yourself for don’t really love you. Love is found in the rejects, the people who really love all of you and what joy it is when you find those people. It’s also just about a cat who keeps falling into water

Name: La Cocina
Review:
Ok this didn’t fully work for me, but I really respect what it was trying to do. It’s chaotic, messy and whiplashes from idea to idea, very much paralleling modern day America.

There’s a spectacular one shot sequence which is truly the highlight of this film, and I think it really works when we do dial in on the staff.

It’s just so unfocused as a film, meandering dialogue about dreams of a green light - which is very clear when this is set up it’s how the film is going to end - the initial character we meet then not really showing up for most of the film, and the whole money stealing situation. I’m not sure I liked a lot of these parts but it just didn’t come together.

Looks absolutely gorgeous and is really a scathing look into how immigrants are treated, and just working class under capitalism. I sadly didn’t fully get immersed as I think it wanted me to.

Name: Brighter Days Ahead
Review:
Please Ariana Grande fans don’t go after me for this. I really thought this was poor. I was really looking forward to this considering I watched the We Can’t Be Friends music video and thought it was phenomenal. I just didn’t find this fully cohesive and it looked really off.

The story was quite thin but having read a little on it, massive fans off Grande will probably gets lots out of this, but as a narrative I didn’t get find all that much. Songs slap though and the deluxe album is really incredible

Name: Miss Congeniality
Review: Ngl this lost me for the entire first half. It felt very of the time, feminism which is really antifeminism, thinly vailed homophobia and classic hot girl isn’t hot until she’s been done up. However, idk the second half really worked, Sandra Bullock sells the shit out of this and the writing became stronger as it focused on the situation being funny instead of just relying on the boring male/female stereotypes and dated ideas

Name: Death of a Unicorn
Review: NO OMG. PLEASE let’s stop with these “eat the rich” movies which feel like they’re smarter than they are. This had a lot of potential and decided to explore none of what made this different. Oh at least we had some bad comedy

Name: A Minecraft Movie
Review:
Ok before I review this, this is perhaps the most insane screening I’ve ever been in. Which is saying something as I’ve been to multiple premieres at film festivals, and have been to opening nights for a lot of blockbusters, but this… nothing prepared me for this.

Nearly every seat was full, in a huge screen. Half of which were teenage boys. If not for these teenagers I don’t think I would’ve enjoyed this nearly half as much. They were screaming and cheering every five minutes like Cap had just grabbed Thor’s hammer. At one point in the film half of the screen erupted out of their seats cheering. I’ve genuinely not experienced anything like this.

In part it made this film bearable, as it’s truly horrible, but also it did encourage this disruptive behaviour which I’m not a fan of. I’m all for screaming and cheering, especially at a movie clearly as bad as this, and quite frankly the added noises helped in the bad scenes, but this evolving into throwing sweets at the screen which ended up just hitting people let’s maybe not.

Outwith this experience, I’m genuinely shocked this movie exists. I’m not even sure you could call this a movie. It’s more like a thin narrative which has been crafted by so many hands that it doesn’t feel real. The plot makes no sense in a lot of places, things just happen to get characters from plot point to plot point. This really isn’t a movie to give too much thought and it will all fall apart the moment you do.

It’s sad since we have had movies based on video games which have worked, maybe it’s because narratives are baked into those video games whereas Minecraft doesn’t really have a story. But then again we’ve had IP movies which are based of just the existence of an IP like Barbie, which have found really creative ways into the story. This film just isn't interested in doing more than it has to, in order to make people cheer and leave with no lasting impact. Why? Well that’s cause this will make money.

I will say this film is carried by the sheer dedication of the actors, well some of the actors. Jack Black is doing the most insane performance here and it works with just how mad this world is. Jason Momoa is also really fun, and works with what he’s given. The rest of the characters are rough and the actors have a hard time improving on what they were given. In particular the female characters really suffer here, the movie finds a way to get rid of them, which doesn’t make any sense, and then proceeds to give them nothing.

I really don’t have much good to say about this, it’s just a bad blockbuster. Some interesting CGI, among some really bad CGI, if anything this was just fun to watch to laugh at how insane it was. Jack Black and Jason Momoa sixty nine in this… I guess movies are gonna be ok

Name: Y2K
Review: Sorry, this really didn’t work for me. The film feels so jilted and really lacks a flow. It looks like a student film most of the time, and the comedy really just didn’t work. It sacrifices story for nostalgia, I’m assuming, most of the references I didn’t get. I’m sure this will have its audience but yikes. Also just horrible acting throughout, Jaeden Martell was fun, but most of the acting around him just felt like they were saying lines on a page

Name: Drop
Review:
Oddly fun. It’s a taut, exciting thriller which actually has the balls to remain filled with tension and doesn’t feel the need to shove jokes down our throats. When jokes do appear they are a welcome relief. 

It’s just a really solid flick. It’s not doing anything revolutionary, the plot follows beats you’d expect, the reveal is obvious and there’s so cheesy moments but I had fun. I was tense. That’s the point of a thriller I guess.

Wouldn’t be nearly half as compelling were it not for Meghann Fahy who is absolutely stellar in this. The film hinges on her performance and she does a wonderful job making us feel the sheer stress a woman in her position would feel

Name: The Amateur
Review:
Ugh this was aggressively mid. Kind of the type of film that annoys me. It’s so mind numbingly boring at points I was actively thinking about how much time was left.

It takes the movie so long to get past set up and once it does it really doesn’t veer off the path you think it will. Nothing’s horrible but it’s just not thrilling. The idea that this man is constantly under threat is just completely lost on the film.

It’s sad as the premise is kind of interesting, having a man in the field who uses his brain’s instead of brawn but it wears thin very quickly and gets lost under a lot of “hacking” scenes which don’t make sense. Which I’m all for if used correctly.

There’s also just some plot things which make no sense. For example, Bernthal’s inclusion in this film is pointless. This just steadily moves along until it reaches its lacklustre conclusion.

Name: Charlie's Angels
Review: Ok I desperately wanted to love this just for the sheer insanity of it, but oddly it’s scared to commit to just how batshit insane this is. It’s caught between wanting to be serious and also being camp and I desperately wanted this to just be full camp. It needed so many more insane fight scenes

Name: Charlie's Angels: Full Throttle
Review: Sorry, this is better than the original for the sheer number of wigs used

Name: The Penguin Lessons
Review: Perfectly fine film that feels so palatable it's hard for people to not enjoy. Lacks any sort of real meat but the real lesson is just how adorable penguins are when they walk

Name: Sinners
Review:
I've been a fan of Coogler's work within the MCU, it's felt like he's always had a uniquely refreshing take to blockbusters, like there's been a vision to his work despite being a part of the larger Marvel machine. It should come as no surprise then that when tasked to bring his first original story to the big screen, he does so with the care, depth and vision which has graced his previous work.

Sinners is monumental, and truly unlike any other blockbuster of note in a very long time. It's not constrained by genre and even within the context of the movie seems to argue the case that genre is restrictive. As Lynda Martell aptly put "Genre's are a funny little concept, aren't they?". This film has the audacity to shatter the lines of genre and fuse so many together to make a compelling, rich story, which seeks to also muse on how past, present and future are all connected.

It's so unbelievable unique from the jump, that it's hard not to get pulled into this world. In the first few minutes, a deeply unsettling feeling casts over the film, and yet suddenly gears switch and we spend half of the film sinking our teeth into the world. Learning its characters through their interactions with each other. Building out and fleshing this world so we care, and it only makes the back half of the movie even more compelling.

It's quite genius actually, something other films perhaps feel to scared to do, to let an audience just exist in this world for a while before any real incident happens. Fleshing out this entire world, only for the back hald of the movie to be centred around a single location, adds this sense of claustrophobia and places you in the shoes of those trapped. It's ingenious.

How does one blend so many different genres together and make the story feel as unified as it does? In large part it's cause of the music. MY WORD. As a self proclaimed score whore, somebody who gets religiously obsessed with the scores in films, this is up their with the greats. Ludwig Göransson may just be the man who's work I've enjoyed the most on the world, everything that man touches is straight up masterpieces.

The way that music is used in this film, and actually I would extend that to the phenomenal sound design, is inspiring. It's propulsive and unique and gives this film the unflitching and distinct vision that makes this so refreshing. It feels so integral to the film it's hard to imagine it without the accompaniments of this sound.

In particular, as nearly every review on here has mentioned, THAT scene is maybe some of the coolest and most inventive uses of music I've ever seen. It's also just one of the most captivating scenes of cinema in recent memory. It's stunning, it sounds unreal but it's saying so much to the wider themes of genre, memory and history that this film is dripping with.

It's also filled with a pleathora of rich performances, and a pair of gravitating and complex performances from Michael B. Jordan. He truly is a force here, and is equally as comanding and compelling as both of the twins. He oozes this charisma that perfectly matches this blend of sexiness and horror. There's also equally compelling performances from Miles Caton, Hailee Steinfeld, and especially Wunmi Mosaku who was a standout.

I would also like this to be a formal ask for every blockbuster to be shot with the care and love that this was. My word. It looks gorgeous in every scene. The focus on the actors, and the shallow depth of field and bokeh is so regal. The film grain, the use of IMAX, it just demanded your attention, and it was worthy of it.

So often now we're graced with these "arthouse horror" films, after we had a few masterpieces and nothing has really came close, studios are still chasing the success of these horror films with more to say. I loved how this is completely in its own lane, it's something new and that's why people are eating it up.

I wasn't in love with all of the filmmaking choices made here, but this made me feel electric throughout. It's film with a pulse and a beating heart that should be seen in cinemas

Name: Warfare
Review:
This film is vehemently anti-war, and I'm shocked to see others so opposed to the idea that it is. It is an unflinching, horror movie that does not seek to string together a narrative, or get inside the brains of these soldiers, or even explain what they are doing. It's showing war - and specifically an American war in Middle East - for what it was: wrong. 

The narrative pretence of simply wanting to show a continuous hour and a half of what these men went through, I think, is an inspired choice. It really emphasises the pointlessness of why these men were deployed in Iraq. For the first half of the film, they're occupying a home, sitting, surveilling, not knowing what they're looking for - this is a far cry from the glorification of war done by Call of Duty and the likes. 

You are just existing with these men who have occupied a home because a soldier "liked the look" of it. The blase attitudes of these men carrying out random tasks, is immediately contrasted with a full on assault to the ears. 

The film becomes this oppressive and unrelenting horror which showcases the ugly, horrific side to war that is often shied away from or dramatized to the point where it's easy to remove yourself from what's happening. 

Not here. This was visceral and uncomfortable to watch, and it had me tearing up in moments with how much it was affecting me. In large part thanks to the sound design which is harrowing. The wailing which permeates most of the second half of the film, the use of silence and muffled noise, it's placing you in the home with these men and showing you it's not a place you want to be. 

I truly liked their decision to abstract the characterisation of these soldiers and instead make it feel like you were watching real people - often young boyish men - experiencing the horrors first hand. D'Pharaoh Woon-A-Tai is phenomenal and commands the screen and Joseph Quinn truly has a hard task of making you feel physically uncomfortable but does so so well. 

Given that this film is from the memories of these soldiers and Ray Mendoza, I think this authentic and genuine approach to this film is really special as it's as close to a narrative depiction of what happened. I understand people who want to know more about the Iraqi people who suffered this war and so many other wars, that story must be heard, but it doesn't invalidate this film. 

The narrative may not have focused on the Iraqi family whose house was taken over, but Mendoza chose not to do this. This is his recount of the situation, and a perspective which does show the damage done by the Americans, it's just from their view. 

The last moments of this film I think encapsulate the pointlessness of this war. There's also a moment in this which I think is quite explicit in how the Americans viewed Iraqi lives, which is heartbreaking but sadly it's real. 

I really found this profound. The gritty oppressive filmmaking on show here is wonderful and demystifies the allure of war. If nothing else the contrast between the opening and closing scenes is so powerful and I really struggle to understand how people don't view this as a shock to the system and will leave you with a single word ringing in your ears

Name: Thunderbolts*
Review:
The MCU is back! Well at least that's what everybody's saying. I'm getting tired of this back and forth of the masses only being able to think in black and white. Marvel Studios is just that, a movie studio. Sometimes it's gonna be good, sometimes bad, sometimes incredible, sometimes a CGI mess, but I think this flip flop between "we're so back" and "Marvel is shit" is just exhausting.

However, I do think in the case of Thunderbolts* that sentiment is actually pretty apt. Marvel **is** back, by that I mean this feels like a grounded, character focused movie from the studio that easily could've been a part of the line up of movies they made back at the start. With the state of the MCU as it is right now as well, that just might be the highest compliment.

Who would've thought that cementing a story around a character - or a few - shooting scenes with a little bit of thought behind them, having a cohesive story centred around themes and a script which wasn't hampered by reshoots, would result in a solid fun time.

The strength of this film is undoubtably Florence Pugh and what she does with Yelena. Without her this entire movie falls apart, so choosing to centre the story on, not only a thoroughly interesting character who has given depth and complexity, but also an actress who seems to care about this film and gives a performance which you would expect from someone as acclaimed as her, was really smart.

This isn't to say that the team around her are uninteresting or superfluous, well minus Taskmaster, but more so that the film needed a grounding presence, and this being Yelena and tying her character directly to the themes of the movie was brilliant. 

I really applaud Marvel for trying something different here, opting to go for a film centred around the theme of mental illness, there's not many other Marvel movies I'm able to point to which actually have thematic depth. It doesn't always work, and I do think some of the conversations that are had surrounding these issues are clunky in places, but I would much rather enjoy watching a good attempt at complexity than some CGI slop.

Speaking of CGI slop, this is a far cry from the shitty cartoonish mess a lot of Marvel films have looked like as of recent. This looked stunning. It's nothing remarkable, but there was clear decision making put into how this film should look and for the most part it looks good, and it was shot with intention. Perhaps some of the best spatial awareness of cameras in a fight scene in a Marvel movie. The first fight scene in the bunker is genuinely brilliant.

I do like that this film was attempting to bring together multiple people from across the MCU and start to have some sort of connectivity, even if I don't think it fully works. 

Yelena and Red Guardian really work. Their chemistry together is brilliant. David Harbour has some great comedic moments, and I'm shocked his comedy works as well as it does here. They are also given some great emotional moments as well. Similarly to John Walker, I thought Wyatt Russell gave a really interesting performance here which had a lot of depth, and it was clear his evolution from TFATWS. 

Hannah John-Kamen and Sebastian Stan I enjoyed but I wish the movie was interested in their characters. The whole Bucky as a senator thing really isn't that interesting and feels like a way to get him into the movie. Weirdly, I really liked Ghost in this, she just isn't given enough. 

I like the idea that this team isn't really a team, they're just forced together, and I think their dynamics as a whole works, it just needed a little bit more. 

Instead of building out the team more though, we get Bob and I'm so glad we did. Lewis Pullman gives a wonderful performance as Bob, and I really enjoyed the idea that his character was a manifestation of mental illness. It makes for maybe the most interesting third act Marvel has made in a while.  

It didn't have to result in a big battle, but actually a quiet more emotional resolution fits the wider themes of the movie. It wraps up quite neat, but I do love the idea that all it takes is a single person to make you feel ok. 

Loved Julia Louis-Dreyfus and everything she had going on; I think she wonderfully toes the line of being funny and insane. If anything, I think they could've just done without the whole of New York being taken over. 

Solid movie, feels like they're on the right path again and who knows maybe Marvel is back this time. It's a crazy idea but maybe the key to a good movie is having characters feel natural

I had fun. This wasn’t what I was expected knowing it was Andrew Ahn and starring Bowen Yang, I was expecting more of a Fire Island, but this is less of a comedy and more of a straight up romance. I adore that queer films like this exist but something didn’t fully work for me here.

I think tonally it’s a little all over the place and this comes through in the performances, they don’t feel like they’re in the same movie. Youn Yuh-jung is the standout and makes her scenes really shine.

I love how Ahn shoots people with a delicacy and tenderness that lets people really live in scenes, he chooses to stay on people instead of cutting away immediately. 

It’s solid but I wish it had more of a clearer vision

Name: Civil War
Review:
So, I saw this movie in the states in April last year and since then I have been wanting to rewatch it because it has stayed on my mind. 

in August, I then got to see Alex Garland talk about the movie and he was speaking a little on the criticism he received about it being apolitical and he said that he thinks the politics the movie are very clear. 

He views himself as a centrist and then spoke a little bit more and kind of dismissed the ideas that the movie isn’t dealing with politics and since then I’ve just had this urge to re-watch it. I remember being such visceral filmmaking but I think I perhaps was a little bogged down in what this movie was being sold as and didn’t really get to appreciate it for what it was.

This time around I vibed with this a lot more, and it’s clearly more interesting in digging into the ethics and politics of photojournalism. The idea of “staying out” of the situation but having to place yourself in said situation. Is this making you an active participant? Is being a bystander still an endorsement of the situation?

I weirdly think the point of this movie is in how little “political sides” matter. Perhaps it’s politics which got this situation to the points it’s at, but once it ramps up to the situation we see, do opinions really matter, or just the shot?

“Why are you shooting at them? They’re shooting at us” is this scene not the exact same as “Will you shoot it when it happens?” Perhaps they wield cameras but they shoot them like weapons, firing until they get the right shot.

It’s still rough around the edges but I really appreciate this film and I do think it speaks to the wider ideas of exploitation and sensationalism that has seeped into war media

Name: Rogue One: A Star Wars Story
Review: OK, after watching Andor season two this is a perfect ending, and that last hour still slaps

Name: Hurry Up Tomorrow
Review:
Woof. This was rough. As somebody who watched The Idol I maybe should’ve saw this coming but I had faith. With this being attached to The Weeknd’s trilogy of albums - which I love - I was expecting the same level of artistry but that’s sadly not what this was.

Hurry Up Tomorrow lacks substance to warrant its runtime and it’s also not feverish enough, or diving into surrealism in interesting ways that makes this something I want to sit with.

It’s very obvious - well to me knowing The Weeknd, it’s clear it doesn’t come through in the movie as friends I went with had no idea - that this movie is exploring the idea of Abel killing off his persona and grappling with who he was and who he’s become.

The characters in the movie are so painfully just metaphors, and not particularly interesting metaphors. Jenna Ortega especially doesn’t feel like a real person and she’s also not that interesting symbolically so it leaves her feeling very much like an idea.

I do think there’s something here had the script been worked on a little more. There is certainly something interesting about a man as big as The Weeknd confronting who he’s became and really digging into his struggles, but it’s all so surface level.

From a filmmaking point of view, the film is gorgeous… well sometimes. The lighting blew me away. Nearly every scene is dripping in red and cyan and I thought it looked unbelievable. However, I’m not sure why the film opens with the camera just spinning uncontrollably, and then continuing to do so for a large portion of the film. “It symbolises how chaotic his life was” ok… I think there’s perhaps more interesting ways of showing this.

There’s an overbearing score and great sound design which makes you feel this overwhelming stress and the incorporations of The Weeknd’s music was nice. Sadly though this needed some better writing to leave any sort of impression

Name: Lilo & Stitch
Review:
Ok perhaps this is my fault for watching the original the same day but… man this sucked.

It’s not horrible, and clearly is better than a lot of the other Disney remakes - shockingly. But there’s so much of this that really rubbed me the wrong way, and I was constantly asking myself (as I do with most of these remakes) why was this made?

Judging this film on its own, and in comparison to the original, the first third of this movie is genuinely horrendous. We fly by the opening stories for both Stitch and Lilo at the most ridiculous pace. I’m convinced this was made for a “TikTok” viewer with no attention span. The editing is just so rapid I was getting so frustrated, and sadly this editing persists through the entire runtime.

The film is just missing the heart of the original. There’s moments in the original which really push on you emotionally and here they’re replaced with jokes often. There are some nice moments, it’s not to say this film is devoid of emotion, but it’s just not as satisfying in any way compared to the original. Sadly I think part of this may also come from performances.

It’s odd, for the most part this is a recreation of the original, with some key points changed, none of which were necessary or resulted in any interesting changes to the larger ideas explored.

I thought the CGI on Stitch and the other aliens looked good for the most part and Stitch’s models were used in really fun ways for some visual comedy and even some emotional scenes. I will just reiterate what I said though, I have no idea why this was made. I have no reason to watch this over the original

Name: The Phoenician Scheme
Review:
Hey, say what you want about modern Wes, but I genuinely think he’s found such a strong groove with his last few ventures and The Phoenician Scheme is no different. I think in his later films, the confidence and execution in conceiving his worlds, is so well realised that I feel so comforted in the runtime as I know what I’m in for.

In this film we’re introduced to a colourful cast of characters, much like his previous projects, however this time we centre most of the film on three characters. Benicio Del Toro and Mia Threapleton are wonderful, both bringing a glorious amount of emotion to characters who are very shut off. I really enjoyed their relationship and how sharply written they were, how the Wes style actually played into their characterisation and how their familial relationship 

The standout of this movie is however Michael Cera. I’ve long sang the praises of this man and he’s phenomenal here. He works so well in this world, in the style of Wes. It’s weird, he plays himself in a way that feels like a juxtaposition with the symmetrical structure of Wes’ films but it’s just so perfectly off. The switch up his character has is also wonderful.

It’s a Wes film, so there’s wonderful cinematography, score, side characters and production. It’s a lot funnier than his latest work, and features perhaps one of my favourite Wes scenes in any of his films with the basketball scene. I need more Bryan Cranston and Tom Hanks as a pair. Some of the funniest Wes scenes

Name: Mission: Impossible – The Final Reckoning
Review:
If this is the end of the Mission: Impossible franchise, it sadly is ending with a wimper instead of a roar. It's a real shame that this is plagued by the issues it has, as somewhere buried beneath is a film which would feel like a proper send-off to this franchise.

The first hour of this film is perhaps the worst hour in the entire franchise. It's exposition dumps, it's unnecessary flashbacks, it's a persistent need to tie everything together, and it's the mythologising of Ethan Hunt to become the most important man alive. "You are the chosen one" is a literal line from this movie.

Moreso, it's all of these issues coupled with the fact that the film feels the need to take itself so seriously, it loses what makes these films special. Even Dead Reckoning, which is hampered by issues, still keeps the tone consistent as has this level of whimsy which makes these films what they are, this is completely missing from this movie.

It would be fine if the tone did anything interesting for the film but all it does is make this self agrandising for Ethan, and Tom Cruise, and it's just boring. That first hour is simply that. Boring. Not a word I would ever use to describe this franchise even in its worst moments.

However, once you get past that first hour, the film finds it's footing. It doesn't completely mitigate the issues the film has. It's still wildly convoluted, features way too many characters and some bad writing, and the villains remain plain. But the sheer spectacle of the stunts and set pieces counteract this in a massive way.

This is what makes this film better than the majority of action slop that's came out recently. Once you're in these set pieces, time flies by. It's shot so confidently and with such control, they're always interesting and evolving and building on each other. It's just pure adrenaline and action-packed fun that deserves to be seen on the biggest screen possible.

It makes sense why you can still walk away from the film feeling like it was a good time as the majority of the film is filled with these set pieces. It's a shame there wasn't a more elegant way to write that first hour which really could be condensed down as this film would perhaps function a lot better.

I don't think the criticisms can fully be contained in that first hour however, I think there's a lot of character assassination in this, or perhaps a better way of doing this is that there's so many characters that the film is just uninterested in giving them much to do, which sucks because there's a plethora of interesting characters this franchise has helped build. Instead that's sacrificed for exposition.

There's a character in particular who I find it completely bizarre that we don't get their reaction to a major thing that happens at the start of the movie. Sadly also this falls into the trap that a lot of Mission Impossible movies have, where Hayley Atwell's character loses most of her autonomy, and what makes her interesting, in service of her being another woman for Ethan to fall in love with.

It's so pulled down by issues in its writing, however I still can't rate this super low because the action in this movie, the stunts, the set pieces, the dedication to the the thrill and spectacle of cinema, is so much better than anything else that we get in these large action blockbusters that still makes this a fun film. However as a finale to one of my favorite franchises this was a major disappointment and letdown

Ethan Hunt Hair Rating - 6/10 it's ok but longer

Name: Ballerina
Review:
It’s solid. I’m a massive fan of the John Wick movies, they somehow manage to transcend their often basic and thin plot with unbelievable action and a compelling lead. Ballerina has a paper thin plot even by John Wick standards, but there’s enough meat in here to make for a fun watch.

I think the film misses a trick by leaning into what makes Ana De Armas unique. The film boasts, at an albeit cheesy moment, for Ana to “fight like a girl” but this never really transpires. The action scenes, while wonderfully choreographed, never take full advantage of her character.

It’s very cool to see somebody less skilled than we’ve seen take on action scenes and they certainly play on this. I love how this film doesn’t just make Eve the “female John Wick”, it doesn’t diminish the lore of how notorious John Wick is, more so it plays into it. 

However, there’s a distinct lack of an understanding of who Eve is. She ends up resourceful - something not wholly unique - and overtly aggressive instead of the cool calm style of Wick but apart from this she feels quite generic. Weirdly, the film doesn’t want to influence her fighting style with what makes her unique, apart from the odd ball shot - since she’s a woman? 

It just feels like they set the scene perfectly with the whole dancing backstory to not utilise this at all in her fighting style is strange. 

The film takes a while to get going, it gets bogged down in a really overdone backstory - very generic and as I mentioned doesn’t really influence her character much - but once it gets past this, it basically is just high intensity great action scenes. Never reaches the heights of most of the John Wick set pieces, these feel more like action scenes than set pieces, however still an enjoyable watch and some of the best and most creative action scenes being made

Name: How to Train Your Dragon
Review:
I’m finding it really hard to rate this movie, because I’m drawn between rating the actual quality of the film that I just witnessed, and rating it based on the fact that it is a pointless, near perfect, recreation of an already very well-made animated movie. I’m settling somewhere in the middle.

As far as this movie goes, it is as you would expect, filled with the same heart and comforting storytelling that made the original so special. I think the story is wonderful but doesn’t really offer any depth or any alternative perspective than the original had. The big scenes and story beats hit but I wonder how much of that actually comes down to this film. Is it more so just seeing this world and hearing the music that’s making these scenes work.

This movie is proof again that animation is just a medium and not a genre because, while the movie looks impressive, I also think it falters to capture the magic of Burk that’s seen in the animated movie. I thought the sets, the production design, the costumes all felt a bit costumey like they were trying to re-create something that had already been made, instead of it actually feeling lived in and real.

The gorgeous onset locations and the vistas look wonderful however sometimes this did draw me out, because seeing the costumes amongst the real world, there was a dissonance there that didn’t quite work.

The performances are great across the board. I don’t think anybody is a stand out and yet nobody is jarring as well so I guess that’s good. I do think Hiccup’s performance in the animated movie is so iconic and so embedded in that character that whoever was going to play him was gonna have a hard time living up to that. And while I think Mason is a good hiccup, I can’t help it, compared to the animated, I think he’s missing that awkwardness and goofiness that that Hiccup has in moments. Some performances felt a little stilted as if they were having to perform lines that already been performed as well.

And that just brings me to a larger point, I don’t know why this was made. It’s doing nothing different than the original and I think I’m comfortable in saying after this and the other live action remakes we’ve had this year I’m just totally against them. If it’s doing something different, it sometimes misses out what makes the original so special. If it’s shot for a shot, then what is the point in remaking it because we already have a movie which is good enough? Perhaps the only circumstance where it makes sense is when it’s modernising a really old story but even then we’ve had instances of that and it doesn’t work. So if this is the best that we get, which is just a recreation of an already good movie, then what’s the point because I would rather watch the animated movie.

Name: God's Own Country
Review:
Really struck me watching this, the tenderness and intimacy on display, some of the most raw vulnerability I’ve seen between people in film in a long time. 

Loved how this film characterised Georgie, more often that not I think romance films fall on looks and physical appearance as what makes a love interest of significance. Instead focusing on his caring nature, his openness, his kindness and his vulnerability I thought was a breath of fresh air in this genre.

Further proof that queer romance movies are endlessly more layered and interesting

Name: Before Sunrise
Review:
"I always think that I'm still this 13-year old boy that doesn't really know how to be an adult, pretending to live my life, taking notes for when I'll really have to do it"

I felt this film in my bones. Honestly the best feeling on this planet is being seen. Being truly seen by another person. There is an indescribable feeling when you feel yourself being observed for who you really are and this film captures that feeling so beautifully.

I genuinely felt as if I was witnessing people fall in love and what a special privilege that feels like. The longing, the glances, the thoughts spiralling through their brains as each other speaks, so loud it’s visible, just real raw honest performances.

Real, simple writing as well. I loved how messy this is, it’s exactly like conversation. Neither of these people are the world’s most perfect person (I kinda liked how insufferable they were at points) but it’s what makes it real.

Reminded me of the beauty in the world. I noticed on their meandering travels, bumping into all sorts of characters, the kindness on display from each of the people they run into. Perhaps I’m looking in the wrong places, or the onslaught of social media and being actively aware of human’s destructive nature has warped my perception of humans, or perhaps the world has truly changed, but I found these random moments, these pure expressive moments to be as enrapturing and meaningful as the central relationship.

Similarly, is the art of conversation gone? I find it so rare that people willingly offer such openness and honesty when speaking now, and in the few relationships I have where it does feel this way, it feels special.

The ending of the places they’d been in the morning as well, says so much without saying anything at all. God what a gorgeous film.

Some lines which really hit:

“If there's any kind of magic in this world, it must be in the attempt of understanding someone, sharing something. I know, it's almost impossible to succeed, but…who cares, really? The answer must be in the attempt.”

“I think I can really fall in love when I know everything about someone-the way he's going to part his hair, which shirt he's going to wear that day, knowing the exact story he'd tell in a given situation. I'm sure that's when I know I'm really in love.”

“But loving someone and being loved means so much to me. We always make fun of it and stuff. But isn't everything we do in life a way to be loved a little more?”

Name: The Man with the Answers
Review:
New favourite genre of film - hot European men go on a road trip, talk and experience love.

In love with how this film feels. It’s stupidly simple, but in its simplicity it shines. Could’ve watched this for hours. 

The chemistry is beyond palpable between these two men, where literally a man placing his head on the other’s shoulder and nuzzling into him became the most romantic thing I’ve seen in a while. Kicking my feet watching this far too many times.

Kind of beautiful to just see queer love where it’s incredibly sweet, it’s not burdened by queer tragedy, it’s just pure love

Name: 28 Weeks Later
Review: Far too much shaky cam as well, somehow managed to be more unwatchable than the original in scenes

Name: Maurice
Review:
Monumental filmmaking honestly, I just truly believe that queer romance in cinema is far more layered, and nuanced, and interesting to watch than most straight films.

The lingering on words, the veiled double meanings of lines, the temptation, tension, and risk that fills every scene. God I just adored this. What a phenomenal screenplay as well

Name: 28 Years Later
Review:
What's always been impressive about this franchise, or rather, refreshing, was it's use of using a horrifying concept, and world filled with atrocities to explore humanity. Each of the films, in this now trilogy, has explored human nature, and really sought to dig deeper into the intricacies of how we as humans interact with each other.

While the previous films in the trilogy were more focused on "the real horror is man" narrative threads, with the odd hopeful theme as well, I was dumbfounded with the route this film took. It's firm stance on love, and radical kindness - both when it came to its human characters, but also refreshingly to the infected - which was a welcome surprise.

I truly walked away from this film touched, and that I wasn't expecting. I was also terrified, that was to be expected, but to be so moved by this return to this world was really such a satisfying feeling. 

The film takes this expansive world and narrows right in on just a few characters, and mainly just on the single character of Spike. What a phenomenal decision this was. To see the world through his eyes, and watch him have to grow up very quickly makes this film even more impactful.

The film plays out by following Spike go on a journey, and he is joined by other characters on his way. This gives room for us to explore the nuance and depth of each of his relations, and while it does make the film feel a little disjointed in areas, in also allows for a fascinating look at masculinty with his Dad's story and makes room for the emotional last half of the movie.

The film wouldn't work as well as it does were it not for the incredible performances, of all the actors, but particularly of Alfie Williams. He has to hold his ground and really lead this film and I truly found him so captivating. Incredibly emotive and is given the hard job of making this character slowly have to harden in front of us but I thought he was incredible.

If only the film chose to just tell the story of Spike, it would've worked a lot better. Given it's a staple of the franchise, the film opens with a horrifying scene, but it's really quite poor and what's worse is it's there simply to connect to the last few minutes of this movie, which is possible the worst part. It undoes so much of the emotion felt right before and is tonally so distant from what we'd seen. I was so confused if what I was watching was real. It's really quite disappointing as the film comes to such a nice ending moments before, but still this didn't sour everything which made this so wonderful

Name: Elio
Review:
I mean this in the most earnest, non-derogatory way, but child me would've loved this. This isn't to say that I didn't, but it's a film I would've obsessed over as a child.

In my mind, this is a return to form for Pixar. A gorgeous story of lonliness, found family and the desire to belong, that was filled with so much heart. Sure, perhaps the story beats are a little well-worn, but I really don't find that enough of a reason to not enjoy this when it does what it does so well. 

Elio is a wonderfully realised character. It's impressive how quickly you get a sense of who he is and his yearning to belong - honestly the way this is portrayed I think you'd be hard pushed to find somebody who didn't relate to Elio. I found him so enthralling to watch and felt so much more personable than a lot of animated leads as of late.

The film has really strong comedy chops, not sure I've laughed like this at an animated film recently, it's got some genius writing, and fun gimmicks but what really astounded me was the physical comedy on display. It's certaintly something which seen in many animated films but rarely is used so intelligently on humans. The humans in this film felt human. They felt tangible and real, and the physical comedy came more from subtle human moments than the often used outlandish physical comedy used in animation as characters lend themselves to this. It was just a thouroughly refreshing choice in how to use the animated medium.

Speaking of, I think this is yet another step up for Pixar. They are at a point now where it's hard for them to iterate on films which already look stunning, but I was really impressed with a number of elements of this. The water here was beyond incredible, and just how textured and real the world felt.

In classic Pixar fashion, it's also not only hilarious but deeply moving as well. I did cry. Shock. Playing on the themes of feeling alone, especially as a child, really struck me. Perhaps what was more surprising, is how moving I also found the adult characters. His aunt and her story of trying her best for Elio despite the situation she was put it was so touching. And this is to say nothing of Glordon's story which was also a really moving story of acceptance, it just all came together in such a lovely way.

Pribably bias as I was a kid who had stars plastered across his room, felt alone as a child and has a Physics degree so loves this shit, but I think this is perhaps up there with Pixar's better outputs

Name: Mountainhead
Review:
Sorry, Jesse Armstrong what happened? I was so looking forward to this given it was made by the man who has made the best TV Show of all time, but this was rough.

It's getting a little bit tiring these projects which essentially boil down to "look at how rich tech people are destroying the world". I think we all know this by now. I was craving just any sort of deeper exploration of this, but I genuinely think the deeper message was "see even the slightly good person is bad" like really?

What is this movie trying to say? It has the same quippy style of Succession but I think fails to understand why that show worked. It worked because the insane lines and deplorable stuff the characters did came from the characters. We inimately knew these people, we understood how they operated, even if we don't like them.

There's virtually no depth given to these characters, so nothing feels like it has any weight. It's a real shame cause I do think the performances here are good but what happens? This never reaches any real convergence point, it just meanders with nothing to say. It's overly long, too wordy and lacking in substance. Weirdly I don't even think the humour lands, I'm just dumbfounded that this was made by the same man who had such cutting class commentary and struggled to do so

Name: Superman
Review:
Lois Lane, esteemed reporter, gets the opportunity to interview Superman. In her first few questions she said “How big are you?” She really just let the horny thoughts take over

Respect for attempting something this ambitious at the time it was made but a lot of stuff didn’t work for me here. It’s really long in the tooth, the first act could completely be cut, and many scenes overstay their welcome. We miss out on pivotal character moments, there’s no real depth to the characters. We miss out on him exploring his powers, or having to live this double life.

Superman is quite literally that, he really has no internal struggles and there’s no real exploration of him as a character. Most of the other people around him are caricatures, the villains are so campy in an Adam West Batman way that doesn’t fit. 

I think Reeve is charming and plays the balance between Clark and Superman really well, and visually it’s really interesting, the use of miniatures was really cool. The real shining element of this film is the gorgeous score from John Williams, particularly the main theme just so perfectly feels like Superman

Name: F1
Review: Wow they really put the formula in Formula One. Incredibly generic screenplay that genuinely had me laughing in the cinema so much. The film doesn’t veer off the tried and true tracks at all playing it so simple. The dialogue is beyond cheesy but it kinda just works. What’s undeniable is how the racing scenes make you feel. The filmmaking on offer in those scenes is nothing short of special. Never has racing looked or felt so good. Just a shame the filmmaking of the track doesn’t quite live up to the action scenes. Plagued with some really awful editing and the generic screenplay you would think it would be enough to dampen this movie, but there’s enough action scenes, compelling performances and a rip roaring score from Hans Zimmer that makes this a formula fun
