But at the same time I don't think there's a level of depth afforded to these characters that makes me invested in this movie by the end of it.

In subverting the biopic, which I'm not a massive fan of normally, it lands on something very plain. The film is telling its story in quite broad strokes, it's dealing with the origins of UFC, the pressures of the masculine ideal, his toxic relationship with his girlfriend, his internal struggles with failure, and it doesn't quite scratch the surface on any of these.

If it weren't for the exemplary directing and this movie flowing so gorgeously, being shot so well, I really would struggle to get anything out of this. As the film is concluding I didn't feel like I'd reached any sort of resolution with the central character.

I really enjoyed the scenes of The Rock and Emily Blunt acting opposite each other, their volatile relationship was really interesting to watch, but I sadly don't think Emily Blunt's character is written all that well, she kind of becomes a plot point for the rock to fight with. Meanwhile, I like a lot of what is going on with The Rock internally, and he gives a spectacular performance, but there just wasn't enough there to make me really feel for his story

Name: Knives Out
Review: I need more evil Chris Evans

Name: Glass Onion
Review: I truly think Rian Johnson is so gifted at writing, being able to make you invested in the why just as much as the how is what fuels these silly, glorious movies. Sure, the mystery is obvious - especially if you spot that scene - but it’s the motivations, the intrigue and dynamics between the ensemble that keeps you wanting more

Name: Wake Up Dead Man
Review:
London Film Festival Film #3

“Young, dumb, and full of Christ”

Rian Johnson is back with yet another mystery (he never really went away for those who watch Poker Face). With yet another star-studded ensemble, and led at the helm by the wonderful Josh O’Connor - fulfilling his rightful place among the ever expanding list of actors who play hot priest - this was bound to be a recipe for delight.

And that is it. It warms my heart that we have another entry into this franchise which feels as refreshing and exciting as the others in the trilogy. The formula is tried and true; Set up the ensemble, give them all reasonable motives, introduce Benoit Blanc and let the chaos ensue. It may be played out, but man if that formula isn’t fun. 

It also feels dramatically different to the previous instalments. They’re all playing in the world of murder mystery, but with slightly different takes. Here, is perhaps the best mystery of the three. Something seemingly impossible is always a good strategy to get an audience invested. If the detective isn’t able to figure it out, then it places it on a viewer to try and get there before he does.

Not unlike the previous films, the mystery itself is kind of obvious…well sort of. I’ve always found with these films that the main culprit, is easy to find. It’s how they did it (which in this film is so wonderfully complex) and why that becomes the intrigue.

The film goes on enough twists and turns though that really challenged whether or not I was right in my assumption and that’s the fun part also. That sudden feeling of I was wrong, well who is it? I thrive off that feeling.

It’s hard not to talk about this film and not mention Josh O’Connor. He is absolutely spectacular here, and I feel overjoyed with the sheer amount I got of him. He’s such a fascinating character, portraying this priest with a rough past. Somebody who is trying to write his wrongs, but still his past haunts him. 

Out of all of the leads of these films, he’s by far the most intricate and well realised. This, I would posit, is because this film is the most intricate out of the trilogy. It’s interesting in exploring themes, on top of being a mystery, in using faith and religion as a way to explore our relationship to these things.

It may be the person in me who was raised Catholic but I got so much out of this. Struggling and grappling with faith is such a potent thing for a lot of religious people. And this really dug into the power of faith on those who believe in it, and finding faith in different things.

Even simpler than that the way that Johnson writes the churchgoers was pitched to perfection. The people who attend church often don’t practise what is preached, it’s an environment that is very cliquey. Now, place a radical religious priest in charge of that church, and the results create monsters. I just think he nailed this exploration of religion in the modern world.

There’s a scene in particular that absolutely devastated me and got me misty eyed. It’s initially a heavily comedic scene, a scene that perhaps goes on too long, and then it shifts, and lord what a payoff. It feels like this gorgeous moment of the power of faith, something which this movie spends a lot of its time criticising, so this scene juxtaposing this, made it all the more powerful.

I sort of disagree with this notion that this is the first of his movies to have some deeper meaning or explore themes. Both his previous ventures have flirted with this idea, particularly the wealth and billionaires of the previous film, but it’s just more so. It makes for such a rewarding experience.

Like all the other films, the cast of characters makes this film, we just need more Benoit Blanc and his immaculate fashion sense

Name: Moss & Freud
Review:
London Film Festival Film #4

Let me pitch you a film. Kate Moss, one of the most influential models of the century, somebody whose story, has often been painted for her. Lucian Fraud, a prolific painter, who was known for his portraits, and unveiling truth in them. Now place these two, on the surface, people who seem opposed to each other, and showcase the friendship which blossomed between them, over a long period as Fraud painted Moss. Sounds interesting enough right?

What drew them to each other? Why did they seem to understand each other in ways other people didn’t?
Surely there is something so palpable about their relationship that kept Moss coming back to him? Well, the film seems to tell you this, but it doesn’t show you this all that much.

I got very little substance out of this film. There’s clearly a chemistry between the two actors playing these two famous people, but in these long drawn out dialogue scenes where we’re supposed to see this relationship flourish and develop, really unpack this idea of seeing somebody’s truth, there’s just nothing interesting going on.

The film plots along with rarely a thing to say until it comes to its conclusion at the end and even in this moment the film is struggling to have some sort of cathartic payoff because there has been no real exploration of these people. It bookends with profound statements about art, and truth and being creative, none of which felt earned in the slightest.

Ellie Bamber does a great job as Kate Moss. I’m not too familiar with Moss but she captures the spirit of what would make somebody so endearing to the public eye. Derek Jacobi as Freud I think a complete misfire. His performance is all over the place. It was bumbling in areas, and then it was really stern in others. I don’t think there was a true through line with his character which justified such wild juxtapositions.

Yeah, this was a real disappointment sadly however child me popped in the cinema when I saw will tutor on my screen again

Name: Father Mother Sister Brother
Review:
London Film Festival Film #5

As I left the screening of Father Mother Sister Brother I heard so much murmuring of people completely underwhelmed with this film. Not satisfied, a lot of people saying they were bored, they felt sleepy and that honestly saddened me so much because I think there is a magic with Jarmusch films and there is magic in this film that was lost on a lot of people.

This film is an anthology, which I know doesn’t work with a lot of people. It’s fragmented storytelling and gives you less time to invest in characters, but I’ve always had a bit of a soft spot for anthology filmmaking, because it leans on being more thematically inclined, and finding a through line through different stories. I think that’s the great joy of very deeply personal cinema, is finding connection to it and finding a through line with your life experiences and the film. In my opinion anthology filmmaking, when done well, just gives more room for this.

I thought this one was magnificent. Sure it’s slow and takes a little while to get into the rhythm that it’s going at, but it’s very in line with other Jarmusch work. It’s so steeped in realism, and as it plays out, both the father and mother chapters deal with a stilted relationship between parents and their children. It’s reflective of how the parental choices made throughout their lives have made them into the people they are today, and have calcified the relationship between parent and child.

In both scenarios we’re treated to really odd families relationships, they feel forced or performative, like family is the only reason they’re there. We’re dealing with absent parents in different ways and that being connective tissue between them.

In the sister brother story, on the surface it feels different, like it’s juxtaposing the previous instalments, we see a familial relationship which is tighter, a relationship which feels worn in and natural. But it’s still dealing with this idea of absent parents. 

These children who are only now discovering about their parents, because they were kept at a distance from them. I really truly thought this was just a wonderful exploration of family dynamics, something that felt so based in realism.

It has a fair bit of comedy in it but I think the entire film is tinged with this deep sadness, that we will never really know anybody, that sometimes familial relationships are out of obligation and they’re not real. It’s all very glossy. 

There’s definitely some issues, there’s through lines, in particular comedy, that don’t really work but equally there’s parallels between the three stories that I think just hammer home the artificial nature of some familial relationships that I thought that was wonderful. It’s a little rough around the edges but gosh if you engage with this you could get a lot of it. I mean it also has Cate Blanchett in yet another weird ass haircut so yet another reason to go see this

Name: Ballad of a Small Player
Review:
London Film Festival Film #6

Welcome back to the world of Edward Berger. A director who now has proven himself with his latest three ventures to be highly competent when it comes to visual stylings. His collaboration with James Friend has resulted in multiple movies now where if you paused the film at any moment you’re bound to land on a gorgeous frame.

This worked somewhat in All Quiet, to the strengths of Conclave, but here… it just feels so empty. This isn’t to discredit how the film looks, it’s truly stunning. The colours pop, it’s got some neat framing and each location feels like it’s utilised to its fullest… but it’s just lacking reason.

This criticism is able to be made about most aspects of the film. Yet again Berger employs another bombastic, repetitive score. Something which worked in Conclave, and just feels so out of place here. There feels like an over-reliance on individual components of the film to get you invested, using these to build tension, but none of which work to make a compelling whole. 

I will say, admittedly Colin Farrell is spectacular, as if it needs to be said. He turns in yet another fantastic performance that's layered and intriguing. It’s just a shame that the writing around him doesn’t provide him very much to do here.

The film feels like a textbook definition of style over substance, where the priority is constantly placed on how the film looks and not how it feels. There’s nothing deeper under the surface here,nothing to sink your teeth into. Even the more dreamlike aspects of the film border on safe and familiar.

Name: Miroirs No. 3
Review:
London Film Festival Film #7

Small and intimate look into the different ways people cope with grief, and using other people to unpack - or to suppress - those emotions. Set amidst a gorgeous backdrop of nature, which in itself is used to explore how healing and isolating solitude is, and helmed by some great central performances this makes for such a beautiful watch.

It’s a very simple film, not everything works, I do think the performances in some places feel a little wooden, which is certainly the point but it didn’t fully work. It gives the air of feeling a bit unnatural which is sad, and also wraps up a little too neatly. Yet it’s still a very pleasant watch and has such a mastery over silence it’s insane

Name: The Voice of Hind Rajab
Review:
London Film Festival Film #8

I'm not sure I'm able to describe the feeling of watching this film. There was a sheer palpable stillness in the room, something that lingered long after the screen faded. As the ushering of the full screen made there way out, there was an all encompassing lack of words spoken, simply the wiping away of tears, sniffles and the contemplation of what we witnessed.

I spent half of the film choked up, wiping tears away. This is a devastating watch, made all the more heartbreaking by the use of real voices. This type of storytelling, using the blurred line between fiction and reality, was so effective here. I’m dumbfounded at the reactions this was able to maintain for the entire film. It so expertly expresses how difficult this situation is, the layers of bureaucracy that have to be upheld to ensure safety, but these same restrictions being fuel to the frustration of the situation.

It’s superbly acting and directed with a propulsion and energy which gets across how horrible this situation is. Make every world leader watch this. Free Palestine

Name: Resurrection
Review:
London Film Festival Film #9

Was absolutely blown away by this. The word epic is thrown around quite frequently when it comes to films but it’s a word which is entirely appropriate for Resurrection.

A film which seeks not to be understood, but to be felt. To imagine worlds, to live in characters, to dream. For those who dream are the people who truly live.

Going on a journey which exemplifies the beauty and power of cinema through the years is an absolute delight. Each period personified through that eras cinematic stylings. It nails all of these. It’s directed with such variety and precision, never did these stories feel like they weren’t a part of a cohesive whole.

In particular I was blown away by the production design across the board, but the silent movie scene is stunning, and the cinematography, once again in every part, but especially the oner at the end is just incredible. It’s truly up there with the best visuals I’ve ever seen, and deserves to be seen in a cinema.

Its richness and intricacies perhaps lose you in some places, but I would lying if I said I wasn’t moved by this insane piece of cinema. Some parts are burned into my mind

Name: Bugonia
Review:
London Film Festival Film #10

Lanthimos is being freaky again… well kind of. This is Lanthimos at his most restrained. He spends a lot of this film crafting its world and its characters, really unpacking what they mean to each other and who they are contextually as part of the wider world. 

This pays off massively when he gets to go full Lanthimos. In his restraint, he manages to land the impact of his more gruesome and strange tendencies which have more of a vigorous gut punch than in his previous efforts. 

This film has all of the staples Lanthimos has become known for, its gorgeously realised - the colour grading is just spectacular - it’s bubbling with commentary on our modern world, and he uses outlandishly exaggerated characters - which somehow feel so deeply true to our world - to really unpack and explore wealth disparity, and class struggle.

The worker bees, who are essential to life, are exploited and used, easily manipulated - but what happens when these bees realise their exploitation. Realise that their life is in subservience of a queen bee who cares not for them.

What I think Lanthimos nails here is the sympathy he affords these conspiracy theorists. He presents them as smart and competent people, people who see the problem but due to the machinations of the internet, have came to the wrong solution.

It’s a deeply empathetic film, it wants you to understand the mind of this man, the humanity that has pushed him to do what he’s done. It’s genuinely such a fascinating way to explore class dynamics essentially making every character repulsive.

The performances are so bloody brilliant in this. Plemons gives his best performance yet and Stone is simply magnificent as she always is. The film knows this as a lot of the time it’s shot tight on their faces as they speak to each other and it’s so wonderful. 

Upon reflection the ending works for me, for my reading of the film. It was initially incredible jarring, and I thought undid some of the great commentary prior, but I actually think it lands on something more nuanced

Name: Jay Kelly
Review: London Film Festival Film #11 Oh Baumbach. My sweet Baumbach, what happened? I’m normally a sucker for these Art is everything kinda films, I love the way Baumbach writes people and I love Sandler and Dern. This should’ve been something I adored. It’s so incredibly self important, and some people seem to be vibing with that but I just didn’t. This hinges so much on Clooney’s performance, and I got nothing out of it. It’s not to say he’s bad, he actually shines in some scenes, but the overall performance felt very safe and lacking in any sort of energy. Rarely did we get any sort of real insight into this man. In love with the idea that movies are memories for this man and revisiting his life through movies. Cool idea! It was in these scenes the movie felt like it was finding itself and then we’d cut back to Clooney and I was pulled out. I do wonder if people’s connection to this film hinges on their connection to Clooney, of which I don’t have, as he’s essentially playing himself. There’s a real lack of consistency in the film though, I just grew frustrated by having real scenes of Baumbach on fire, and then scenes which lacked any sort of energy. Sandler and Dern are fine, I actually was finding Sandler more of interesting character throughout the film until it just goes in a boring direction. I will say this movie has a phenomenal ending scene, which I imagine, had I been enjoying the movie up until this point, it would’ve hit hard, but this sadly lost me along the way

Name: Star Wars: The Last Jedi
Review: Screening with Rian Johnson I wish I had a more coherent review, given I got to see this with Rian Johnson in attendance, but this came at the end of what was a four-day film at the London Film Festival, so my brain basically boiled down to this:  Oscar Isaac is the most attractive human being to ever grace the planet, and every time he appeared on the screen, my smile grew bigger. The amount of times my brain went where is Poe Dameron was unhealthy.

Name: The Testament of Ann Lee
Review:
London Film Festival Film #12

Wow. Gorgeous film. Vibrating with passion and rhythm that will make you want to get out of your chair and shake. I’m not sure if this is a musical but rather using music as a way of expressing and telling a story.

It’s so effectively utilised throughout that it almost becomes indistinguishable when you flow in and out of songs; the whole film becomes this sort of dance. There’s a particular sequence on a boat that got me so hooked.

Kind of crazy how good this looks; it’s so well realised in its folk aesthetic, the colouring, the sets, the looks, everything really. The only thing holding this back is how it explores Anne; the film ends up feeling more like a recap of her life than truly digging into who she was.

I wanted to know why she inspired so many people, what internally compelled her to do what she does; sadly, the film doesn’t seem interested in this. However, as yet another Corbet and Fastvold joint, this thing is yet another epic to see.

Name: It Was Just an Accident
Review:
London Film Festival Film #13

Panahi manages to craft such a delicate and charged portrait of the lasting effects of the Iranian regime’s horrific treatment of people. It’s a masterful display of repressed emotions bubbling up to the surface.

It’s tonally so precise, often humorous at times, which makes the moments of anger leave even more of a devastating impact. 

It’s dealing with morality in such a fascinating way, exploring whether people are truly capable of change, the monsters oppressors seek to make out of their prisoners and the fight to remain righteous. This made all the more striking by the final scene

Name: Sound of Falling
Review:
London Film Festival Film #14

Admittedly, this took me far too long to get into. The jumping around in time periods, which I later found mesmerising, was really hard to get into. 

This was a distant portrait of women throughout generations, connected by an interweaving web of isolation and dark internalisations. The distance was precisely the point; it was purposeful in showing how glossed over these women were. In a sad way, there were all connected to each other but only through location and not period.

Some truly horrific, haunting imagery here, and very much interesting in what it seeks to tell us. Not sure how much of it lands, but managed to be superbly miserable

Name: After the Hunt
Review:
London Film Festival Film #15

Ok, here we go. My hot take, I guess. The reactions to this film, I feel, represent the climate that we’re in politically right now. This idea that— especially on the left— if you don’t share the same opinion, completely, then your opinion is invalid.

The screenplay for this movie has a lot going on. It’s part of the problem with the film; towards the end, it tries so desperately to add more and more, to the point that it overcomplicates the plot and gets lost in its messaging. In its overcomplication, I do think it’s turning people off to the movie as a whole.

I truly thought this was a refreshing and authentic look at the impossible complications that come with, not only sexual assault allegations, but happening in a workplace and intergenerational. 

This film isn’t holding some moral superiority, dictating to you how to feel; it’s really trying to dig into the machinations of how a situation like this would be handled.

It’s balanced in the ways it wants to criticise how different generations handle dealing with sexual assault. The older generations sought to repress that emotion and internalise it for fear of what coming forward would do for their futures. The younger generations are desperate to externalise and let it be known what has happened to them.

The film is not telling you either is better or worse; it’s more so interested in playing in the middle, in looking at the conflict itself between these ideologies and the people who hold them. How do they feel about each other?

As much as it’s looking into both generations, it really is scrutinising Robert’s character’s way of dealing with things. Something which crops up in multiple areas of her life, this avoidance of causing others hassle and in turn doing herself more harm.

It’s truly fascinating to see how her destructive pattern results in the way she has ended up; it does perhaps go a little too far, which is a real shame.

In equal measure, I think Edibiri’s character is put into focus, less to scrutinise her, but more to look at the viewer’s relationships to victims of sexual assault. What happens when those victims aren’t perfect people, when they’re equally fallible? What if they also are wealthy and privileged? Do you believe them less?

There’s a really interesting line there and Edibiri plays this character so perfectly, honestly Roberts and Garfield are also incredible. She’s characterised near perfectly, she’s somebody, like many in younger generations, who is able to be so accepting yet at the same time lacking any sort of understanding for an experience which isn’t theirs.

I got a lot out of this. Really needed to be neatened up near the end as it does get so messy, to the point that I understand why people aren’t giving this time, but having sat with this, I think there’s a lot more going on.

Name: Hamnet
Review:
London Film Festival Film #16

I just have to talk about the experience I had watching this because, oh my goodness. I was lucky enough to get a ticket to the UK premiere of Hamnet and it was a sensational way to watch this film. To be in a room with 2,000 other people, primed and ready to be emotionally invested in a very primal, naturalistic portrayal of grief was something that I imagine will rarely be replicated.

On top of that, I got to see Steven Spielberg in the flesh!!! Which I still struggle to comprehend. He gave a lovely speech about Chloe Zhao, who then led us all through a meditation before the movie and I think it was such a profound and vital addition to me enjoying the film as much as I did. Really centering yourself and allowing you to be in touch with the frequency the movie was operating on from the get-go, I think was super beneficial.

What proceeded that was one of the most gorgeous displays of humanity I've seen onscreen. It is very hard to do realism, hyper-realism really, and very naturalistic scenarios right but almost immediately, this film made you feel like you were part of this world.

I could put this down to so many elements of the movie. I think every department here is operating on just another level. It is gorgeously realised by Łukasz Żal, who cements himself further as one of my favourite modern-day cinematographers. It's composed to perfection by Max Richter. It is so beautifully coloured and composed, this world feels real. The editing is so unnoticeable, it makes you feel like you're living amongst this family. But truly, this is a film that's acting and performance, and none more so than the incomparable Jesse Buckley.

This is her movie. She is absolutely outstanding. The modes in which she has to operate in here are really extreme, but never did it feel like she was putting on a performance; it felt so real. Obviously, she will get a lot of credit for the larger-than-life moments, the screaming, the unbearable weight of her grief, but I think it's in her quiet moments that she truly shines. 

And then the film wouldn't work half as well as it does if it weren't for the family in the beginning of the movie, feeling as real as it does. I find myself tearing up at those joyous scenes of family just as much as I was the more devastating parts. This comes down to their family dynamic just feeling so authentic. It truly just felt like you were witnessing, like, home movies from that period of time, which is so special. And her emotional resolution towards the end is something I'll be thinking about for a very long time.

It's not only her; there are a plethora of great performances here. Paul Mescal is good; I think once he eases into the role of Shakespeare as time goes on, he becomes far more of a standout. It's truly the child performances around Buckley, though, that stand out. Both of the Jupe brothers are absolutely phenomenal here. Jacobi Jupe sells the absolute hell out of the performance, makes you fall in love with him, and makes the situation all the more devastating. And Noah, I have to give a heap ton of credit to because this is perhaps the most I've ever enjoyed Shakespeare.

This film certainly feels like a constant emotional rise to the point of the story, which you know is going to happen. This is more so an observation and less a criticism, but it does feel slightly emotionally manipulative up until that point. In the way things are shot, in how the characters speak. And particularly in the utilisation of music in these scenes. I do need to rewatch it to see how I feel about them perhaps playing on the audience’s knowledge to provoke emotional reaction prior to the emotional turning point of the film happening.

I do love how much this feels like a Chloe Zhao movie though. Her signature style is written all over the film. Especially hearing her talk prior to the movie. You can see the stamp she left in this story. The ethereal qualities that the movie possesses, the naturalistic ways that she shot, and the very human way that she crafts characters. The best word that I can use to describe this movie is just incredibly human. It feels so real. It's so delicate and tender and explores the healing powers of nature and people in the context of nature.

The film begins to mount and mount, and it feels like it is all heading towards this emotional resolution, this climax at the end of the movie. Hats off to both Zhao and O’Farrell for managing to write something so poignant that does feel like this emotional payoff but also feels like a release not only for the characters in the movie but for the journey. You've been on throughout the entire film. A truly gorgeous film that had me weeping

Name: Hedda
Review:
London Film Festival Film #17

Really solid film, dripping with style and pulsing with a feverish sense of temptation. DiCosta manages to pull you in so quickly with the allure of Hedda, a woman so tantalising and intriguing, it’s hard to look away. 

Played so expertly by Tessa Thompson, the film crafts a portrait of a woman who is desperate to control and who believes she is, but what’s more fascinating is the lack of control she holds and the delusion she feels over her situation.

It’s when her facade fades, when she cracks, and we see the real Hedda, the person who recognises how out of hand this situation is getting that this film really shines.

Name: Sentimental Value
Review:
London Film Festival Film #18

I'm not sure anybody is exploring human emotions on such a profoundly unique, internal, intimate level as well as Joaquin Trier is. Sentimental Value is yet another addition to his oeuvre of films that is exploring what it's like to be human. What it's like to experience the myriad of emotions that we all go through, what it's like to make mistakes and to fail and to feel regret and to make up for it. He's exploring incredibly fallable people, which at the end of the day is what all humans are.

This movie is profoundly tender and ever so sweet. It’s a far more mature piece of work. The people that he is characterising here feel more fully formed, and it's as much about looking back as it is about living in the present. It's this almost pull between the past and the present that creates beautifully naturalistic tension and gives it room to flourish.

I'd be remissed if I didn't talk about the overbearing similarities between this and Hamnet, two of the biggest films of the festival, and of the year, both of them dealing with a father's inability to express love in the ways that the people in his life need from him and resorting to this love language, this way to express the emotions that are buried inside him.

It's a very unique take, which is hilarious saying that since there have been multiple instances of this explored in the festival, but I really love the resistance that is portrayed here to their father's way of expressing his love and also the complications that the father struggles with in conveying across this piece of art as his way of expressing love. Whether or not that's clear to him and is a conscious thought or not. It's fascinating to be in a position to realise the faults of why the characters are not as close even if this is unclear to the characters in the film.

It differs quite profoundly from Hamnet in its execution of this artistic moment. As it never really feels like it's building towards any sort of emotional climax, it is not really emotionally manipulative throughout the film; it feels very naturalistic. I mean, it probably feels this way because everything happens quite quietly. It is all happening from a distance, but it's quietly powerful and just because you're at a distance, it doesn’t mean that you don’t feel the interpersonal burden that these characters are carrying and the emotional turmoil they're going through.

It's actually this distance that helps get across our characters’ lack of intimacy in their lives and their craving deeper, more meaningful relationships. I don't think anybody else is writing interpersonal relationships as well as Trier. You're treated to four characters in this; the cast is kept so small, but there are so many different, fascinating dynamics between all of them to explore. All of these are held by incredible performances, and surprisingly, this does build to a gorgeously realised finale that really struck a chord. The power of art, the power of a father's love, and the power to truly understand people

Name: Sirāt
Review:
London Film Festival Film #19

Propulsive, explosive filmmaking, that will shock you to your core. Oddly existential and exploring really big ideas despite its really stripped back and simple story.

I don't think I've seen a cinema full of people so taken aback by some of the things that happen in this - granted I was in the BFI IMAX which was perhaps the most intense way to experience this film.

It's made even more impactful by the intense score which becomes a character unto itself. Really becomes symbolic for the transendant properties the characters find in music

Name: Is This Thing On?
Review:
London Film Festival Film #20

So Bojack coded its nuts. Bradley Cooper’s third directorial effort now cements him as a director I truly will get excited for. This was good! Far less ambitious than his previous outings, but still a solid piece of work.

It’s absolutely secure now that Cooper is so talented when it comes to getting performances from his cast. This is a career highlight for Arnett and yet another wonderful performance from Dern.

Not everything works here, but it’s a charming little story about how people need passions outside of their relationship to fuel their love. It’s about the insecurities of being in love with past versions of people and the mature reality of parents having lives outside of their kids. It’s small, but I got a lot out of this

Name: Roofman
Review:
London Film Festival Film #21

What a charming film. Funny, sweet, tender and so perfectly portrays who this man is. Tatum is spectacular, so is Dunst, in lesser hands this wouldn’t have worked

Name: The Stranger
Review:
London Film Festival Film #22

Sorry, I got so very little out of this. It's gorgeous, the world is miserable and the protagonist is unbearably handsome

Name: Blue Heron
Review:
London Film Festival Film #23

It would very easy for this film to simply be an exercise in emotional exploitation. The harrowing story that Romvari is recounting is enough to provoke an emotional reaction from the audience. However, what they choose to do here is something more profound, so deeply internal and reflective, that it ends up being moving in a whole different way.

What transpires here is a piece that is only achieveable through, not only a first-hand account, but somebody who has already done a degree of reflection on the situation themselves. The film is fractured and purposefully cloudy, as this is something that no person would ever really come to fully grasp, but there's an infinite well of heart, love and understanding on offer here.

Romvari has crafted something wholly their own, a family dynamic that at once feels warm but also distant. A past which feels fractured but full of love, and the simultaneous sadness and gratefulness for her childhood. 

Initially, the switch up in the style of filmmaking was jarring - as I was so immersed in this world they had crafted - but it wins you over very quickly, it becomes a far more internal piece, about trying to process memory both as child and grown-up. It's unlike anything I've seen, it's quietly devastating, and feels almost theraputic in the ways in processes memory and grief through artistic outlets

Name: TRON: Ares
Review:
Honestly, nowhere near as bad as I was expecting. I was expecting something offensively bad, instead it's... just boring. Ultimately, that's worse.

I have no affinity to this franchise, but even still I'm able to see how it's a breeding ground for interesting conversations surrounding AI and the commercialisation of technology. The film is not interested in this in the slightest.

It's got some nice visuals, the score is good but after the first half - which I was actually enjoying somewhat due to some good action scenes - the film just becomes so bland and uninteresting. It's difficult to completely hate though when there's so much neon red

Name: Good Fortune
Review:
Look, this is pretty fun for the most part. It's got some decent laughs and I do believe the film is well intentioned in its exploration of how wealth is able to affect quality of life in today's world. However, as this film progressed, I got the feeling it was starting to talk down to its audience, as if it didn't really know what it's central message was past "sure money makes life better, but it's not everything".

Going into a movie like this you know the main character is going to have to go back to his old life at some point. You expect the narrative would show, despite his life being easier with money, it's nothing compared to the authenticity of his old life. The film takes a while to get to this point -  which I adored actually - it really shows how easy his life has became with money, but it makes a really poor argument for why he should go back to his old life.

Essentially, it just boils down to the fact that he's not living his real life and is missing the relationships from his old life. That's it. It even happens all in a single scene. It feels like somebody out of touch with the struggles working class people go through trying to write a narrative which is meaningful.

This is further emphasised by the just ludicrous ending where, tech CEOs are suddenly going to treat their workers right, and a bunch of working class people quitting their jobs cause they no longer want to deal with the bad working conditions.

It would be great if this happened, but what happens a few minutes after this film ends, where the CEOs reject the idea of paying workers fairly and the people who've quit their jobs have no means to survive. I do respect the idealism of the film but it felt more insulting than anything. Like people who have money trying to understand people who struggle

Name: Wasteman
Review:
London Film Festival Film #24

Intense, visceral and raw. Wasteman is a fantastic debut feature that’s operating on a level of realism that I’ve rarely seen executed so well.

It’s propulsive, and will make you squirm in your seat. It’s shining a light on how brutal and unforgiving the prison system is, sometimes fashioning more dangerous people in the process, really makes you laugh that it’s meant to be a place of rehabilitation

Violence is not something unknown to cinema but the violence here truly feels so real, it’s quite hard to watch. However, you’re drawn back to the screen by Tom Blyth and David Jonsson, who by my estimation are the best young actors working today

Name: Nouvelle Vague
Review:
London Film Festival Film #25

Black and White filmmaking, a story about one of the most famous periods of film history and Richard Linklater dialogue, yeah this was always gonna work.

Felt like I was watching Avengers: Endgame for French New Wave fans

Name: The Mastermind
Review:
London Film Festival Film #26

The first half of this sings, it's so precise and calculated, but also so loose. Following this deadbeat dad who is desperately seeking purpose in his life and self-sabotaging himself to feel something.

Josh O'Connor continues his streak of why he is indie cinemas' it boy right now and the jazzy score is a delight, but this take a turn in the second half that just didn't work for me. It demonstates the consequences of his actions well, and the mindset of this truly lost man, but the way in which that's told to us I didn't love

Name: Springsteen: Deliver Me from Nowhere
Review:
London Film Festival Film #27

Look, I'm a known hater of musical biopics, so I wasn't going into this with high expectations, and I guess I was pleasantly surprised? Jeremy Allen White is absolutely phenomenal, as somebody with very little Springsteen knowledge, he did a really great job emboding the man and exploring the emotional complexities of him.

It is laiden with the typical biopic problems, really noticable here is how the love story plays out, but there's a bit more going on. I feel like I got to understand Springsteen at a point in his life. It's cozy, warm and intimate in areas as he processes his life through song, but simultaneously it was incredibly surface level with the problems he was going through. 

Speaks more to musical biopics as a whole though that even a mention of mental health felt like depth compared to others

Name: La Grazia
Review:
London Film Festival Film #28

Really solid character study of man made almost frozen by the weight of his life, and results in the inability to make choices moving forward. Has some issues but Tony Servillo gives a phenomenal performance and I really dug the odd rave score

Name: 100 Nights of Hero
Review:
London Film Festival Film #29

Visually fun - in parts - and a wonderfully realised world, with quite a shallow story that didn't really work. For a film that's about a revolution, a queer, feminist revolution no less, it lacked substance and depth. I really struggled to get much out of this, it feels very juvinile in its portrail of gender as well

Name: Blue Moon
Review:
London Film Festival Film #30

God, I'm realising that Linklater might be one of favourite writers. The combo of him and Hawke is just too good. Hawke gives perhaps my favourite performance I saw at the festival, he fully embodies Hart and was mesmerising to watch perform.

The film is utterly hilarious but also very quiet and profound, there's a deep sadness running throughout this night and the comedy feels like a mask for the inner feelings of Hart. Such a great hangout movie, at once hilarous but deeply poetic, Linklater makes this verbally hypotising, and shocking interesting enough from a blocking point of view as well

Name: Pillion
Review:
London Film Festival Film #31

Utterly hilarous, sexy and doesn't shy away from homsexual intimacy, which is cool to see on screen. A journey of both sexual and romantic discovery, which feels really authentic and intimate.

There's a clear palpable chemistry between the leads here that does a lot of the heavy lifting in making you feel this is a real realtionship. Skarsgård and Melling are wonderful, really baring themselves to these roles and making it feel so emotionally vulnerable. Melling especially I think gets across the unsure nature of himself when it comes to being sexual so well, his learning to become more comfortable and confident in himself was a pleasure to witness.

I love that this film was able to explore the beauty of the BDSM LGBTQ+ community but it also did try to explore the darker side of this, the complexities and problems which form in these sorts of relationships.

Unsure how to feel as a whole about the film, where Skarsgård ends up is beautiful, but I'm just no sure how to feel about the messaging of the last few scenes. However, a really good debut feature that truly is utterly hilarious

Name: Black Girl
Review: Kind of monumental filmmaking, it’s very hard to imagine how impactful this would’ve been at the time. Truly a horrible look at African exploitation and the fetishisation of people. I really loved how internal this was as well, how we got to feel how suffocating this life was

Name: Regretting You
Review: Objectively insane. Like the plot of this is truly mad. I do think had this either leaned a bit more camp, or a bit more serious it could’ve worked a lot better. I still had fun though I had a white wine which feels mandatory for this film

Name: Zootopia
Review: Honestly kind of major. How a movie like this tackles racism, government interference with stoking culture wars and manipulation of the public, and makes it really digestible is so bloody intelligent

Name: WandaVision
Review: Ugh Wanda you are so loved. I’m so glad they definitely followed this masterpiece of a character study up with equally good writing

Name: Spotlight
Review: The amount of times I audibly gasped is truly insane. That Silent Night song choice was truly diabolical omg

Name: Bugonia
Review:
Wow, really rewards you for a repeat viewing. On an initial watch, I wasn’t sure how to feel about the last scene, but I really think it does a phenomenal job at hammering home this point: if humanity wants to survive, we need to learn to have a conversation with people, even if we don’t agree with them.

And actually, the way that we have constructed society, we have ostracised people so much that we don’t listen to them at all, and that is only doing us more damage. Jesse Plemons’ character is so interesting because he is so sympathetic in this movie; you can see how he’s ended up where he has, and while you don’t condone his actions, it’s very clear to see how the way we have fashioned our world would lead him to doing things that he does instead of just being able to talk to people because nobody will listen to him.

Name: Now You See Me
Review: I would credit Dave Franco as a bit of a sexual awakening for me so tell me why I was more drawn to Jesse Eisenberg in this film, why is he is so fine in this movie

Name: Wicked: One Wonderful Night
Review:
Why did this get me emotional? You know what, the love and effort that was put into this was truly something special. It's clear that everybody involved in every aspect of Wicked, including the marketing which this essentially is, clearly has love for the musical.

That's all it takes though, people who are genuinely interested in what they're doing, people who are pouring love into every piece, to make something feel important. I guee it doesn't hurt having some of the most special voices you've ever heard who are pouring so much emotion into the songs. For Good, Popular and of course Defying Gravity were incredible

Name: Now You See Me 2
Review:
I don't trust people who see that card scene and their first thought is "UM THATS NOT REALISTIC" stfu it's cool that's all that matters. 

Aside from that scene my god is this a downgrade, it's plagued by mid 2010s comedy. It's funny how 15 year old me must've thought this was peak cinema though, like the shot of the stairs as an eye was genuinely so cool and that being the entrance to the eye, might've as well told me cinematography was invented by this movie

Name: Frankenstein
Review:
Hm. I have feelings about this which are in dichtomy with each other. There's clearly so much to admire here. As if it needs to be said, but the film is gorgeous. The sets are so intricate, the costumes so detailed, GDT has crafted a world so full of life and colour, it feels tangible. Conversely though, it's also visually a bit disappointing.

In an effort to continually showcase how intricate and expansive the sets are, the film ends up feeling a bit flat visually. Everything is shot in wide and there's no dynamism, which ends up hurting the film. In places this is why there's an emotional detachment, as so rarely are we up close and personal with these people. Some truly horrible CGI as well.

The writing in places is so romantic and gorgeous. It leans heavily into the themes of fatherhood and belonging, there's a tenderness and love throughout. Yet, at the same time, there is some of the most hamfisted dialogue. I mean the main culprit of this is straight up telling Frankenstein he is the monster, come on who needed that spelled out for them.

It's a real shame but the film is so overly simplistic, that it takes away from the moments of depth. Frankenstein turning on his monster in an instant, Elizabeth falling for the monster for no real reason, the monster being so pure and innocent, in all these instances characters are stripped of any real depth or nuance and it hurts the film.

I really didn't love Elizabeth in this. Feels very much like the typical female character we get in these stories, to the point where I was kind of rolling my eyes when she ends up doing something.

Frankenstein's story is somewhat interesting, seeing what makes him tick, it's no surprise though given how much time we spend on his story. However, even parts of his story felt rushed, we meandered on parts which didn't add much to him. The runtime drags, especially for the story the film covers.

Performances across the board were great, Isaac was brilliant and so was Elordi, even if I don't seem to be as head over heels for him as others are, his voice work was incredible though.

I'm just bewildered at the lack of complexity, the film feels stripped of any real depth sadly

Name: Spider-Man: No Way Home
Review:
Watched this in the Royal Albert Hall with the Royal Symphony Orchestra!!!!

Gorgeous music, Michael Giacchino you are so loved!

I will say this time round I really noticed how ugly this movie is :( It’s a real shame that not only do the VFX look shaky, but the composition of most every scene really isn’t great. However, it’s made up for by the sheer love and passion clearly poured into this

Name: Pirates of the Caribbean: The Curse of the Black Pearl
Review:
Y'know it’s funny watching this and realising it’s kind of the blueprint for the modern blockbuster - quippy humour, large set pieces and a formulaic story.  What sets it apart from these modern day blockbusters though is a commitment to filmmaking.

It feels so real, there’s a level of care put into this, tactile filmmaking people! Who would’ve thought that putting care into this helps it hold up.

Name: West Side Story
Review:
Just magical witnessing this on the big screen with a full cinema. The colours! The music! Rita Murano! 

I will say I still don’t give a shit about the central romance which is a real shame, and the love over hate message falls a little flat when I still don’t get why somebody would just suddenly forgive their man for killing their brother like come on.

Really funny to have a cinema full of adults laugh at the line “When you come, use the back door” just a nice reminder that we are all silly

Name: Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest
Review: I’m sorry nobody has ever looked as good as Orlando Bloom does in these movies. On an unrelated note, in this family movie, we learn how big Will Turner’s dick is.

Name: Now You See Me: Now You Don't
Review:
Ok, look. These movies aren’t good, they truly are not, but there’s still something charming about them.

I was incredibly worried about the first few scenes of this film, truly horrible introduction back into this world, and honestly I wasn’t completely feeling on board until the final sequence.

It strikes a balance between practical magic (which it should’ve leaned into more!) and CGI slop that I wasn’t completely uninterested, but this world is far more interesting when the things they’re doing are actually possible.

There’s a fun continued sequence of classic magic tricks that captures the feeling of what these films should be. 

I will say it’s better than its predecessor because it’s lacking that horrible 2010s humour and actually gagged me with the final trick. (Says more about my mental state as of late than the actual film)

Name: Die My Love
Review:
Good lord, what a maddening journey. JLaw reminds us why she is amongst the best working actors right now with an absolutely all-encompassing performance. Loved how primal and animalistic this felt, it feels incredible raw and honest about the sudden burden motherhood takes on a woman and the world around her failing to reflect that change. Perhaps it’s not she who is mad, but the failing to acknowledge the change that is.

It’s cyclical, and doesn’t really push the needle, instead opting to hammer home the same point, but I found that so compelling. This idea that motherhood is consuming - both in good and bad ways. We don’t learn much outside other than the fact that she is a mother, but I feel like this intentional, to show how all consuming motherhood is, and how women get reduced to that role.

Some really gorgeous symbols and imagery here, fell in love with idea of using a house in place of a body to show the toll children take on their mothers. Truly monumental filmmaking feels unlike anything else I’ve seen

Name: The Running Man
Review:
More like Glenn Towel, am I right?

Directed by Edgar Wright. Are we sure?

Lacks any sort of directorial flourish, and feels woefully generic. It's fun enough, and gets by on the charm and charisma of its main cast, but it's tonally a mess and feels so unbearably surface-level.

There are pockets, and moments where this shines - normally in the action scenes - but the more I think about this film, I just find more problems. There's a plethora of questionable choices made in the film, a severely underdeveloped lead character - he's angry… and? And the film tries to become so grand by the end that it ends up feeling void of any real punch.

Just a really undercooked script that beats you over the head with the same message and, shockingly for a Wright film, truly bad editing in places. Man I'm so sad

Name: Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End
Review: Lowkey the best wedding to ever be filmed

Name: Wicked
Review: It's sadly still bugs me how flat and lifeless this looks, but it's a testament to the performances that I enjoy this as much as I do, that Ozdust scene is still so moving

Name: A Very Jonas Christmas Movie
Review: I watched this for Chloe Bennet, who is far away the best actor in this movie and somebody who needs to be in more

Name: Wicked: For Good
Review:
Makes sense that this film is a part 2, and it was filmed back-to-back with the first, as most of the criticism and praise feels identical to the first movie.

These are not pretty movies. I feel that there was a concerted effort to improve the colour grading from the previous movie, but in place of that is an over-reliance on CGI, often making locations feel non-existent.

It’s not shot with any sort of magic; there’s no real enhancement to the musical scenes. There’s no real dynamism or wonder to these scenes; it feels like they’re taking place in a theatre; it’s not using the medium of film to enhance the songs.

It’s no wonder that the songs which work best are simply the emotional songs when they hold on to the evocative faces of the actors singing. It’s the undeniable best part of the movie. 

There is no way these films would work as well as they do without the central performances. Erivo and Grande are spellbinding here; they fully embody these characters, making them so layered and complex. Particularly Grande in this part, who is wonderfully characterised, maintaining my love for Glinda as a character, put together I think the entire story she has is truly something special.

The songs, while not featuring the bangers from the first, I think are really good. Perhaps because they’re a lot slower and more sad, people aren’t vibing with them as much. But Girl in the Bubble and No Good Deed are great, and For Good is up there with some of the most gorgeous songs I’ve ever heard - made me cry, of course it did.

I’d be very low on this film if it weren’t for how much I’m moved by the performances. It’s incredibly messy, especially towards the beginning; it’s like the film is desperate to get Elphaba and Glinda back together as that’s where it’s best, and it knows this. I also don’t know if this is part of the musical, but the ending is weird. Makes me feel like it undoes a lot of what makes it impactful, but nonetheless, I had a good time watching this. I must’ve been in a crowd of super fans on opening night as well, which was adding to my enjoyment. The integration in Wizard of Oz is also super weird

Name: The Bend in the River
Review:
Truly a special documentary, so impactful that I immediately want to go back and watch the director’s previous work. 

What a gift reflection is, the ability to look back on our lives and see how we have changed. In capturing such raw, authentic moments, film allows the viewer, and the subject, to engage with others’ lives in ways that narrative filmmaking often doesn’t.

It’s often the small moments, the tiny pieces of magic. The small gestures, the little glances, the things we do impulsively that compound to make a meaningful life.

Kind of obsessed with this idea of seeking an authentic life, and seeking to understand life and how it mirrored both in filmmaking (from film to digital) and the powerful symbolism of the river.

Truly an achievement in documentary filmmaking, edited so powerfully - the amount of footage poured over to get something this lean is spectacular - what an absolute delight that has fundamentally changed the way I look at documenting through film

Name: Stiller & Meara: Nothing Is Lost
Review:
I was always going to love this, I have such a soft spot for documentaries made using archival footage, especially when they are personal.

The sheer volume of footage Stiller had to work with here is a filmmakers dream, not only having famous parents but a parent who was obsessed with recording. What materialises out of this is a really intimate, and brutally personal, portrayal of his parent's marriage.

It’s an examination of them but also how their relationship left ripples throughout his family, and how those ripples seem to ripple themselve. Just a really gorgeous doc

Name: King Hamlet
Review: Not sure if this had a big an impact as I wanted, it at times is both intimate but also feels removed. What I will say though is that there is a palpable love running through this entire thing. Love from behind the camera for the subject, love from the subject for their work and love surrounding the subject in hard times.

Name: Behind the Curtain: Stranger Things: The First Shadow
Review: Made working in theatre look like hell. Cool insight into this world just needed to show us more, it felt like they were making this with an arm tied behind their back due to what they didn't want to have

Name: Heat
Review: Um… am I missing something. This has a really rough first half imo, put really finds its footing as time goes on, Pacino is an absolute delight though every single scene he has is unreal

Name: Desperate Journey
Review: Not sure if it's the fault of the film, as it's based on a true story, but this is stuffed with every cliche possible and for the most part looks like it has a really low budget. The main performance was good though, not really sure what the point of telling this non-linear was as well as it kind of diffused the tension of where the story would go

Name: Zootopia 2
Review:
Well...shit. Congrats Disney on making a sequel that was genuinely good. I had a lot of fun with Zootopia 2; it's essentially the same movie as the first (kind of beat for beat), but it works.

Have a central mystery based on prejudice, explore the dynamics between Judy and Nick, fill the world with tonnes of media references, and then pull a genuinely satisfying ending to wrap it up.

What makes this as satisfying as it is, though, is how genuinely compelling the mystery and the central theme are. The first movie was a very light but ultimately gratifying exploration of racism, and here they further expand that to exploring even more marginalised groups and how the 1% and the people who really run the world have been manipulating and exploiting these marginalised groups. It's very easy to understand and unsubtle, but it really works in this movie.

The references in the world as well just feel like world building; they're genuinely funny but also feel a part of this world. This world itself is so expensive that actually making more projects within this world feels right.

I was kind of gagged by where the story goes as well. I had a good time, man. I didn't realise how crazy of a fan base this movie has; people were cheering in my cinema.

Name: Wicked: For Good
Review:
I’ve felt so conflicted about this film since I first watched it, so much so I dragged myself back for a rewatch to see how I felt…

It cemented in my brain that this film is really riding on its performances (specifically Grande) and the undeniable incredibly emotional ending (I somehow was sobbing).

If it weren’t for these elements the film truly is a disaster. It’s horribly shot, it features some really odd pacing issues, it has bad performances, they forget to do anything with the plot, it’s somehow rushed in places, and it just feels so tied down by having to tie into The Wizard of Oz - which it doesn’t even do properly! 

Still happy that For Good exists, and truly the main performances are unbelievable, but there’s just so many issues and I don’t think they’re able to be overlooked

Name: Eternity
Review: Would've been a better movie with more Challengers vibes. Please put Da'vine Joy Randolph in every movie ever now!!

Name: The Muppets
Review: Why is this lowkey a sequel to The Devil Wears Prada?

Name: The Devil Wears Prada
Review: “Oh by all means move at a glacial pace” best line delivery in cinema maybe

Name: Love Actually
Review:
Huh, I’m a known hater of this film, so much so it doesn’t make it on my usual rotation of Christmas films but I thought I’d see if I still held that opinion. And I don’t, and do?

The film is messy, paced horribly and some stories are more interesting than others, but it’s got a heart and love to it that’s also so satisfying

Name: Elf
Review: Where are these types of movies now? It’s kinda weird that since this has came out there’s been very little films able to become main holiday films

Name: Eagles of the Republic
Review: Absolutely fascinating story that had me enthralled throughout but really towards the ending. I was fortunate enough to be invited to a screening with the director and my word, I was equally compelled by him. Using art as a political message and learning to stand up in trying times, using art as a weapon. I left the screening feeling quite hopeful about the bleak world

Name: Thunderbolts*
Review: Um hey Sentry drop your ab routine

Name: Kill Bill: The Whole Bloody Affair
Review: It’s been a while I was this in awe with a film. Why tf did it make me watch a Fortnite film though

Name: The Lost Chapter: Yuki's Revenge
Review: I mean you just can’t make this shit up, in the same week QT releases a film full of Fortnite characters and says Paul Dano is the worst part of There Will Be Blood…

Name: Titanic
Review: Might be the best love story on screen. Unbelievably well paced, I’m kind of shocked how well this holds up

Name: Sunset Boulevard
Review: Just a sheer force of nature. Was maybe the blueprint for the obsessed artist trope which is found in so many modern pieces. Norma Desmond is an absolute delight on screen. Transfixed and enamoured by this film and its wonderful final scene

Name: Train Dreams
Review: Gorgeous portrait of a man’s life that is absolutely devastating. Stunningly shot, helps that this has some of the most beautiful sunsets, and really digs into something that plagues every human in the modern world, have our modern sensibilities taken us away from what’s real

Name: Rental Family
Review: Such a perfectly solid film. Was hoping the entire time it would go just a bit deeper into the invasiveness of this job and the impact it has on both the employee and the people who hire them but it’s not concerned with that. Instead we get a perfectly wholesome film helmed by a great performance

Name: Ella McCay
Review:
Completely and utterly baffling movie. Feels like a first draft script with a first draft edit. Why do characters random exclaim things as if they’re not in the same room and why does it take so long for people to react?

The narrative structure makes absolutely no sense and has no payoff. We spend so much time with all of these characters and yet none of their relationships feel real, spare a few scenes with Emma Mackay and JLC. Wow what an absolute misfire. She doesn’t even fix her shoe in the movie, I think this speaks to how Ella is meant to be viewed - this clumsy do gooder who is just trying her best to make everything good - but that’s not who she is

Name: One Battle After Another
Review: Jump scared by the Haim headshot

Name: Avatar
Review: Ok broad shoulders and a snatched waist! Where do I get the Na’Vi workout routine?!?

Name: Avatar: The Way of Water
Review: I mean it really is insane how this looks. Literally every close up of a hand I was just completely transfixed. Suffers from weak characters and the most cliche plot, but there’s something still quite engrossing about this. It helps that this is so expansive and really lays the pieces down for a wonderful third act and did I mention how good this looks!

Name: Lurker
Review:
So bloody smart. The oppression of the surveillance state we are living in, which for people in the public light is turned up to the nth degree, but also the thrill of said surveillance state, the allure of it. The adoration and power it promises and possesses and the temptation of that status being enough to keep us in it, no matter the risk.

Hats off to the camera work here!!! My god this movie sings. What a stellar choice to just have the majority of the film being this oppressive and invasive camera focused purely on Matthew. It gives you ample time to fully immerse yourself in his insanity. How this slowly starts to change as power dynamics shift as well is utterly genius.

The film wouldn’t work nearly as well if it wasn’t for the performances but specifically Théodore Pellerin. Wow wow wow. He blew me away. His micro expressions are simply captivating, I couldn’t take my eyes off of him, pair that with the oppressive camera work and it makes for a real treat. I thought this was stellar

Name: Twinless
Review:
Adding this to the bob hall of fame alongside Alana “headshot” Haim from OBAA.

Sweeney has already proved himself with his debut that he knows how to portray and write a neurotic gay incredibly well, and this feature continues that streak.

Something truly impressive about this screenplay is just its sheer originality. I thought I knew what I was going to be watching and this was a hard pivot from my expectations (positive).

He takes this core idea of loneliness, of an existence longing for companionship, and bakes it into such a baffling premise that provides equal parts tension, humour and warmth, but it never fails to remind its audience of the idea he wants to interrogate.

Really thought this was wonderful and helmed by a career best performance from Dylan O’Brien, who manages to bring such heart and emotion to, what could be considered on the surface, very stereotypical characters. Insane this wasn’t a huge success

Name: Oh, Hi!
Review: I could say so much about this film: that it’s a fun way of exploring the modern dating landscape, that it’s perhaps a little long in the tooth, but the real thought dominating my mind is just how dreamy Logan Lerman is

Name: Chicago
Review:
Forgive me for my past; I was so wrong about this film. In previous me's defence, I do still think this is over-edited; some numbers don't get as much love as they should due to this.

However, I was categorically wrong about the musical numbers. The whole point is that each number is a stage; life is but a stage, and other people are the audience.

This is such an interesting piece of musical history as well. If you look at the modern musical landscape, we are so scared of not being completely literal in scenes (which is absurd considering we're watching people burst into song); everything has to be based in realism, and it often leaves musical sequences feeling very flat and uninspired, like they could be performed on a stage.

Previous me found this a criticism of this film. If each number is on a stage, why does the film exist? What he didn't take into account was the filmmaking in these scenes. There's so much dynamism and creativity on display that simply having this on a stage wouldn't have the same effect. It's thematically rich, cutting between the real world and the dream, the longing for the stage. The musical sequences are just downright gorgeous; they're bold , inspired, and add so much to the story. Truly loved this on a rewatch, and the songs will be getting streamed for the foreseeable future.

Name: Freaky Tales
Review: Just pure cinematic delight, not sure how this didn't make a huge splash. Wears its homages like a badge of honour. A film for people who love film, and who doesn’t love film.

Name: Avatar: Fire and Ash
Review:
Many, many, many thoughts on this.

The sheer technical craft on offer in these movies is second to none. Many will cite the CGI and the incomparable visual effects (as they should), but Cameron's technical prowess is not just limited to this. The way he crafts and composes scenes, his strong sense of positional awareness in action scenes, and the glorious worldbuilding are all equally discernible reasons why these movies work.

I've never been the biggest Avatar person, have always enjoyed them on the technical level, but never fully found myself immersed in the world or these characters. This film changed that.

The set-up of the world in the previous two movies pays off here, where we finally get to interrogate the characters a little more. Neytiri continues to be the standout of these films; her dealing with the fallout of her son's death and the complications that arise from having Spider be a part of their family is really scrutinised in this film.

Her storyline of faith and this being the only thing in her life that connects her to her previous life, feeling like she has lost all semblance of who she used to be, and this being contrasted with Varang is such an interesting main core of the film — which could've been pushed further.

The other characters get varying degrees of depth afforded to them, but on the whole, it feels like a level up from the previous installments. Jake and his battles with grief and Lo'ak really cemented him as a more compelling lead. Loved how this was contrasted with Quaritch as well, who just continues to be an absolute delight; Stephen Lang is so underrated.

There are elements which don't work fully. The writing in places is formulaic and doesn't seek to unpack what happens and instead plot points just hit cause they have to. I felt this way with Kiri a lot, and while in theory the idea of Spider works, and his character serves a purpose to improve other characters’ arcs, but him himself doesn't quite work, whether that's the writing or performance, I'm not sure.

You could say what you like about these movies. They don't have the most compelling characters in the world, the storylines aren't the most complex or interesting, but the visual spectacle that these movies offer, not only in terms of the VFX but also just as an action blockbuster, shouldn't be underestimated. I thought this was fire and could've sat in Pandora for hours longer.

Name: The Love That Remains
Review: Subtly gorgeous. A wonderful meditation on life, nature and connecting more deeply with the natural world. Often transfixing in the way it lulls a sense of warmth over you, contrasted by surrealist moments of very European humour. Just a very pleasant watch, with delectable cinematography and a lovely score

Name: The Holiday
Review: "I've found almost everything ever written about love to be true. Shakespeare said "Journeys end in lovers meeting." What an extraordinary thought. Personally, I have not experienced anything remotely close to that, but I am more than willing to believe Shakespeare had. I suppose I think about love more than anyone really should. I am constantly amazed by its sheer power to alter and define our lives. It was Shakespeare who also said "love is blind". Now that is something I know to be true. For some quite inexplicably, love fades; for others love is simply lost. But then of course love can also be found, even if just for the night. And then, there's another kind of love: the cruelest kind. The one that almost kills its victims. Its called unrequited love. Of that I am an expert. Most love stories are about people who fall in love with each other. But what about the rest of us? What about our stories, those of us who fall in love alone? We are the victims of the one sided affair. We are the cursed of the loved ones. We are the unloved ones, the walking wounded. The handicapped without the advantage of a great parking space." Oh Nancy Meyers you just get it

Name: The Greatest Showman
Review: So…. This was a fucking mess. Not gonna lie I think that guy who makes videos of Hugh Jackman singing ruined Hugh Jackman singing… or made it better honestly

Name: Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows: Part 1
Review: Oh how my memory of this was far better than it is. The colour grading in this film… why is every location so dark, you are able to tell a dark story without every scene looking like varying shades of grey. Some fun and necessary character development but also just so lacking as well

Name: Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows: Part 2
Review: Why is this film so grey and ugly??

Name: Marty Supreme
Review:
Why did I get blue-balled by orange balls?!?

Yeah, this blew me away. Propulsive, energetic filmmaking that lights you up inside. There's something special here with the way that Josh Safdie has managed to distill this idea of ambition, this pursuit of greatness, into something so powerful, entertaining, thrilling, and also seeks to scrutinise that ambition, where it's born from, if it's right, and what it does to the person who is holding that ambition within themselves.

It's very clear that greatness doesn't just happen; it is worked on, chiselled away piece by piece until it perhaps replaces the person who was once there. It feels very fitting for the modern-day grind set that is almost being forced upon younger generations. This ambition has consumed his life to the point where it's all he knows. If he is so assured and confident in his delusions, it becomes hard to discern where the lies begin, or even where he begins.

Marty Mauser must be one of the most fascinating characters put on screen in a while. He's deeply morally flawed, but in a world which itself is corrupt. Is he just in his pursuit of greatness if he only ever punches up? Somebody who has been beaten down by the world and told to shrink, like most people do, somebody going against that grain, against that oppression, and fighting back makes people feel uncomfortable, and the situations that arise from this and how Marty feels cognizant of the illusions that are placed in a lot of us is utterly fascinating.

None of this would work as well as it does if it weren't for the magnificent Timothée Chalamet. People can see what they would like about him. I think he is absolutely magnetic in this. He completely loses himself in this role. The ambition feels like it is clawing at him beneath his skin; you can see it etched across his face, the desire to be something more that he is, always striving forward, to be pursuing something he is deeply longing for. It is a magnificent performance that feels like a bucket of blood, sweat, and tears went into it.

Love the utilisation of the supporting cast as well, which feels like little pockets in Marty's life. Marty's life feels lived in and coloured, people come and go into his life, which feels very natural. Massive shout out to Odessa A'Zion. She was a standout performance, getting to balance the intense drama and stress with hyper comedic scenes.

It's funny looking at other Safdie brothers’ work. This feels on paper less stressful than the others; however, what I find fascinating is that stress underpins Marty as a character, it's not necessarily a constant in the events that are going on, but it lives within him and thus creates this utterly fascinating atmosphere where you are stressed because he is, because he is never resting; he's always looking for more. He has to constantly be moving forward, and that in itself creates stress.

At the end of the day, this is a movie about choices and the culmination of those choices; how the choices in life that we make do have an effect on us. It is the pursuit of greatness and the trappings that come with that; it is being the underdog in the world that perceives you as a single thing and does not like it if you aim higher; it is a masterpiece in screenwriting from Josh Safdie. Just an utterly brilliant display and balance of tone; one of the funniest films of the year yet also one of the most profound; it has one of the most delectable 80s scores that blends in and out of its soundtrack so seamlessly. It is chopped full of great performances, and it is one of the best movies of the year. It has lit me up and inspired me in ways that very few films have.

Dream big.
