Name: Stranger Things 5: The Finale
Review: The more that I sit on this finale, the more it just works. This felt like Stranger Things on top form. Larger-than-life action, scenarios that the ordinary person would never find themselves in, but thrust into this situation, the collective power of community, friendship, and family is found and is enough to overcome any evil. It’s what the show has always been about. Loved that it felt that the Duffer Brothers were speaking through Mike in the final scene, that just because everybody has a happy ending, that there is a level of joy doesn’t negate the journey, the horrors that they went through, but also compelling stories don’t need to be riddled with horror and sadness. That perhaps the love that you surround yourself with is enough. The finale hit me like a ton of bricks being a kid starting this show and ending it as somebody who just moved away from all my friends and family, fuckkkk it hit. It’s what I’ve always admired and loved about this show, it’s the mystery and the world building, sure, but really it’s more about the community and strength that a person is able to get through friends and family.  I’m so glad I’ve been off the internet while this finale has been airing as it seems to me from the little I’ve seen that the main critiques of this finale is very cinema sins in nature. Which I just hate. Did the episode make you feel? Did it bring about a poignant conclusion to multiple characters’ stories? Was it a reminder and culmination of the power of nostalgia, and the collective mourning of childhood? The finale served its purpose and I couldn’t be happier. It’s kind of a faultless wrap-up to this show, it made me laugh and cry, and cry, and cry, reminisce on the journey this show has been on and the journey I have been on at the same time. I seriously don’t think it could’ve been much better. Like how many shows wrap up this good? With the just insane number of characters in this show I’m shocked they managed to give them all an extremely satisfying conclusion but they did it and it made me sob so much

Name: The History of Sound
Review:
Kinda loved this? Hermanus has such a command over timing, the lingering shots, the yearning out of the performances and letting them breath , and the choice to focus on listening as much as speaking. Just another shining example of why queer romance is endlessly more fascinating to tell stores about. 

The sheer palpability of the chemistry between Mescal and O’Connor in the beginning of the film is something to behold. It’s so erotic, both of them playing on the implication of what could happen, their longing glances speaking so much. 

None of this sexual tension between them is reliant on sex, and very little physical intimacy or nudity (save for some Paul Mescal butt cleavage) which feels refreshing for queer media.

Where this goes is very well worn but when it’s crafted this beautifully it’s hard to go wrong. It stumbles a little towards the end, it’s reliant on a performance I didn’t love, but when you have two of the greatest actors working today and have spent an entire movie with them, it’s hard to maintain that quality

Really loved this

Name: The Secret Agent
Review:
Been sitting on this movie for a while, something about it really stirred me in a profound way.

I was lucky enough to attend a Q&A with both Kleber Mendonça Filho and Wagner Moura, and it really contextualised what was already a really heavy, powerful film.

What’s beautiful is the film isn’t weighed down by the heaviness of its narrative. It crafts an accurate portrayal of this world at the time, the humour, the horror, and the honesty is all on display.

Using the guise of “The Secret Agent” is genius; you expect a completely different movie. You expect a slick, spy thriller. Our introduction into this world is trying to piece together who Wagner Moura is. Why is he on the run? What has happened to make him end up in this situation?

As the truth is slowly peeled back, you realise how wrongly you read the situation, and suddenly it casts a large, looming shadow over everything you’ve seen up until this point.

The film sings in its middle section, as you begin to learn more about him, and the frustration is able to sit in. With so much of this being based in history which has tried to be erased, it rings loudly.

The film ramps up to a poignant and jarring ending - in love with the idea of Brazil killing people twice, both in person and in the press. Incredible, moving piece of work which feels so singular in its storytelling to the north east of Brazil yet simultaneously so relevant and timely for our ever-growing divided society

Name: Lost Highway
Review:
"I like to remember things my own way, not necessarily how they happened."

Watched at the BFI with the packed crowd to celebrate Lynch, the ideal way to experience this movie for the first time.

What an absolute mindfuck in the best way possible. I truly think this is one of those movies that's not meant to be fully understood but instead meant to be felt. Like that woman in Tenet said "Don't try to understand it, feel it."

Such a fascinating exploration of the delusions which interfere with how we move in the world and fragility of male ego. The blurred lines between reality and memory and what we considered to be an objective truth. I'm gonna be thinking about this for a while, also you just can't go wrong opening and closing your movie with a David Bowie banger

Name: 28 Years Later: The Bone Temple
Review:
Hm. If I'm being honest, it’s a little disappointing coming off the sheer high that was the previous film. 

It used the character of Spike to take us on a journey of two halves, those being nihilism and optimism, hope, and love. Through his eyes, that film becomes more powerful as you see each of these halves bleed into each other; it's like you're experiencing this world through the eyes of a child trying to comprehend and make sense of a world which was shielded away from him.

This movie opts to tell you a very similar story; however, these two halves are very much told in isolation, and sadly, I just don't think they add much more to this that wasn't already explored in a more fascinating way in the previous movie.

This very much feels like the second installment in a trilogy; it feels a bit rushed and like it just amped up the intensity for the sake of being intense. 

I don't feel like I learned much about Jimmy and his gang, or as much as I would've liked for the amount of time that we spend with them. Spike is cast aside, which feels a bit like a misstep. He feels lost amongst the story. And in that half of the story, the person who I think is the most fascinating, Jimmy Ink, I needed a bit more to understand her turn, what was going on inside her head.

Where I think this movie shines is in the characterisation and exploration of Dr. Kelson, expertly portrayed by Ralph Fiennes. It feels, in a sense, like it is interested in further exploring the humanity in the infected, and it's doing so in a way that feels additional to the previous movie.

It's become very apparent in this franchise that the real plague infecting the world is humanity, and Jimmy represents that, but I don't think we interrogate that enough until we get to the best scene of the film, which is between him and Dr. Kelson. 

It was in this scene that I was reminded why this franchise is so wrought for discussion. It was also in this scene, though, that I found myself longing for the rest of the movie to bear some of this importance. 

It's interesting because I think they opted to tell a lot of the story through explicit violence and the perverse nature of humanity, while I do think that comes across and it's absolutely horrendous to watch, in a good way, I think I'm more interested in what makes the people tick instead of seeing explicit violence.

Not to mention this film also ends much like the first film with what feels like studio interference. I cannot believe that this is the second film in this trilogy that has a really poignant ending that is completely unravelled by an MCUification of cameos just to get people excited for more

Name: Frozen Fever
Review: Um why is the song in this so not good

Name: The Umbrellas of Cherbourg
Review:
Not saying anything new here by saying that this is one of the most gorgeous pieces of cinema I've ever seen put to screen, and I was lucky that I got to see it on the big screen. The colours, the vibrancy, the sets, the commitment to the almost dreamlike world that they live in - all colour in this world so beautifully.

I was unaware that this was a musical that involves singing the entire time, and while I think some of the musical pieces work incredibly well, and I'm very willing to admit that perhaps some of this does come down to translation, the fact that the musical goes on the entire time and the film is so lived-in and dramatic it makes some scenes feel heavier than they should be - like they're not moving with the same rhythm that the rest of the movie does.

This clears itself up by the third part of this movie, which is absolutely spectacular, a truly wonderful world that I could've stared at for hours longer, and a magnificent ending.

Name: No Other Choice
Review:
Hit me harddddd. As somebody who since graduating has been made redundant twice due to very ephemeral reasons, and since has thought A LOT about the plague that is capitalism and corporations.

Unsure I'm able to unpack this film on a single viewing. There's so much going on in here that it feels silly to make an assessment after only seeing it once; it has more going on than most films.

It's at once thrilling, funny, light and breezy. It's a romp that I struggle to see anybody not enjoying, but the undercurrent of this whole story is so deeply sad, and the film never lets that go. It's an absurd situation rooted so strongly in feelings, emotions and situations that are too relatable - and only becoming more so.

The film is both directly and indirectly tackling the forcing of AI into every part of our lives. Sure, in the film, job losses are due to the introduction of automation into their industry, but this permeates and ripples into every aspect of our lives - because for better or worse, work has become many people's lives.

With AI being introduced, not only are real humans losing livelihoods and meaning to machines, but those left are losing humanity surrounding them. These inventions, meant to be tools to assist workers, are instead becoming workers themselves.

The film interrogates this further though by making our character’s motivations and goals intangible. He wants back into this life he had, but why? He actually isn't able to verbalise this until he hears somebody else verbalise why. He becomes machine-like himself in his responses.

Why is he so hell-bent on getting back to the life he knew? Was his life that fulfilling and gratifying, or is the alternative of changing path and direction, doing something unknown, in a world that likes people to fit into moulds, more scary? Is having no goal, no thirst and need to grind his life away harder than having a goal he doesn't really care for?

Is there fulfilment he could find in opening a café, in changing his path, or will this invalidate his life that came before him? The chains of capitalism shackle him to his job, and he's unable to see life without it.

One of many powerful images in the film is the idea of a hug at the beginning of the film. We see him embrace in a hug with his family. He has it all: a good house, a good family, good food, love, all of the things that he should desire in our capitalist society. This contrasts so starkly with the hug towards the end of the movie that feels devoid of the things that make it naturalistic and real.

He may have got his job back, but what cost? He will spend his days in this factory, which is stark and barren and devoid of humanity. Where, meanwhile, at home, he's missing out on love, people, and music. And yeah, he's happy, or he's chosen to buy into the false promise of this happiness

There's just copious amounts of layers to this film. The idea that capitalism breeds people who are willing to throw other people under the bus just to serve their greater goals and ambitions. Our main character actually feels subhuman before all of the things he does.

This is to mention nothing of the technical work that is on display here. You will not find any better editing in a film. Park-Chan Wook is one of the most creative filmmakers working today and is enhancing and changing the medium and using it in dramatically different ways to other contemporaries. Wook's films really stand out due ot the modernity of it. Many directors have came out and said that they are unwilling to tell stories in modern times because they don't want to or they can't see a way in, and yet his work tells a thoroughly modern story through a thoroughly modern setting, and I think it just hits all that deeper. Gosh, yeah, what a movie insane that it has been snubbed for all of the awards shows.

Name: Two Sleepy People
Review:
I really admired this honestly. There's something so incredibly endearing about independent cinema and yes it's certainly has the feel and look of something low budget but also in the same thing it has the creativity and freshness to it that you only got from independent films.

Apparently, this was written within 14 days, which is just mental as there are some really profound lines about human relationships. The film has a lot of you to just be on board with the way that these characters are intrinsically verbose many will cite the obvious parallels between Baron and Woody Allen. However, I do think what is digging at is something worth exploring in this sense there sometimes relationships grew apart not because of the fault of either person but because people just inherently become incompatible.

There's a little to be desired around the main women's story, which is a real shame as I think Caroline Grossman is absolutely captivating in this. Nonetheless, though this is a gorgeous independent movie and I love the work that the people behind us are doing.

Name: Wonder Man
Review:
God, it's almost as if the collective masses crying for grounded, real stories, which didn't have a heap tonne of required reading to enjoy, were right. Who would've thought?

Wonder Man is a thoroughly refreshing, innovative, and delightful small-scale adventure taking place within the MCU. It's so grounded and detached from all of the large-scale events going on in this universe yet still feels a part of this world.

Something I think the MCU always gets right, no matter the quality of the project, is the characters it crafts; Wonder Man is no exception. However, here they strike the balance between an interesting person to root for but also adding a bit of murkiness to their character - they're fallible. There's no surprise looking at the shows which have done well; all of them contain complex characters and are more concerned with exploring the internal.

The buddy cop relationship that's formed between Simon and Trevor really is a delight to watch. It's funny in places. It's really odd in others; like this is a pairing that shouldn't work, they just do, and it's through this weird pairing that we get to explore them on a deeper level.

The show has so much going on, in the best way possible. It's concerned with acting, the falsehood of Hollywood, the selfishness and self-centredness that one believes they need to possess to make a name for themselves in the industry. It's about learning thatelationships are the most important cornerstone of our lives; without other people, we're nothing.

And something I've seen very little about, but I think this is very clearly allegorical for being closeted in Hollywood. There are numerous occasions where this pops up, and I like how that's thematically woven throughout the series.

There's heaps of creativity on offer here. It feels like a real show; that's the biggest compliment I can give it. There's an attention to craft and detail, shooting on location, making these characters feel lived-in in a short runtime; there's a ton of interesting choices. It's never bland to watch, I really, really enjoyed this. Please keep making more stuff like this, even if it's just dumped for some reason

There’s a degree of creativity on offer here that I really do appreciate, the campiness, the horror, the music fight, but I just don’t think it comes together that well, and that comes down to the worst element of this film - its script.

It’s boring, it’s tonally all over the place, it’s the most plain story they could tell, and outside of this film it destroys a character arc in Wanda, say what you want since it’s technically not a critique of the film but I do think with franchise storytelling it’s fair criticism to level. There’s still parts I like but I really struggle with this, probably since I want to love this

Review:
I’m gonna spare my words cause on this rewatch my video essay juices started to flow.

Maybe it’s my state of mind as of late but there’s a profound sadness to this that I never really noticed, the moments of shared dreams with your closest friends that most likely won’t come true but you still say them almost to will it into existence, even after so many times of life pulling you in other directions. The joy, sadness, perseverance, wonder, pain, pleasure, hate, love, it’s all in this tiny screenplay, I truly love this film

Name: Crime 101
Review:
Shockingly, I really really fucked with this. It’s not revolutionary, and it’s very easy to draw comparisons to other films of this ilk, but this is good in its own right.

Truly fascinating work from most of the actors here. Keoghan is doing his weird guy act yet again, of which he is too good at, Ruffalo is doing his whole “cop who desperately needs to solve the case” act, of which he is also always incredible at. Berry is fascinating to watch and always captivating whenever she’s on screen, however I was truly blown away by Hemsworth.

His performance here is so understated and very different for both him and just lead actors in general. The entire time he very rarely speaks, he rarely opens up, he remains this vessel of stress and pressure the entire time. This could’ve easily became quite boring or tiresome to watch but I was enthralled and it truly made the film maintain this high level of tension.

The film is slick and gorgeous, stunningly shot, scored to a wonderfully atmospheric score, and edited to near perfection - another way this could’ve been boring or drawn out but the edit keeps the pace.

Love how the film pushes people to their breaking point and feels like a exploration of where that is for each of them, the shackles of capitalism and money tying them to their situations and the sudden dawning that to achieve what they desire may be unachievable by following the law. 

Shocked but this was great. Also I had to watch this in 4DX and it weirdly was great? There was very little going on so it only started for the chase scenes and it was far more engaging than the seats just choosing to hit you every scene

Name: GOAT

Name: Wuthering Heights
Review:
Well. Here we go. For all the chatter and discourse, the wide spread of ratings and opinions (even amongst my mutuals, which is shocking), Wuthering Heights is finally out, and to my shock, it's pretty tame and really bland in parts.

I'm just going to get this out of the way: this isn't an accurate adaptation. Having seen so many adaptations in the past few days, none of them are, and nor would I want them to be. In my opinion, the point of adaptation is never to reproduce or replicate a story from one medium to another, and I find that desire incredibly boring. 

There's plenty of discussion to be had about the whitewashing of characters, the omission and race swapping of other characters, and the stark changes to the story, but for the purposes of this review, I will solely focus on the film presented.

When adapting to film, what I far prefer is somebody coming in with a distinct point of view and vision for the story they want to tell, and that Fennell does... well, kind of.

In her already incredibly controversial filmography, Fennell has presented such an eye for visuals, producing starkly unique images, images which provoke and challenge the audience and using the film medium to tell her story as much as dialogue and plot.

It really shouldn't be understated the technical craftsmanship on display in this film. I mean, seriously, this film is dripping in style. Fennell proves that she is more interested in making large-scale, high-budget stylish films than most of her contemporaries.

Having seen so many adaptations prior to this, it's so refreshing to see her vivid imagination burst on screen. She's not concerned with realism, a thing which plagues most modern blockbusters, and instead opts to make the most gorgeous film possible.

Sure, do the costumes and production design make sense for the time period? No, not at all, but it's not going for that. It's still using these elements to tell a story. The glossy pastel nature of Thrushcross Grange, filled with costumes of plastic, latex, and harsh block colours, symbolising the unnatural state of Cathy's life there, a life not filled with love. Rooms barren except for a few seats, rooms trapping her within her own skin, it is a phenomenal feat the design of this place.

Meanwhile, Wuthering Heights is a dark, bleak, but ultimately more real place, a place which could easily make monsters out of people. The nature between these places represents the time when they are both free from the shackles of their lives, a place where they are free to love.

The film follows in her stylistic footsteps of her previous endeavours by having a real master of sound. If there's a single aspect which understands the motivation of the novel, it has to be the delicate score and the soundtrack. The deep, harrowing strings reflecting the feelings of Cathy, the bursts into song when their love is on screen, it just works so well. Not to mention the soundtrack from Charli XCX, which is unbelievable, but that's its own thing.

At the end of the day, all of these technical elements could be brilliant, but if there's not a story there to match, then it's just not going to click, and this seems to be the aspect causing so much hate, but if I'm being honest, it's the part which confuses me the most. 

The screenplay for this movie is so sanded down to the point that it essentially becomes a typical romance movie with a dash of manipulation.

Judging it just on the film presented, it's kind of boring? What Fennell has decided to do is really dial in on the obsessive nature of their love, of how they misinterpret each other and how their shackles in their life, the positions they are born into and the positions they force upon themselves keep them from being together. There's a clear link here to sex and that being the reason that keeps them coming back to each other, something I think is really interesting. If the film was interesting in exploring this any further than a montage of sex, some fingers in mouth and some broken eggs.

If Fennell was going to go in this direction, it would've been nice if she went for it. If she followed on from Saltburn and really made this into the physco sexual romance that's being told to you. Yes, that's not Wuthering Heights, but it's this version. That novel deals with repression and here it's more obsession, but I fear it's just not all that interesting watching their romance.

There are great performances from Robbie and Elordi. I really liked Robbie and the film feels more so from her obsessive perspective and the regret and guilt she feels. But really when it comes down to it, it's quite a bland romance between characters that have very little going for them. Cathy gets quite a bit. I do like the intention behind really narrowing in on her as it feels like you witness her spiral more naturally, but Heathcliff is really not that interesting and when he returns the film kind of loses what they want to do with his character. He engages in BDSM with Isabella why?

There are threads of an interesting story here, linking sex and death like the intro to this film which goes nowhere, playing into the physco sexual realitonship between Cathy and Heathcliff since they were kids, the queering of Nelly and how that changes their relationship, nothing is exploring in much detail to the point it just feels like a very well-made standard romance. I truly am so shocked that this has provoked as large reactions as it has as I think it's pretty standard romance, I want Fennell to go full freak

Review: The best decision to have my first watch in a packed cinema, nothing beats leaving that room hearing multiple "wtf did I just watch" conversations and people saying they're gonna need explainers videos

Name: Apocalypse Now
Review: Kind of at a loss for words for what I just witnessed. So glad I held off to watch this in a cinema, might be the best looking film ever? Still processing the sheer scale of this, puts other war films to shame

Name: The Moment
Review:
Charli XCX is one of the biggest pop stars the world has right now, due to her meteoric rise thanks to her album brat and brat summer, it placed her at the height of her career, and at this height has decided to do something so left field, she has made a mockumentary about the subsequent tour following her album release, a film which perhaps presented itself as a look behind the curtain, as a glimpse into what her life was like, and while it might be these things, it’s also doing something way more interesting.

The moment is less so concerned with the minutiae of brat but that whole ecosystem provides a great launching pad for what is, at its core, quite a fundamental struggle that artists go through.

In a world so integrated and enmeshed with capitalism, what is the role of an artist? And furthermore, is it even possible for an artist to exist, to truly be making art their own unfiltered way, when they are burdened by the shackles of capitalism? If you're successful, and profiting from your work, your art, is it truly yours anymore, or has it been altered and modified to capture a wide enough audience.

There's a quote in the movie, and a quote which Charli has said herself surrounding brat, that "the world finally caught up to her". She hadn't really changed her output as an artist, instead brat was a result of culture intersecting with her. It was just the right moment.

This is the film's starting place. The harsh first few minutes of Charli going wild, with the maximalist flashing lights, and the 365 remix (godly hearing this in a cinema) blasting, this encapsulated brat - as it was intended.

Immediately, this is followed up by Charli being asked to perform this number again, and thus starts this shedding of art into commerce. It immediately provokes this question of performativity, was any of what we just watched real, is that who Charli is, is it what she wants brat to be, how much of her artistry is now being controlled and diluted?

The rest of the entirety of the film centres around this idea of brat getting completely lost, malformed and evaporated into the most commercially viable outcome, art so warped it no longer represented what it was in the first place, which ironically is what made it connect with people. It was authentic which in itself was unique.

The reason I say the film used brat as a launchpad is that the rest of the film doesn't really concern itself with reality. I'm sure some of these experiences happened, in some shape or form, but instead the film seeks to rip the idea of brat to shreds in order to explore its themes.

Loss is happening on two scales in the film, loss of self and loss of art. They’re occurring concurrently but in different ways. The idea of brat, which was so singular in the film, birthed from a human, has now entered the world and is at the world's whims. The artistic intent of that piece of art is free to be interpreted and malformed as the world sees fit. This idea of wanting to prolong the cultural zeitgeist as much as possible is deeply inhuman and antithetical to the art which was made.

It's the distillation of art into a product, into a brand, an identity, a movement, something which is able to be packaged up and sold. The film features a brat card, which plays a large factor in the film, but when it comes down to it, what does that mean? What is a brat card? Is brat a font and a colour scheme, is art able to be reduced that easily?

In the film, it's described as a way to get the fictional bank Howard Stirling to access a new demographic, which in a hilarious scene Charli's manager basically just admits it's gay people. Suddenly art which connected with people on a meaningful level is being sold to them, their identity is being weaponised against them to further the capitalist agenda of a corporation. What's hilarious is in real life, this was a card you could also purchase, and it sold out. Which I think only speaks to this idea of monitizing art further, that people insist on being part of a moment, rather than caring for the art itself.

It's insane how far removed this is from the original piece of art, in these scenes as well you see just how unwieldy the whole situation is, Charli has no say in this partnership, she randomly signs contracts which she doesn't read; the intent of an artist dissipates and is replaced by a room of executives and business people.

This clearly takes a toll on the perception of self, and it's this internal struggle between wanting success and the art to reach people, but losing the art in the process of doing this, of being a shell of who you are. Weirdly, I initially viewed Charli in this as kind of the weakest part; she feels very hollow, more of a vessel for ideas than a realised human person, but I think that’s the point.

When we first meet her, it's in this period of pure euphoria, of letting go, of dancing, and we never really see her return to this. The film interestingly doesn't show her perform again until the very end, where she has become an almost caricature of herself. Throughout the film, she seems drained and pulled in different directions by all of the voices around her; there are no real moments of rest in the film. Despite there being clear instances of rest.

It's in these periods of time, though, that when she's able to ruminate, and it seems like she very much has a one-track mind for the entirety of the movie, and that is just how can she keep this moment alive? That's why in her periods of rest, she isn't fully relaxed because her mind is consciously aware of the fact that if she stops, will all of this success stop around her?

For that, I think the film is very interesting. It is a single idea that is planted in her mind at the very start of the film, and it is the only thing that is able to come through in the decisions that she makes. There are small glimmers of respite, specifically when she's talking to her creative director, where the artistry of brat and the identity of self seemed to resurface, but all of that is washed away by the idea and the pressure of maintaining success and keeping the cultural moment that she managed to hold.

The film plays on this idea of the intersection between art and success and it's a very interesting parallel to view the film in the context of what actually happened in real life. What happened in the film didn't happen; it is an alternative universe in which she gave in completely to this idea of success and fame and being palatable and reaching a bigger audience.

In reality, she didn't do that; she remained authentic and did things her own way, as far as we know, but it's interesting seeing that these pressures clearly manifested in her life enough to want to creatively tell this story. Initially, I deemed it a little bit of a criticism that this film doesn't ever really tackle what brat is, what it really meant to her, why she was desperate to capture and prolong the success that she had; I just don't think that's what the film is. The film is able to abstract the idea of brat and instead it just becomes more realistically about art and the compromises that come with wanting to retain success.

These compromises manifest themselves implicitly in the film. Sure you're able to feel the album becoming more sanitised and corporate in real time, and Charli loses herself, but every time we move to a new location, we would get these flashes on the screens of the different locations in different fonts and stuff, and as the movie goes on, that devolves, and brands and names start getting inter spliced in which further just perpetuates the idea, that in this version of the story, she has lost control of what her art, and it has instead become something to be sold to people.

It’s why the speech at the end felt a little out of place because most of the film isn't concerned with what made brat special and why it connected with people, so to get this monologue with Charli detailing what that piece of art personally meant to her felt a little jarring.

However, using brat as a concept, as a way to just talk about the pure state of art, the art before it was touched and mishandled, when it was just about the piece of art and nothing more, this scene really shines. It is an artist coming to terms with the fact that they have lost their identity, they've acknowledged it, and they are saddened by it, but it is the reality that they're having to deal with.

It's deeply sad, but it's the acknowledgement that her art no longer holds the same meaning as it used to; the ending of the movie, while hilarious is also profoundly upsetting. It is somebody who has lost control completely of what they made, of a piece of art that meant something to them and instead is this corporate product which has been engineered to appeal to the most people.

I honestly found this really fascinating. I have my reservations here and there about the film, but I think it is so commendable for someone of her status to make something this unapologetically them, to tackle feelings and situations and ideas that very few people will have experienced and yet are still able to relate to on some level. 

We’re all being packaged and sold to people. People are selling things to us all of the time; it is hard to find authentic art that hasn't been burdened by capitalism in some way. To acknowledge that within a film, to show what that compromise and pressure results in, and then to compare that to the rollout we got... In an odd way the film then feels like an ode to creative liberties and trusting your vision, and for that I love it.

Name: Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die
Review:
Welp, completely on the opposite side of the consensus here. Found this to be messy, repetitive, and hollow, and honestly quite a slog to get through. I will admit, as somebody who has a lot of issues with Black Mirror, it was clear pretty quickly this wasn't going to be up my alley.

The film plays out like multiple Black Mirror episodes stitched together into a larger narrative, albeit a little more wacky and outlandish. The issues I have with that show, of trivialising issues with technology and packaging it in very unnuanced ways, crop up here time and time again.

This film actually feels like it has very little to say, so instead it proceeds to bash you over the head with the same simple messaging.

It's commendable in areas; I do like how crazy it gets, and the attempts it makes at humanising some of its characters, but on the whole, it's incredibly trite, long, and unfocused

Name: Wuthering Heights
Review:
First half: "hold up is this going to be incredible"
Second half: "oh wait it's ditched what made the story interesting"

Name: Dr. Strangelove or: How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love the Bomb
Review: Well. This felt strangely relevant. But then again, I wonder if the enduring power of this film is that it sadly will always feel relevant. The cinematic equivalent of a laugh followed by a deep pit of rage and sadness

Name: Hoppers
Review:
Solid Pixar fun. I don’t subscribe to the idea that Pixar have lost their spark as of late. Quite frankly their recent output has been great. I thought Elio was fantastic. So, with Hoppers, Pixar are not back, but rather continuing to make entertaining, emotional family films.

Really enjoyed how humorous the film is, it’s what sets it apart from a story we have seen done before. The characters are charming, and fun - despite not completely loving the animation style.

The film shines in its commentary on unity over division, shining a light on how collective action makes change, perhaps less so in its environmental themes - but perhaps I’m being harsh when they have a masterpiece in their catalogue that deals with similar themes.

Lotta fun, could’ve done with more of the side characters, but for a streamlined story, it had some good laughs, some classic Pixar cries and Alan Carr what more could you want from a film

Name: How to Make a Killing
Review:
What does this film want to be? Props to Glen Powell for continuing to just be effortlessly charming and charismatic, but that isn’t enough to elevate this film from the fundamental problem it has with its writing.

The film presents itself as an eat the rich story, but I’m not sure that’s what the film is. Powell’s character manages to accrue quite a bit of wealth and has himself a life of luxury at a point in the film, but that’s not enough for him. This is an interesting angle to take, to explore class differences and the pressure to always want more - but the film isn’t interested in this.

The other angle, being just a tale of pure revenge, could also be interesting on its own, but this falters too. Powell is nowhere near as bloodthirsty as he should be to convey this revenge to the audience; he kind of just placidly does these killings. There’s no internalising of what he did; this is a man who has never killed anybody before and seems relatively well-adjusted, but just never contends with the fact he’s killing people.

Then you have the Margaret Qualley factor, who just muddles the narrative even further. Making her character manipulate Powell into doing dirty deeds, it successfully makes you more sympathetic to Powell, but why? Are we really to feel sorry for him? The film ends on a note which makes it feel this way, but the entire film leading up to this just doesn’t make you feel that way.

This idea of wealth being a trap only seems to apply to Powell, and even then, is it… I really have no idea what this film was getting at, and sadly, the journey isn’t all that engaging. What a letdown. My main takeaway is I need more Jessica Henwick; she’s delightful and such a brilliant performer

Name: Two Prosecutors
Review:
What an interesting experience this was. The film is mind-numbingly slow. I'm all for slow cinema, have kind of fallen in love with it, but there needs to be a reason. Part of me wonders though if the "slowness" is the point. 

The film is a critique of the Soviet regime and how authoritarianism corrupts people. It's so dialogue-heavy, and exemplifies the slow, painstaking effort to enact change in a system so built against it.

The central protagonist encounters many hurdles on his journey to do something so simple; the film feels like it's trying to put us in that mindset of having to endure the slow procedures of wanting to make change, even the central character nods off at a point.

The film, when things start to feel like change might happen, speeds up. The pace of the film clearly feels intentional, but I just don't think it really gets much across other than pacing. The conversations, save for the last few when it picks up, are just really dull and uninteresting; you understand what the film is tackling very quickly, and the film doesn’t really seek to engage you in any other way or push that idea more.

When the film picks up a bit, I really was getting into the story, and then it abruptly stops, which again is most likely intentional, but it just angered me more. Which sure, argue that's the point of the film, that it successfully bored me then annoyed me but it feels like it needed to do more with it other than make me feel a few emotions. 

Also might literally be the funniest fucking ending when I turned to my friend at the end of the press screening and was like did they just do that because he's a virgin

Name: Frankenstein
Review: It's certainly a simplfied version of the story, and I fear that's what holds this back slightly, but for when this came out wow wow wow, this is lit better than most modern movies.

Name: Zero Dark Thirty
Review:
Pretty astounding. Bigelow's command behind the camera, paired with the best? working cinematographer in Greig Fraser, and an actress who should be in conversations for the best working today, Jessica Chastain.

Tense and unnerving, and flirts with the idea of endorsement and neutrality that I really appreciated. Chastain is playing a character who is really tough to get a read on, guarded, not personable,  markers of a character who wouldn't make a compelling lead, and yet that's the point. 

It's a monumental achievement from Chastain to be able to paint a one-track-minded individual with so much depth; the ending scene, this reckoning with what she's done, this overcoming of emotions she's suppressed, not only works for this stoic character we've been watching, but works as a really powerful note for the whole film.

The entire time you could argue it veers into patriotic victories, that the inhumanity and violence is somehow being justified, but that reckoning rings through the entirety of the film; if you win but compromised yourself along the way, did you really…

Name: The Bride!
Review:
Oh. No. Not sure what happened here, but The Bride! (Don't forget that cheeky exclamation mark -  which in hindsight feels very fitting for what this film ended up being) is as chopped up and stitched together as its lead longing for love.

I really do have to hand it to Maggie Gyllenhaal for committing to her vision. In an era where adaptations are feeling a little lifeless and made to fit corporate agendas of maximising the demographics of audiences, it's refreshing to see something so distinctly and uniquely her, even if that vision doesn't quite work.

The film feels fragmented and disjointed, which I fear has come from the reshoots and edits this movie went through, but then again who knows, because I'm unsure the bones of this film are that compelling.

There are disparate threads here that go nowhere, like the Bride inspiring women to be violent and paint their faces? À la Joker. Which, speaking of, the comparison has been made, but it's apt. This truly feels like a spiritual sibling to the Joker movies.

The film feels a little muddled in its feminist ideas, exemplified heavily in Penelope Cruz's story. It just feels so in line with the "girlboss feminism" era. The Bride herself doesn't feel like this liberated character free from the oppression of men. The delivery of calling herself not "the bride of Frankenstein", just "the bride", is beyond eye-rolling. Where is the idea of a bride coming from if not attached to a man? The film is just very confused in its feminist ideas. Mix in the multiple personalities and the framing of Mary Shelley, and I just thought the characterisation of her was a mess.

Sadly, I couldn't say much for the performances either. Bale was fine. Buckley, I think, does her best with a really poorly written . The biggest shock, though, is that this film is so dull. It meanders and just plods along. There doesn't feel like a cohesive storyline, or rather, a throughline, which is that interesting. What a disappointment, sadly.

Name: Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man
Review:
Massive letdown. Glad people are getting something out of this, but I found this to be stale, predictable, and lacking the depth which the show was full of.

This comes from the dissonance in how Peaky Blinders tells a story. It’s fine to be methodical, and more drawn out with Tommy, after all we have had seasons of build-up with him - though if I'm honest I'm not sure him sulking around and musing about ghosts is all that interesting.

But most of the characters in the film are new, or characters who weren't explored much in the show, and this pacing, this way of characterisation just doesn’t work for them, it ends up making them feel incredibly flat.

Barry Keoghan's Duke is a heightened exaggeration that just doesn't fit properly in this world, part of this is his performance too, and Tim Roth's villain is the most one-dimensional villain who is just plain uninteresting, especially compared to the show.

Really not sure the purpose of this film, filled with new ideas and characters which aren't explored at anywhere near the same depth as the show, and yet this is the send-off for that world. Very odd, and very disappointing sadly

Name: Charli XCX: Alone Together
Review: Unbelievably fascinating as both an insight to this insane endeavour, but also as a time capsule to this weird time we all went through. Really wasn't in love with the presentation, and editing of this as a doc, but interesting still

Name: Reminders of Him
Review:
Ladies and gents she’s done it again. Shoutout also to the most irritating foley work I’ve ever heard, BOOTS SHOULDN’T BE THAT LOUD.

lowkey did start tearing up at the end so…

Name: Bride of Frankenstein
Review:
Kind of major in its feminism, considering it was the 1930s! Yes a film from the 1930s expressed the sentiment that a woman born with the sole purpose to be the bride to a man, actually has agency and a say in their own life.

I’m dumbfounded this was made when it was, it’s gorgeous, the sets, the lighting - bring back gaffers we need you! - and the great VFX. Loved this, tackles the different sides of Frankenstein that most adaptations fail to do so

Name: Arco
Review: Gorgeous film which floored me by the end of it, stunning animation, wholly unique and quite a lovely mediation on how capitalism has corroded our relationships and way of life and that human connection is the most important thing we have

Name: Little Amélie or the Character of Rain
Review:
What a gift, and a burden, to see the world through the eyes of a child. To experience the beauty and sadness that come with being a human for the first time. To sit with the joys, love, tears, and pain that come with living.

Gorgeously realised film that has a commanding voice in its innocence. It captures that childlike wonder so vividly but feels distinctly adult at the same time. Maybe that’s the point: to both realise how much a child notices and feels, but also to find a way back to feeling all of our emotions, experiencing it all through the eyes of a child, realising we’re not able to control everything but not letting that shut ourselves off from people

Name: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You
Review:
Completely harrowing, haunting, oppressive and yet so unbelievably human. The older I get, the more I truly don’t understand how a parent copes and deals with the immense pressures and stress that come with raising a child.

This stress is amplified tenfold in the film, with a number of disasters going wrong - something that easily could come off as feeling too convenient for a high stress story, but it feels so deeply real. This intensity is only further enhanced by the inescapable performance of Rose Byrne.

The camera is unrelenting in its proximity to her, most of the film simply medium-close ups of Rose’s face, which just enforces this stress she’s going through onto you. It’s not a pleasant watch at all but that’s the point.

Byrne is a force is this film, the stress she’s able to evoke is monumental, however more than this she’s able to convey this idea of a parent holding it together for her child, her stress oscillates between the fore and background in this really profound way.

It all culminates in an incredibly powerful ending, a thesis statement on the hardships facing a parent and the enduring spirit they have to have less they be taken by the sea

Name: The Red Shoes
Review:
The blueprint for many of my favourite films, so it's no wonder I devoured this as if I had been starved of good films. Simply breathtaking and really brings to mind questions, as cliché as it sounds, like "why don't they make them like this anymore?"

It's kind of unfathomable how this was made in the 1940s. The 1940s?!? The central sequence where we watch the performance of The Red Shoes is a contender for my favourite sequences in cinema. Sounds hyperbolic but it truly is that good. What I noticed here is a flirtation with magical realism; we break the confines of the theatre so many times, we use the medium of film to tell a story only film could - something modern musicals could learn from.

Kind of in love with how simple the story is, set up three interesting, compelling, complex characters who have desires, dreams, ambitions, and let them loose. Moira Shearer's performance as the dazzling Victoria Page is truly mesmerising, and incredibly refreshing from a feminist lens given the time period too.

Anton Walbrook's role as Boris Lermontov is the key to this working as well as it does. It's not unlike other roles which are seen in cinema, the devoted mentor who pushes his prodigies to their limits, but often these roles sand down the complexities of them and smooth them to make them almost characters who are needlessly cruel; there was a depth and an understanding to him that I really appreciated. That or I might have a fucked-up idea of mentorship.

Truly have no idea how this was made but so thankful it exists.

Name: Weapons
Review:
It's fun enough, more interesting when it concerns itself with investigating the effects of trauma on individuals, and the hidden weights some children bear, than when it's a full on horror.

I will say, not to be the woke friend, but it's kind of baffling to me that they reserved the most gruesome kill for the queer couple. The kills in the film are all incredibly methodical and serve the villain's greater plan, except this. It felt very exploitative, and simply there as shock value. Interesting when viewed as part of queer character's representation in history, not a flaw or criticism of the film just strange

Name: Casablanca
Review: Jeez. Kind of scary the relevance that this still holds and insane when you realise this was released during the war. They had no idea who would win. Just a timeless classic, and for good reason, funny, political, and heart-wrenching

Name: The Revenant
Review: Witnessed this in IMAX as it was intended, I simply have no idea how this film was made, and this continues my streak of entering a movie not really excited to watch Leo and getting reminded why he's so good

Name: The Lego Movie
Review:
Me: Wow, who would've thought that a film based on a literal brick would have such prolific commentary about the subservient nature of the masses, how authoritarian governments rise, the power of collective action, and how society is structured to serve the few at the top.

Also me: Everything is awesome

Name: Pink Narcissus
Review:
So... I was invited to a nice press screening of the re-release of this film, with no real knowledge of what it was. There's something weird in watching, what is essentially 1970s soft core gay porn, in a crowd. I didn't have watching a penis cum into camera with a bunch of film critics on my plan for today but here we are.

The film is mesmerising as it wanders and floats between these vingettes of love, lust and desire. Who are we when we love others, when we engage in love though we don't feel it, when we're perceived only as sexual objects, who do we find in our own eyes.

Name: Resurrection
Review:
To search for explicit meaning in Resurrection is to completely misunderstand the film. Ultimately, there are small, blink-and-you’ll-miss-it lines which explain the narrative set up for the occurring dream, but that almost doesn’t matter.

The film is a celebration of the cinematic, the wondrous world of film. This medium so full of life and meaning that it transcends time. It feels as though Bi Gan has poured his heart out into his hands, begging you to see what he sees in this magical medium. And you do; it's hard not to be moved, both on a narrative level, but more so from the sheer cinematic scale of this. It’s filmmaking at its finest; this is what makes the art of cinema so special.

Name: Lady Champagne
Review:
BFI Flare Film #1

An elite way to start off the festival, in a crowd full of drag queens. Part of the reason I love doing festivals like this is going into films with absolutely no idea what the film is going to be, and nothing was more rewarding than the experience I got from doing that with this movie.

Lady Champagne is in the vein of spoof comedies that came before a la The Naked Gun, but ramp up the camp and just flat-out gay (complimentary) energy. It’s an absolutely silly, stupid movie that's low-budget and barebones production is part of its charm.

I think something that encapsulates this movie very well is what the director said introducing it, she said that one bad joke is a bad joke. 100 bad jokes is Lady Champagne. It works so well in a crowd that is along for the ride and invests itself in the story, an elite viewing experience that I won't forget

Name: Perro perro
Review:
BFI Flare Film #2

I was profoundly moved by this simple, albeit weird little film. The film’s use of a human male playing a dog, this anthropomorphisation of an animal, leads to such interesting commentary on gender, how we treat our pets, and also our relationships as humans.

I found myself deeply moved by the story, a story which I don't think has anything definitive to say yet still managed to be absurdly thought-provoking. Perhaps this is where the film might lose some people, as it does feel very much like this odd fable is presented to you, and you are supposed to take away whatever you can from it. It's not dictating anything to you, but I really adored this, it allowed room for the story to flow in and out of different modes and explore a wide variety of things using this very simple premise.

Juan Ramos, who plays this anthropomorphic dog, his performance is absolutely captivating. He really bears it all, in more ways than one, ha ha, but towards the end of the film, you do believe that this man is a humanisation of a dog. His longing stares, his non-verbal performance - it's all contained within his face, in his body language, and I was enamoured by his screen presence.

A lovely movie that really expands on the idea of what a queer film can be, and it's using queerness to explore so much about human nature, which I just loved.

Name: Uchronia
Review:
BFI Flare Film #3

Monumental film that is unlike anything I've ever seen before. Interesting that its subjects are queer, but that also could be said for the form. The entire film’s narrative structure, its blending and re-shaping of history, feels inherently queer. I can only imagine the amount of research I had to go into a project like this because it is so densely packed, I fear the only people who are going to get the most out of this are people who are academics who have studied queer history.

Interestingly though, I do wonder if that matters; if instead the film is proposing this idea of queerness running throughout history, the plights and struggles have always been the same and we're always fighting towards the same goals. Definitely a thought-provoking watch. Admittedly, I didn't understand or fully grasp all of it, but I think that will be a pretty standard experience of this film

Name: Washed Up
Review:
BFI Flare Film #4

Incredibly charming and makes up for what it lacks in filmmaking with heart. You can definitely tell that this was a low-budget film, but interestingly that adds to the endearing and cute setting of this small fishing town.

I understand why people get a lot out of this. It's definitely something that's missing in the queer space. I did have some minor complaints with it. The acting is shaky in places. The sound mixing was off. Narratively it feels a little predictable. We don't really explore the internal lives of these characters enough, but still a nice, charming film.

Name: Out Laws
Review:
BFI Flare Film #5

Absolutely fascinating doc. My lord, the work that some people do unnoticed which ripples to impact queer people's freedom is incredible. It was again a wake-up call to the struggles queer people have in other countries around the world. I do wish we dug a little deeper into the subjects of the doc, but nonetheless very moving

Name: To Dance is to Resist
Review:
BFI Flare Film #6

Interesting subjects of a doc, do not make an interesting doc. The aimless nature of this documentary, the lack of narrative threads, and just the idea of dance being used as the primary force were not enough here. You need more; you need to guide your viewer on why they should feel compelled and moved by these dancers.

If we are going to use this doc to simply spend time with these people, then it needs to be structured in a way that we know who they are; if not, we get random sequences of them shopping or talking about things unrelated to the idea of the doc that just feel out of place.

Just fell completely flat; was not a fan of how the filmmakers decided to draw comparisons in the film between a piece they did and a tragedy that happened, but in the credits, they said that this piece was not made as a response to that event, that they were drawing comparisons for artistic purposes. That is not clear in the film, and it feels disingenuous. It feels this way because a lot of the film felt a bit like people who have the liberties to continue to make art in a time where many aren’t, and their art actually wasn’t a response to the atrocities; it was just a group continuing their work. 

I found the film to float between these beautiful dance sequences, and then just the most random pockets of their lives. I didn't learn much about them, I didn't understand how they felt, why they felt like they needed to dance, even tackle the feelings they have of continuing to be creative in a time where many couldn't. Incredibly surface level doc which could've been good

Name: ìfé: (The Sequel)
Review:
BFI Flare Film #7

I’m so sorry, but I was just beyond bored with this. It is the queer story you’ve seen a thousand times, this time with more infidelity. The edit was horrible in places, with repeated lines, audio not synced with video, and Foley work that just does not fit in the film. The relationship between the leads is not convincing; it’s very rough around the edges. I didn’t get much out of this at all. So funny that dramatic reveal had tiktok like sound effects playing

Name: Ready or Not 2: Here I Come
Review: You know what... I had fun. It's the same as the first movie; it's a cash grab, but it was fun. The movie is only as good as it is because of Samara Weaving. The raw, unfiltered rage, anger, and pain that she has throughout the entire movie is a delight to watch. The sister dynamic is also pretty fun and added a slight twist to what was essentially the same plot. I do think that in making the plot larger, it loses the intensity that the first film had, but it's a very easy, fun watch.

Name: Impure Nuns
Review:
BFI Flare Film #8

What an experience getting to see this with a packed crowd. This film is a forgotten piece of queer film history, a radical piece from 1950s Japan. I could wax lyrical about the history of this film, which I was lucky enough to learn from somebody who found this film in Japan and brought it to the festival, its place in history and its radical nature, but honestly the film is equally fascinating viewed through a modern lens.

The film is a camp masterpiece, its subtle cues, its hilarious script and its wonderful performances, but it also harbours something incredibly dark and twisted. It manages to balance these heavy events with this light-hearted campiness so well, in a way the laughter and silliness breaks down your walls to make these darker moments hit even harder.

Real treat of a movie, perfect for a crowd of queer people, give this distribution or more festival runs as I’m sure it will be a hit with people

Name: Satisfaction
Review:
BFI Flare Film #9

Loved this methodical, powerful, slowburn of a film, this deeply personal story which has been brought to the screen. The film is hauntingly uncomfortable, made even more so because of how the story eases you into this horrible event that happens.

Powerful performances that really rely on subtlety, given that a large portion of this movie is non-verbal. Gorgeous cinematography thanks to the setting as well, and some impactful sound design, especially when the movie is non-verbal and has no score. The film uses the waves and the water around them to score the movie in a way, and it's just brilliant. 

Really fascinating as an experiment on the audeince as well, to play with our feelings and perceived versions of characters we've seen, to then obliterate that perception and have to contend with the aftermath, it leaves you really feeling like this betrayal has happened to you and recontexualises the rest of the film very intentionally

Name: Bearcave
Review:
BFI Flare Film #10

Gorgeous, gorgeous film. God, Greece is so stunning. Love a good slow burn relationship; found this quite refreshing from a queer perspective that it wasn't unrequited love but a couple in love with each other just unable to communicate this. Phenomenal performances, especially for first-time features. Very simple, and moving story

Name: The Little Sister
Review:
BFI Flare Film #11

Surprisingly hilarious, it's no wonder Nadia Melliti won the best actress award at Cannes because she is just outstanding here. The film is essentially an acting showcase for her, the camera almost invasive in moments, really just holding on to her, but in doing so, you get such a profoundly moving film about acceptance of self

Name: Chicago
Review: Oh what a journey I've been on with Chicago, from not enjoying it to seeing it in a cinema. Please modern musical movie directors watch this, you are allowed to be creative with your musical scenes

Name: Singin' in the Rain
Review: Grown man sitting next to me in the cinema, who I presume was also his first time watching this, turns to me at the end after we were both crying and just says "what a film". That sums this up perfectly. The people yearn for earnest musicals!!

Name: PlayTime
Review: For some reason I went into this thinking it was a horror not basically the french Naked Gun. The restaurant sequence is generational

Name: Interstellar
Review:
Saw this in the Royal Albert Hall, scored by the London Symphony Orchestra… like Zendaya says at the end of Challengers, COME ON.

Words really will not do this experience justice. I had full body shivers multiple times, full on sobbed at the sheer force of the music in the venue.

Will forever remain one of my favourite screenplays, haters be damned!!! Maybe love is the answer, maybe it is this monumental force which transcends time, maybe it’s a force which is inexplainable, which defies reason and maybe that’s why it’s the answer to everything.

There’s a moment in the film after the tragedy on Miller’s Planet, where they must choose their next destination. Brand wants to go to the planet her love is on, while Cooper chooses the “logical” destination. To dismiss Brand as only acting out of love and to not apply the same logic to Cooper is silly. Cooper’s solution is perhaps the most logical, but in this instance he’s also acting out of love, going here guarantees less time slipping and him going home.

In this decision they’ve both been drastically changed by what they witnessed on the planet and the repercussions that had. They’re both acting out of love. It’s what I love about this film!!! It’s heady and dense and larger than life, but it’s just so deeply human, it’s so rooted in our desires, our dreams, our fears.

To speak on Zimmer’s score for just a second, it might be his magnum opus. It encapsulates this film so beautifully. This idea of time being the enemy, time being this unbeatable force, forever marching forward and yet those moments of time ticking are still able to be filled with love.

Ughhhhhhhh this movie is loved for a reason. Thank you Nolan

Name: The Magic Faraway Tree
Review: I'm glad children's media is being made but could we ask for more? The whimsy and magic of the other world is lost, given the real world doesn't feel real. Lots of phoned in performances and not great visuals. Really didn't love this sorry

Name: Everything Everywhere All at Once
Review:
You think I'm weak don't you?

All of those years ago when we first fell in love, your father would say I was too sweet for my own good. Maybe he was right.

Please! Please! Can we... can we just stop fighting?

You tell me it's a cruel world and we're all running around in circles. I know that. I've been on this earth just as many days as you.

I know you are all fighting because you are scared and confused. I'm confused too. All day, I don't know what the heck is going on. But somehow, this feels like it's all my fault.

When I choose to see the good side of things, I'm not being naive. It is strategic and necessary. It's how I've learned to survive through everything.

I don't know. The only thing I do know... is that we have to be kind. Please, be kind - especially when we don't know what's going on.

I know you see yourself as a fighter. Well, I see myself as one too. This is how I fight.

The most I've ever resonanted with a piece of screenwriting

Name: The Super Mario Galaxy Movie
Review:
Genuine question: what constitutes a film?

This is an hour and a half of references, easter eggs, noises and colours made at such a breakneck speed that I was bored? Plot and cohesive storytelling? Who needs that nonsense when you have an obscure villain in the background of a frame for a split second.

To credit the artists involved, there is still an art in weaving these  together, but I'm not a fan of reverse engineering a story to fit the most references into a movie. Seems to be working for many though, so BRING ON THE SLOP

Name: Project Hail Mary
Review:
It’s no wonder Project Hail Mary is taking the world by storm. It almost feels engineered to be the most crowd pleasing film possible.

The most charismatic, charming leading man, Ryan Gosling ✅
A cute companion made to pull on your heartstrings ✅
Script by Lord & Miller with a killer comedy hit ratio ✅
Visuals by the greatest living cinematographer ✅

It’s all culminating and coalescing into a film which is primed to be appealing to the masses. Not to diminish the artistic intent, as “mass appeal” has kind of been sullied.

I do think what it leaves, in favour of mass appeal, is any real interrogation of its lead. Gosling is so compelling and Ryland Grace is genuinely such a fun protagonist, his relationship with Rocky a delight, but there’s little interiority to him. It’s not the biggest issue in the world, but it does leave him feeling a bit thin.

This really becomes a problem given that the narrative of the film is predicated on this idea that Ryland doesn’t remember his past, and he slowly recalls who he is, but this doesn’t seem to affect him ever. He never contends with his past; this is especially odd when it’s revealed how he ended up on the mission. There’s no reckoning with this reveal, which is both odd in terms of character but also given that the narrative builds up to this moment.

It’s a strange narrative decision to write Ryland in this way, and as much as I like him, and Gosling’s endless charm, I seek a bit more out of my characters, more to chew on and process. Still as a cinema experience this was sublime

Name: Pizza Movie
Review: Went in for a stoner comedy. Got a stoner comedy. Tell me why tf I popped for the Goldbergs guy though and why is he lowkey good

Name: Challengers
Review:
Religious to see this back on the big screen.

To the man who was on my right, if you happen to be reading this. It's ok to watch boys kiss. We don't need to be whispering "WhAt ThE fUcK" under our breath every time there's an allusion to some gay shit or we see a male butt. The cinema gets it, you are straight! In fact, repeating this every single time, as though the goriest, most gruesome horror was in front of you, might suggest the opposite... and if that's true, then I hope you revisit this film and see it for all it's wonderful, horny perfection

Name: Citizen Kane
Review: Kind of nuts, for the time it was made. It took a while but I really got into this, until the last shot... like really?!?

Name: Amblin'
Review: The very early seeds of Spielberg are laid here. Confident, assured and a cute story. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg

Name: Something Evil
Review: A very bog standard television movie, which is hard not to get disappointed at given how stellar Duel is. Still it's apparent the themes and idea Spielberg would later be known for, were planted and developed early on Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 3

Name: Duel
Review: So far removed from the Spielberg that I know... but also feels distinctly him. Such a wildly simple premise, directed with such vigour and passion it's hard not to be completely engrossed by this road rage fuelled flick Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 2

Name: Savage
Review: Sure, it gets bogged down in places and people and things which aren't particularly interesting, but at its core this could be something interesting. The political corruption that is so commonplace, and the media trying to expose it. It wraps up a little neat for my liking, whether or not that's just a result of modern news reporting I'm not sure Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 4

Name: The Sugarland Express
Review: It's so well crafted, and you feel in such safe hands watching this. No wonder this Spielberg went on to do some more directing Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 5

Name: California Schemin'
Review:
McAvoy has the sauce as a director as well. This does feel like a standard biopic affair, but it's confidently directed, buzzing with Scottish energy and electric performances from it's leads. It just warms my heart to see a Scottish story like this, a tale of some lads from working class backgrounds, on the big screen and getting a proper distribution.

The film has an added layer of thematic relevance with its discussion around accents, which was handled really tactfully, and nails the feelings of a lot of Scots. Perhaps the film could've pushed this idea further of if you "make it" but not being your authentic self, did you really? Is it better to be yourself and never make it or sell yourself to make it big? But I had a lot of fun

Name: Empire Records
Review:
Packed cinema. The best way to experience this for the first time. This cult classic has became a massive part of people's lives for a reason. The room was electric, with people clearly knowing the beats of the film.

This genre of film is missing from modern day cinema, which is a real shame. However, this did stir questions in me as why? Is it simply because this films don't appeal to people, or is it emblematic of a larger systemic issue with Gen Z and cost of living, that this environments no longer exist? I will spare you Letterboxd from the spiral I went on.

The film itself has cemented a place in my comfort films. Brilliant characters and relationships, all given room to breath in such a short runtime. The script flows between people so wonderfully, it captures this strange messy period in people's lives so well. 

Really could gush so much about this, the costuming (A.J I need that cardigan), the lived in set, the jokes, the rawness, the music!! It's horny, silly, funny, and weird, this perfect snapshot of growing older

Name: La La Land
Review:
I will always be an advocate for the theatrical experience. 

This is my third country seeing this in the cinema. Each time it's marked different points in my life.

The first, I distinctly remember being scared of this film. Sounds strange because I loved the film, truly adored it, but that was the problem. I held in my hands a brochure with reviews from numerous outlets, one of which was "Gay Times". They gave it five-stars.

It didn't matter that hundreds of other publications gave it glowing reviews, it mattered that they did. It signalled to my dumb, naive teenage brain that I enjoyed this piece of art simply because of my sexuality. Something I didn't quite understand, or want to understand at this point in time.

Now, I could write essays on the intersectionality of sexuality and the media people consume. In short: it doesn't matter?

I say this hesitantly because I don't fully agree; there's some art that speaks to us more based on our own lived experiences, but to be a good consumer of art, to appreciate what an artist is trying to say, simply requires one thing, an open mind.

Being open to the art that is being presented to you and fully embracing it, yes, bring your lived experiences to it, bring your thoughts, and ideas, fears, and hopes, but most of all bring empathy, be open to hearing other people's stories. When it comes down to it, a good movie is just a good movie.

Maybe slightly off topic from La La Land, but that first viewing sent me on this journey, to be more open to art - to be more open to myself - to let go of this fear that being queer meant having a diminished or different opinion on the art I watch and my opinions of it.

The second theatrical experience I had with the film was in a small cinema in Paris. I lived in Paris for a month - though didn't dive into the Seine sadly. I did this to escape what was becoming a constricting environment; it was COVID, I was attending university, and was living life trapped between walls.

It was a really pivotal point for me that if I chose to write about it here, this would end up being thousands of words long. But I had time for introspection, to really embrace and learn who I am.

So I found myself in the middle of a hot summer day in July, in Paris, in a tiny indie cinema watching this film which had grown to mean so much to me, this film which was a marker for a point in time which seemed so far from how I was feeling now.

Since then... you know a bunch of stuff. You have experiences. You learn from them. You grow up, you learn about the world and the choices that befall any human who is writing their story.

In the pocket between cinema watches, it's clear to me now that passion is what matters in life. Passion lights up a room, passion is what makes us unique, passion could change your life if you manage to keep that love and passion despite the world's efforts to knock it out of you.

So that brings me to the Prince Charles cinema in London, a place where I've found a slice of home. Not because it reminds me of home in any sort of way, but that feeling of passion, that love for art and for life can be felt in those little tiny rooms full of strangers.

There's something magical about being moved by a piece of art, but to share that experience. To be sitting next to a complete stranger sobbing at the lingering glances that grace the screen at the end of this film. Both appreciating the story being told, perhaps bringing lived experiences to the story too, to be moved by the passion and love on screen, nothing beats it. Here's to the ones who dream.

Review: Gosh, what a difficult subject matter that the film does a good job of coneying to its audencie in a cinematic way. It perhaps a little to the poitn and could've fleshed out its characters more but moving
