Name: Moulin Rouge!
Review: I'm so sorry this just wasn't it. I appreciate the artistry of Baz Lurhman but it just doesn't always click. This was incredibly messy and the songs...

Name: You, Me & Tuscany
Review: I'm gonna pretend that that little The Devil Wears Prada easter egg was put in there for me. Cute movie, looks awful most of the time, the leads have little chemistry, but I had fun! I do miss when theatrical rom coms were good however. I need leads with chemistry!!!

Name: The Station Agent
Review: Mystery movie at the PCC. Delightful film which just wonderfully explores really complex individuals. Maybe my favourite Bobby Cannavale performance ever. He's so lovable in this but brings a lot of depth to what could've been a very shallow person

Name: The Worst Person in the World
Review: So many scenes of this film are burned into my brain. Simply gorgeous

Name: Eraserhead
Review: I think I watched this the way Lynch intended, really fucking tired so the entire thing felt like a waking nightmare. I really thought I was onto something in the first few minutes too when it dawned on me "this is about having a child!" little did I realise that as the film went on, I wasn't a genius who understood Lynch, I just have basic literacy skills

Name: Oslo, August 31st
Review:
Gosh. Trier really is just so masterful at writing the human condition. There's a moment in this film, an incredibly quiet scene that almost passes you by, where the lead character is just observing people and their conversations. It's deceptively simple, but it is so powerful.

This small, single scene encompasses this entire film. The complexities that exist within us all. The desire to be heard and seen, to feel love and joy, to know that somebody who really cares is with you. When life is absent of these things, it can feel incredibly lonely. You could be in crowded rooms, surrounded by people, but feel unnoticed and unloved.

Honestly, this hit at the right time. I've been thinking a lot about loneliness and how horrible it is, how debilitating it is, and how much damage it does to people. It makes me so sad in a world that should be more connected; there are people who drift through without feeling like they were truly seen and heard.

Name: Mother Mary
Review:
A cinematic pop song.

What I love about pop as a genre is its ability, often to take something dark, uncomfortable, and buried and spin it into something joyous, uplifting, and danceable. It doesn’t negate the deep, complex feelings which are baked into the songs; those emotions and feelings aren’t suddenly gone, they coexist.

It’s this tension which makes pop music so transfixing. In one moment, elation; in another, tears.

This film is precisely that; it’s trying to make peace, make art out of something dirty, something buried deep inside, longing to be let out and take on new meaning.

Pain, guilt, and sadness left untouched can fester and grow into something haunting, but expelling that darkness, letting it out into the light, might just make something beautiful.

Lowrey has such a strong sense of direction in how he wants to tell this story; in lesser hands, this could’ve really not worked, but, at least in my opinion, this is something special.

The dialogue may be verbose and performative, but it’s so grounded in the stellar performances. Never once did I not believe Coel and Hathaway as they lament to each other about their pains. 

Specifically, Coel has this air of elitism in the way she speaks down to Hathaway, but this feels completely intentional, a wall to protect her. Words, the amour, to shield her from being exposed and vulnerable, a fear of a time when she got hurt.

The bold choice to linger in intimacy for extended periods of time, and slowly expand that out before closing it right back in and focusing on the relationship. The film flows expertly and knows how to pull you into this relationship. Is it important to know the details of their past? I don’t think so. This abstraction, in my opinion, favours this ethereal quality the film possesses.

Spectacular, transfixing performances, specifically Coel, absolutely phenomenal set design and direction in telling a large story through unique visuals, great sonic storytelling, and a hauntingly beautiful core relationship, working in harmony to make something gorgeous.

Name: Michael
Review:
Painfully bland. Not to be hyperbolic, but perhaps the worst musical biopic put to screen.

Is this because it does something offensively bad? Not particularly. But it’s because of whose story that’s being told.

You’re telling me, perhaps the most influential, biggest musician to ever live, gets a film like this? A film which falls into the same trappings as every other modern musical biopic.

There’s no tension, no depth. It’s a Wikipedia page summary of his life with no attempt to dig into Michael.

Not a single character in this gets any depth, it’s the most one dimensional flat representation of who these people are. It’s chocked full of montages set to Michael’s songs to keep your interest in the film, but remove the songs and it’s painfully boring.

The concert sequences as well just look lifeless. It’s the most rapid fire glimpse of who this man was, and while the performance is great, if you really want to understand why this man was special, go watch him perform.

The film is more concerned with preserving his legacy than interrogating it.

Name: Speed Racer
Review:
Completely singular in its vision, there will likely never be a film like this again, and for that alone this should be commended.

There’s a lot to love here, the strong fervent anti capitalist themes which only ring more true, the family bonds which are done to perfection but I find myself enchanted by the visual effects here.

They’re, at least from an objective standpoint, bad, however I find them utterly captivating. It is a concrete example of how committing to a vision and having the  guts to go all in, is far more artistically rewarding, than only doing what you’re able to. I find this endlessly more captivating than the, so called good, VFX in modern blockbusters which is often just grey slop. They build an entire world, and because they believed in it so much, you end up believing it too.

Endlessly fun, please let’s bring back colourful movies!!

Name: Close Encounters of the Third Kind
Review: The masculine urge to abandon your family, destroy your home, kiss a random woman and then head on a ship with a bunch of little aliens who speak in the language of John Williams Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 7

Name: It's Such a Beautiful Day
Review: I don’t quite have the words to articulate how moved I was by this. It’s a testament to the power of creative vision. Form is simply the way an artist expresses themselves, when you have vision anything could be meaningful Watching the Letterboxd Top 100: No. 100

Name: Beau Travail
Review:
I fear I will need to revisit this, I just know I wasn’t in the right mindset. There’s a skill on display here, which very few directors are able to articulate to the screen, of showing the foils and pitfalls of masculinity but doing so in a very bittersweet way, a way that is removed from the harsh portrayal of masculinity. There is a juxtaposition on display that Denis exploits to showcase the corrosion of male ideals and the harm masculinity has on our relationships, the embrace of men, not from love but from obligation. Really powerful filmmaking.

I really struggled to grasp onto the story, as much as I enjoyed what it had to say, I wanted a better in. That’s not necessarily the fault of the film, perhaps my expectations, as by the end it becomes clear that the story has been unfolding in front of you the entire film

I don’t quite have the words to articulate how moved I was by this. It’s a testament to the power of creative vision. Form is simply the way an artist expresses themselves, when you have vision anything could be meaningful

Name: The Misfits
Review: Plagued by some mysogyny which dampened my experience of the film. I do appreciate where this film ends up; it's just about broken people stumbling their way through life. Was the film ever about their relationships to each other, or was that just a disguise for the deeply seeded cracked nature of their souls? First Munroe film too and it's clear why she was such a powerhouse

Name: The Drama
Review:
Many many many thoughts, but perhaps the most pressing is how unsurprising the “twist” is, and what that perhaps says about the state of America and its handling of gun violence.

No doubt there will be complexities that get lost on an international audience, despite how America centric our world has become. To deny this would be silly, we don’t grow up being trained in school how to deal with shooters, or live in fear while we learn. However, what I find deeply fascinating is how unprovoked I was to this twist, how normal it felt, and how that normality scared me.

It speaks to the ever desensitising of society, particularly young people, people growing up with a centralised platform like the internet. It’s not out of the ordinary to have somebody like Emma slip into these dark thoughts. - well side note the choice to make Emma a black woman is certainly interesting… given the lack of people with her profile who have committed crimes like this, like truly there’s only been a single person and they were accompanied by a white man, that is a whole other conversation though that the film seeks to not touch, or it does? I think it does affect some choices and especially audience perception of the film.

The film wants to tackle this idea of what is worse: Doing a medium bad thing or thinking of doing a bad bad thing? But I actually don’t think the film is about this at all. The film isn’t particularly concerned with Emma. She has reckoned with what she’s done and instead the film is Charlie’s and his contention with his ability to understand and move past what he’s learned about his lover.

The whole film has this pressure cooker of a timer, the wedding. It’s genius because not only does Charlie have to reckon with his feelings but he’s forced to accelerate that process. One does question whether or not he would’ve had any issue at all with Emma, were it not for the starting scenario. Placing Charlie in a position where he is surrounded by others is particularly important because it seems as if he’s more concerned with their thoughts than how he feels. So not only is he trying to understand himself what’s happened, but he’s also trying to justify his love and his decisions while not even sure he believes them himself.

It makes for such a brilliant character study, I truly was enamoured by Charlie and his choices and this might just be my favourite Pattinson performance.

I love how the entire thesis of the film attempts to explore the idea of starting over, and who that is granted to. Most of the characters in the film remain static from their “bad thing”. They have yet to work through that bad part of themselves, and it manifests itself in their actions, this is especially clear in Mike and Rachel. Yet these people are granted a second chance.

On the other hand, Emma has worked through her darkness. Her upbringing, and modern life in general is a breeding ground for individualism, it’s an explanation for why she had the thoughts she did, but not the sole reason - of which I don’t think there is. However, she worked through it, and found that community was the antidote. 

The whole film hinging on the idea that we require justifications for actions and that if we don’t have this then something’s wrong is decidedly an issue with moral reasoning, something we’ve become obsessed with. We must have explanations for a world which is chaos, it’s contradictory. Similarly wanting clear lines, knowing when they’ve been crossed will never get us anywhere. At the end of the day the only people whose thoughts matter are the people you love, if they’re willing to see past the darkness and allow for a second chance. 

People will never know the whole story of anybody else, so why concern yourself with their thoughts, maybe the scary thing is that we will never truly understand the people we love, but if we truly love them and know them as best as we can, maybe it’s on us to grant second chances, even if the world says otherwise

Oh, yikes. What a major miss, too many jokes, hardly any land. Too many characters, very few compelling. Underneath the racism, there's some interesting direction, specifically the use of miniatures is incredible. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 8

Review: I mean... does it get better than this? Just a blast from the moment it starts. I maintain that this is up there with the best car chases ever, but now that I’ve seen Spielberg's work prior to this, it's unsurprising, he's so good at car chases. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 9

Name: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial
Review: Such a deceptively simple story, but it manages to feel so grand and sweeping - in part thanks to Williams' incredible score - seriously think this is an underrated work of his. Clearly the precursor to a lot of modern-day pop culture and it's understandable, perhaps a perfect family film. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 10

Name: Twilight Zone: The Movie
Review: Was completely unaware of this history of this film until I researched it, and it explains why the first couple of parts feel off. It feels weird reviewing a film like this; it's fine, not something I would revisit really. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 11

Name: All About Eve
Review: I find it so fascinating the more I explore older cinema that the complexity of the characters who had stories told about them, the people who live in the grey and aren't so easily categorised into small boxes, were around in abundance. Not only that, but the depth afforded to female characters far exceeds the sort of richness we get in modern-day female characters (broad statement but largely true). It allows its female characters to be vengeful, messy, cruel, and mean, and that results in some brilliant dynamics between the leads. Stellar performances from Bette Davis and Anne Baxter. Really loved this; they don't make screenplays like this anymore. Watching the Letterboxd Top 100: No. 99

Name: Billie Eilish — Hit Me Hard and Soft: The Tour (Live in 3D)
Review:
I've said it umpteen times, but I hate the musical biopic. Too often I find that genre reductive. Its portrayal of these larger-than-life stars always ends up falling short of the magnetism and charisma that they often possess to uphold the position that they do. To capture the hearts and minds of many.

It is why I love a concert film. These films aren't concerned with the play-by-play of a person's life, trying to whittle down a human being's story into a couple of hours. Instead, it focuses on the few hours that they spend on stage, and in this realness, the product is often more intimate. 

The film doesn't have to be concerned with re-creating what made these artists so special, what made them connect with hundreds of thousands. That energy is already there. You can see why they are so special, why so many people are moved by the artistry on offer. That is why, in biopics, when these concert scenes are depicted, they feel empty, as they will never capture the hold that the artist really has over the people in the crowd. 

It seems as though both Eilish and Cameron are aware of this fact. Not only is this an intimate portrayal of Billie Eilish herself (by the nature of the production of her concert, it would be, as it's simply her for the most part on stage), but also in how the film chooses to be a sweeping portrayal of the venue itself. You feel a part of the crowd. The crowd is as important to this film as Eilish. 

It takes a moment to get used to this, to be intercutting so much between fans screaming their hearts out and then back to Eilish in a concert movie. In lesser concert movies, this could be seen as emotionally manipulative, where the film shows you how emotionally provoked audience members are and thus implies you should feel the same way. However, that's not the case here. It is more so an act of immersion, to make you feel what it's like to be in that room and specifically what it's like for Eilish to be in that room. 

Once you settle into how this film operates in immersing you in that space, with stellar sound design that again uses the crowd's effects as much as Billie's vocals to make you feel as if you're there, you begin to understand why this film was constructed this way. The interstitials, learning about Billie's process and her creative insight into how she wants the production to feel, are very clear, more so than most artists of her size, about how much she cares about the concert experience and the individual fan experience. 

I've personally always had a lot of adoration for Eilish. She's a stellar musician, and also her approach to creativity I've always found kind of inspiring. If you're reading this, I highly recommend you check out the doc that she made, called The World's a Little Blurry. It's one of my favourite films of that year. She just seems like a genuine person who cares about her artistry. Not only is that coming through in the concert itself, as an avid concertgoer, I've never seen a crowd that participatory on a scale this size, and that's even more impressive given that she's the only person on stage for most of the time. It's also in the way that she chooses to construct the visual language around her art. 

She cares about the overall experience that a person is getting, from the concerts to her videos to her music. Even to the way that she styles herself, which is touched upon in the film, she is in the interest of doing what makes her happy, not trying to appease the masses but instead doing what is right to her. It's all coming from a genuine place, and that even comes through in this film, making the viewer at home feel as if they are a part of that experience. 

Obviously, I was even more excited with James Cameron being attached to this. The work that was put into the technology that they developed specifically for this film is insane. The rigs that they are able to use, being so small and getting 3D out of them, is wild. It's probably up there with the best compositions of a concert film I've ever seen.

Obviously, with it happening live, there's very little room for error. Everything has to be very precise, and the cameras also have to be placed in a way that isn't going to interrupt the concert experience. It all just looked seamless. Eilish and Cameron killed it with this, a truly special immersive experience, and has made me more of a fan of them.

Side note: Eilish might actually be the most beautiful person I've ever seen. There are times where she will look into the camera and I was transfixed by her eyes omg

Name: The Sheep Detectives
Review:
Ok, colour me shocked, but the movie featuring sheep as detectives is actually good. I was told this, I didn’t believe it. The critics said this, I didn’t believe it. This is a reminder, sometimes the masses aren’t completely wrong.

After a shaky start, this thing really finds its footing, and I would go as far as to say it ends up being up there with the best family films to come out in years. Choked full of really lovely, touching, and heavy themes as well, which work perfectly for children, but also for the whole family. I cried multiple times.

It’s a little overwritten in areas, specifically the start has far too many explanations of tropes, but I will say as somebody who has prided himself on figuring out all of the Knives Out movies, I was surprised by the twist, so… funny, emotional, and has some truly wonderful performances.

Name: The Devil Wears Prada 2
Review: If you're a visual learner, click here. Welp. Not sure I've ever sat on a movie for as long as I have with this. I'm seriously conflicted about how I feel; the following is where I'm at currently, but I will be seeing this again to get a better grasp of how I feel. "You're not a visionary, you're a vendor." This line from Miranda Priestly has, quite rightfully, gone a little viral since the release of this film. It's emblematic of the thesis of this film, the grasp that the hands of capitalism have in print media and journalism as an industry which is, and has been, facing ever-shifting changes in the digital age. These changes often come, not out of a love for journalism, but as survival to adapt to this new media landscape, a landscape which is driven by tech bros who have no interest in journalistic integrity and providing the best for their readers/consumers. Instead, the bottom line is how much money they're able to squeeze out of these legacy institutions, which often comes at the expense of hard-working individuals. And while the film is getting praise, quite rightly, for opting to tell a story with substance, a story that is hardly seen in a film of this size, let alone a legacy sequel with something to say— a rarity in modern-day cinema. I'm not sure it's really saying anything; instead, much like the quip from Miranda, it's suggesting a problem which is there, letting that quip do the talking instead of investigating it further. It feels like a grabby headline, rather than a substantial article. The medium is half the message a lot of the time, and I couldn't help but feel this stench of capitalism all over this film. This is certainly an important topic to explore, and interogate, but is this legacy sequel from the House of Mouse, whose sole purpose is to milk as much money as possible from the nostalgia-infected population, really the right vehicle to explore this? It even feels ironic in the reality of the film: is the quote-unquote Vogue dupe really meant to be the shining beacon of journalism, a publication which has been plagued with issues in our world of perpetuating unrealistic body standards and is a capitalist machine in its own regard? (Not a read specifically of Vogue but more fashion journalism in general). Pair this with the copious amounts of Diet Coke in every scene - sounds like hyperbole, but try finding a scene without a perfectly placed silver can - you get a product that feels disingenuous in its messaging, and this is before you even unpack the script. Once you are able to accept the ridiculous way that our core cast is reunited in what is an abysmal opening 20 minutes set to every it girl's summer playlist, the film actually begins to set out its thesis statement. "What is the role of Runway in the digital age?" Keep this in mind. The film lays out all of the problems facing a print publication in the modern day. The need for engagement translating to short-form disposable content, far from the thoughtful investigations and articles on which the institution was built, the magazine is no longer a book bought in stores; the physical magazine is perhaps the least important part of Runway. Instead, Runway has to be on every platform possible, optimising each for the most amount of engagement. It's a sinking ship trying to keep up with the modern world by endlessly producing content. It's clear from all of this set-up though, that the current way of running the magazine will only last so long. Change is necessary... The film seems averse to change, to explore new territory, and this is even felt in its characters. The new characters who are introduced are an afterthought at best; they serve the role that they must for the plot. Miranda would still have a pair of assistants, and so she does. What are their names, their dreams, desires? Why do they work for her... irrelevant. The film is not concerned with expanding out the world around our core cast. Which is detrimental for the film. Instead of feeling lived in and an evolution of Runway, it feels empty and hollow. This is further exacerbated by the inclusion of references, which we must have as this is a legacy sequel after all. Some are handled tastefully and actually build up the world the film takes place in, particularly the idea that the Spring fashion show which opens the show is floral-themed. This is a blink and you'll miss it reference, but it informs the state of Runway; in this desperation for engagement they've reverted to something very safe, something Miranda herself had previously mocked. The vast majority are just cheap tricks, look, Andy spotted some belts! She’s wearing cerulean! But now it's stylish. By far the biggest offender of this refusal to change, however, is in its treatment of its returning cast. Most of whom get very simplistic or non-existent character arcs; in fact, some of them feel like they have failed to change in the time between the first film and where they are now. While I enjoy Miranda having to navigate this new working world, where her old style is deemed inappropriate, does this have any consequences other than a cheeky line or two? Her actions throughout the years have affected people; you only need to look so far as the people closest to her to see this. Nigel had this blind loyalty to his job at the beginning of the first film, and he remains this way years later. He has failed to evolve, or we are not privy to the conflict inside him. His mistreatment by Miranda not only happened in the first film but it's alluded to that this continued to happen, so why did he stay at Runway? Why is there no pushback to how he was treated? It takes Andy to suggest to Miranda that Nigel wants more, to show her how loyal he is, and that story is immediately resolved. It weirdly absolves Miranda of her misgivings. Which is a primary issue I have with this film. It's pretty definitive that Emily is framed as the opposition in the film, the villainous person who seeks to destroy Runway, but is she really in the wrong? After years of being mistreated by Miranda and having nothing to show for it, she finds a way to seek out the recognition she thinks she deserves, and she does that through what she's learned is perhaps the only way to climb, capitalism. Emily is a product of the capitalist nightmare she was stuck working in. She had blind loyalty to Miranda, and it led her nowhere. She ends up working in retail, albeit a good position but clearly not what she wanted. I find this dichotomy between Nigel and Emily strange when viewed through the lens of devotion to Miranda. Nigel, who didn't complain for years, who had the quiet blind loyalty to her, ends up receiving the recognition. Meanwhile, Emily, somebody who tried to move up the ladder without the permission of her tyrannical boss, is punished? I think there's something buried underneath this that speaks to how power is gatekept from people, and the effects that has on the system of capitalism, but the film isn't concerned with this as it would require framing Miranda as the villain. The original, while it doesn't fully reject the idea of having devotion to your boss, it does interrogate it. Andy realises that Miranda's behaviours were inappropriate, yet Andy's devotion to her also helped her land her next job. Speaking of Andy, as much as I love Anne Hathaway and truly think she's one of the best working today, the way she just slipped back into character was a wonder to behold. As far as Andy goes, the film leaves a lot to be desired. What is Andy's arc, or does she even have an arc? What does she learn in the process of this film? That journalism might be a sinking ship, but climbing aboard whatever Miranda is on is perhaps a good business move. The film doesn’t exactly frame it this way, but isn't that what happens? She's so absent of any conflict in the film that they have to manufacture a boyfriend out of thin air to give her any sort of scenes with tension, which is so superfulous. The most we get outside of that is her having to make a lot of phone calls in a montage, like surely there’s something more interesting than giving a single older woman a love interest because god forbid she be single. She is the reason the film reaches the finale it does though... Let's go back to that quote I had earlier. The central question of the film is: "What is the role of Runway in the digital age?" The magazine had been struggling to figure out its voice and relevancy for a while and is beholden to higher-ups who seek to drain it for all the money they're able to. This only becomes more problematic when the magazine is under the oversight of a tech-bro billionaire. So what is the solution this film proposes... ah yes, to go ask the good female-POC billionaire instead to own the company as she will let us do what we want... I genuinely think this is laughably bad. The film spends a lengthy amount of time demonising the billionaires and the lack of care they have for the institution Miranda has helped make into this empire. They lament about the dangers of being beholden to these people who would sooner drop the company if it was beneficial to them, but this billionaire, she's different????? Why? This ending undoes so much of the commentary the film made, by completely turning its back on it for a glossy, happy ending. Sure, there is a throwaway line where Miranda expresses that Runway isn't saved; it's only saved for now. But the ending would have you thinking differently. As the film pans out to show Miranda, Andy, and Nigel running Runway just as Miranda wanted. Oh wait, the central question of the movie, the idea that things have to change and evolve in order for the magazine to survive. Oh, that idea is abandoned, in favour of a neat ending. What kind of message is this really signalling to people about the role of people like Miranda, and contrast that with the treatment of Emily in the film; I truly find this so backwards. The ending of the original showcased how ruthless it was to work under Miranda; she demanded all of your time and for you to sacrifice your personal life in order to achieve greatness. This film says you really may have it all as long as you cling to the right billionaire. I will say as a huge tie guy, I absolutely adored the fashion in this. I want to own every single thing that Andy wears in this movie.

Name: The Color Purple
Review: Was Spielberg the right director to make this movie? Perhaps not. This leans into his overly sentimental side quite a bit, and there is a bit of an abrasion with the story that's being told. However, as far as this being a left-field switch-up in his filmography, this is a solid drama with spectacular performances. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 13

Name: Empire of the Sun
Review: Completely underrated in Spielberg's filmography, to the point that I hadn't heard of this movie before this endeavour. It's criminal that this isn't mentioned in the same breath as his other masterpieces. It's a profoundly evocative movie on the loss of innocence. The dichotomy of childlike wonder versus the realities of war makes for an utterly fascinating character study, only made better by a young Christian Bale's performance being utterly mesmerising. It's an incredibly fine line to walk to still feel the harshness of the situation, yet understand it through a child's eyes, and I think Spielberg nails it. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 11

Name: Always
Review: So.... this is certainly a film. What's baffling about this is it's a remake? I have no idea what has to do through somebody's brain to make them think this story deserves another film. The screenplay is bizarre but there's some solid direction, Spielberg continues to show off how good he is at shooting scenes involving vehicles. Also, the first half is kinda fun. Who doesn't love Holly Hunter playing a Holly Hunter type role? Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 16

Name: Inglourious Basterds
Review: I was super excited to revisit this after some time. I somewhat maintain my slight issue with this movie: that there is not enough characterization or depth given to the Basterds themselves. However, I think I was hung up on that a little bit more than I should have been, because the rest of the movie is so strong. The extended dialogue sequences in this movie are just phenomenal. Mélanie Laurent is spectacular as well. I thought this the first time around, but every single scene she steals. It's rarely through dialogue either, it's her mannerisms and slight expressions that just are so effortlessly real Watching the Letterboxd Top 100: No. 98

Name: Hook
Review: It's not without its fair share of issues, with the runtime being the primary culprit. However, there is this joy and whimsy and that signature childhood magic that Spielberg bakes into this story, which is often really hard to pull off. It's very hard to walk away from this movie with a massive smile on your face Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 17

Name: The Devil Wears Prada 2
Review: Rewatched for my lengthy video review click here Plagued with all the issues I believed it had, offers up a compelling story and then does nothing with it, oh and then goes back on its message?

Name: Dua Lipa: Live From Mexico
Review:
Always going to be a defender of the concert film. Mad props to Dua for just dropping this for free on YouTube. However, as somebody who loves a good concert movie, I find this a bit all over the place. 

The direction felt as if they just placed cameras all over the venue and then decided to make the most horrible edit out of it, cutting with no real rhyme or reason and cutting so frequently it took out all of the atmosphere and sense of scale from the film.

It's a shame coming off the back of the Billie Eilish and James Cameron collab but I thought this was quite weak.

Name: Finding Emily
Review:
I'm not a massive fan of the rom-com genre. 

Now I actually don't think that statement is particularly true; however, it rings true for me personally when I look at the state of the rom-com over the last few years. The genre has become disposable and has been the primary victim of streaming slop.  
  
Finding Emily was a reminder that this genre can be more than that. What's incredibly smart about the film is it isn't reinventing the genre; it's not doing anything particularly surprising. However, it's taken the well-worn familiarity of the heyday of rom-coms and modernised it for this era. 

The movie feels profoundly Gen Z, and I mean that in the most complimentary way. The way characters spoke, the way that they approached this scenario, the integration of technology (which is up there with the best integration of the internet I've seen in a film). It would be so easy to set this film in the past because it makes its narrative far easier but dealing with it in the present makes for a far more enjoyable experience because it's seldom we get stories like this.
  
The leads are super compelling, they feel so naturalistic and they've got great chemistry with each other. I could go on and on about different things, but at the end of the day I think for a rom-com to work it just needs to be comprised of two elements (as stupid as this sounds). It needs to have good comedy and good romance, and this had both in buckets full

Name: Schindler's List
Review: As I have been going through the filmography of one of the most acclaimed film directors, I feel like I know what I'm going into a lot of the times. This blew me away. This isn't a film I believed Spielberg could make. This isn't to say he's not a good director. He is, but this is so distinctly different from everything he's made. There are obviously elements of him still infused in this. There's a sentimentality and love in places that you can find in his other films, but it's also such a drastic shift from the stories that he'd previously told. And not only that, but the way he goes about composing the story. It sounds silly to say that I was shocked, but I truly was. Interestingly, I feel as if this movie is in conversation with his previous pieces. For example, the red coat. I was sure it stood for a certain thing that would feel in line with the Spielberg that I have seen in his previous work. When that turns out not to be the case, not only is there a punch in the gut for what happens but also for Spielberg to abandon his usual tendencies. It hurts that much more. A profoundly moving experience, and a stark reminder of the horrors that humanity is capable of. Schindler is perhaps the most complex and interesting character that Spielberg has in his filmography thus far, I was truly enamoured by the depth afforded to him, and the performance of Liam Neeson, just incredible. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 19

Name: Backrooms
Review: Embargoed

Name: The Punisher: One Last Kill
Review:
This is the first time, to me at least, that the MCU has put out content. There's a solid argument to be made that some of the Disney+ shows started this trend, but I'd argue that at least within them they had some compelling character arcs. Even if the story was nonsensical and inconsequential, it still gave us character exploration. 

In this special, it feels like half of it is to catch people up on who he is if they've not seen his show, and the other half is an extended action sequence, albeit a well-choreographed action sequence, that is jarring.

This New York is not the New York we've come to know in the MCU, where people are rioting in the streets and Frank is able to kill people in broad daylight with no repercussions.

Its villain is paper-thin. The gratuitous violence felt unearned and simply to shock people. Worst of all, this special ends up right back where it started, so it feels meaningless. So maybe this isn't the worst thing the MCU's made, but it's the most inconsequential.

Name: Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom
Review: If you thought this wasn't as racist as you remembered, think again. My favourite thing I've learned about Spielberg films so far, though, is that the screenwriters were hired specifically for their knowledge of India, and yet when presenting India with the script, they were denied permission to film there because of how offensive the script was. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 12

Name: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu
Review:
I'm flirting with this being the worst Star Wars movie ever made. 

Let me lay out how every scene in this film functions:

Side character "Here is the plot, and this is what you must do."
Mando: "I must do this plot. This is the way"
(cut to Grogu doing something cute)
Mando: "Grogu come on, we must do this plot."

The screenplay for this movie is lacking in nearly every way imaginable. It lacks the propulsive energy of an action-adventure movie. It lacks the intrigue and steelyness you would find in a Western. Worst of all it lacks the magic that even the worst of Star Wars movies are able to muster. 

It feels, from the jump, as if it's dragging its feet to get towards the end. We needlessly jump from plot point to plot point because we must. There's no depth to anything that's going on. It's no wonder people are walking away from this feeling as if this was serialised TV shaped into a movie, despite it always being conceived as a film. 

It functions like multiple episodes of TV stitched together. Large plot points have resolved, with small threads lingering that can lead to the next set piece. We needlessly repeat this cycle until the film ends. 

The moments where the film shines, which is very seldom, are when it breaks out of this formula. There is a small section where we follow Grogu trying to look after his father, and that relies on the visual storytelling to communicate the fear that he has to the audience. It's a rare moment in this movie that lets the film sit with what's happening. 

It's a rarity as the film is so determined to reach its conclusion that it allows no room for any scene to breathe, to deal with character interiority, to poke and prod and marinate in what's happening.

Perhaps that's because there's nothing to interrogate. This film feels like it knows itself how disposable it is at its core. It's a random adventure for the sake of capitalism. 

What are the character arcs for literally any character in this film? The final frame of this movie could have been the start of this movie, and it wouldn't have changed anything about The Mandalorian and Grogu. Which begs the question: who was this film made for? 

If this is meant to be a continuation of the people who've poured years into watching The Mandalorian and are invested in his character, this surely is a disappointment, as it has no real connection to the show other than the characters. 

If this is meant to introduce new viewers to the world of the Mandalorian, then surely this is a disappointment as well. You follow a nameless bounty hunter surrounded by lifeless side characters, who might I add are phoning in their performances. I'm looking at you, Sigourney Weaver. He's joined by a cute puppet who does cute things, but that's it. Surely you want more out of cinema.

Save a few action sequences, some great puppeteering work and a score from Ludwig Gorranson that is far better than the film deserves. I found myself so bored.

Star Wars on the big screen used to mean something. Even the sequel trilogy, which has its issues, still elicited that feeling: those shivers that you get seeing Star Wars. This, and the spectacle it could be, failed to give me that feeling, except from hearing Ludwig's score through massive speakers.

Star Wars should feel like a cinematic spectacle. The love that is poured into making these movies, the talent that is behind them, it should feel monumental. We shouldn't be accepting disposable filler content like this that feels like it only exists for money. 

It sadly commits the biggest cinema sin, in my opinion, in that it just feels forgettable. At least the "bad" Star Wars movies feel like they have a vision and passion behind it. This isn't the way.

Name: Amistad
Review: There are elements that really flourish here, particularly everything involving the slave trade journey, but it is surrounded by a laborious and repetitive legal drama that isn’t all that compelling and seems to simplify the real-life events. Some scenes truly shine, like the opening, and it's full of incredible performances, but I did find this quite weak. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 18

Name: Saving Private Ryan
Review: Many people cite the opening scene as the greatest war scene ever put to film, and while I don't dispute that, I truly believe the climax at the bridge is this film's standout. After journeying with the soldiers for a while and contending with their internal struggles, it makes those final sequences even more brutal. The complexities in the soldiers’ mentality seen across the entire film and how that manifests in this sequence is truly brilliant. The slow draining of humanity replaced by the brutality and reality of war. The burdens of being complicit in the violence, of abstaining from the violence, of seeking out violence, it all washes away in the end; war is war. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 22

Name: Masters of the Universe
Review:
Just a diabolical mess. Like everything I hate about modern blockbuster filmmaking wrapped into a green screen mess.

I totally understand why people will vibe with this, namely those who enjoy modern blockbusters, but I was really not enjoying myself.

The painful jokes, the horrible delivery, the lack of any sort of interiority in the characters, the mind-numbingly paint by numbers screenplay, the really weak performances and to top it all off I was just so bored watching this, the biggest sin.

Credit where it’s due some of the action was inventive, loved the sheer force and impact you could feel in each fight, but when the best part of your film is Jared Leto maybe there’s some problems

Name: Power Ballad
Review: Just an extremely palatable movie. Nice comedy, some good emotional beats. It's also digging at something quite relevant with its commentary on what makes an artist and who gets to pursue art as their job, is it the most talented, is that the purpose of art? Just a shame it undoes all of that in its ending.

Name: Paris, Texas
Review:
Love reading old reviews of films and walking away with such different opinions, in regards to this I was unsatisfied with the ambiguity of the ending, this time I think that's what makes the film genius. We don't know if Travis inserting himself into Hunter's life is yet again bringing about destruction and the stability he'd managed to get, or if he's going to finally live in the home he should've.

Yet again like last time, I think this film has two or even three of the greatest scenes put to film. Gorgeous film with such careful performances, truly up there with the best in cinema

Name: Cloudy with a Chance of Meatballs
Review: I did an eat along to this movie... if you thought that was a bad idea you might be right as I felt like I was gonna throw up when this film ended. God I love this silly stupid movie

Name: Backrooms
Review:
Still don’t think this quite meshes the liminal horror with the deeper storytelling and character work as the film progresses. The horror wore off a little on rewatch (minus that great found footage sequence) and when we’re not dealing with the horror sequences I found myself really tuning out, Renate Reinsve monologuing about patterns doesn’t really hit when you know where the film ends up.

I will say I saw Kevin McCarthy ask Kane Parsons about the relationship between the backrooms and OCD, which really resonated with me. I really do love the distortion of memory and what the backrooms represents, I just don’t feel like the film showcases that to the audience in a way that does service to the depth he clearly gets from this. The narrative which is explored in the film is far less interesting than any of the ambiguity which is obtained from his less conclusive ideas

Name: Obsession
Review:
Loved this, and love this mini YouTuber rise in Hollywood. The horror in this film is genius. It’s not cheap or exploitative, instead the horror is found in good old filmmaking, and storytelling.

Sharp edits, fascinating setup and an atmosphere which perfectly captures the feelings of our main characters.

Yes, in some areas, this screenplay feels very simplistic and certainly made by a man of his age, but Barker has such a strong vision for this movie it’s hard to not get wrapped up in awe over what he’s able to achieve and elicit within you as this film reaches its climax.

Stellar acting from both Inde Naverette and Michael Johnston, whom I really loved with his more nuanced performance. Loved its soft commentary on modern dating and the world we’ve constructed breeding these types of men who on the surface mean no harm but are deeply insecure individuals. Fantastic final third as well.

Name: Catch Me If You Can
Review: Just dripping in charisma and oozing with style, the perfect concoction of a young Leo and a Spielberg who is really loose and free with the camera. It's so light on its feet. Spielberg manages to integrate his exploration of fatherhood without being saccharine or overly sentimental, as he sometimes does, and weaves it in so skilfully Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 25

Name: A.I. Artificial Intelligence
Review: Really not a massive fan of this first act, some shaky acting and stilted performances, but then sex worker Jude Law appears and the film kicks into motion. Where it climaxes to perhaps Spielberg's darkest, and best?, ending. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 23

Name: Minority Report
Review: I never expected a Spielberg movie in its opening minutes to have a line like "the twink from the fed". I adored this, some of his best character work and world-building mixed with a really fun mystery and a hot Colin Farrell Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 24

Name: Sorcerer
Review:
Walked into this with no idea what I was watching, and what a journey this is. Slowly being introduced to four random characters, having no idea what links them together, and building to that painstaking drive of the vans.

Very much like Sīrat in its tension building, utilising precise filmmaking to make the simplicity of a van making a treacherous journey feel so large. It’s absolutely gorgeous in its murkiness, the grit and realism of watching these men throw all that they have at this mission. Still a little in awe of what I watched, like how did they make this? Truly filmmaking and tension building at its most wonderful

Name: The Terminal
Review: Oh Steven really sentimentalmaxxed with this Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 26

Name: War of the Worlds
Review: Gosh, two acts of this are some of the best alien invasion media put to screen, but the vitality and propulsive energy goes away in its third act, which instead loses the energy from the start. It's strange given that the film follows the book, but it reaches a very Spielberg ending which feels at odds with the rest of the film. There are true scenes of horror in here that really elicit how horrifying this would be; it's a real shame it doesn't completely follow through. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 27

Name: Munich
Review: Many things to say about this film, the political complexity and its portrayal amongst them, but what I will say is that this is perhaps my favourite Spielberg ending; it has remained in my brain ever since I finished the film. So profound it made me rethink everything that led up to that scene. There's still some issues with the screenplay but it’s undeniable the sheer magic of the filmmaking on show, perhaps Spielberg's best looking film thanks to Janusz Kamiński Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 27

Name: Disclosure Day
Review: Not allowed to disclose my thoughts

Name: Confessions II – The Film
Review: Wild. Very unfamiliar with Madonna as an artist, but this was far more out there and avant-garde than I was expecting from a major pop star. I was so taken aback by many elements of this but it created a world I was interested in, really wasn't expecting to get jump scared by pussy lasers

Name: The Adventures of Tintin
Review: Oh, how sad I am that there aren't more Spielberg animated movies, and that he never worked with these screenwriters again because this movie is capital F fun. Low-key the best Uncharted film we have. Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 30

Name: War Horse
Review: Really found this a bit of a drag. There are some wonderfully directed set pieces - but I mean, who is surprised this is Spielberg. The constant switching of perspectives, though, did this film a disservice. The tone is all over the place and meshed with his classic sentimentality; it fails to do justice to the stories. It fails to fully sustain a gallop and instead feels like a slow trot for most of the length Director Deep Dives: Steven Spielberg Episode 31

Name: Lincoln
Review: Possibly the most insane group of actors ever assembled for a film, ever? Maybe my hottest Spielberg take, but I really didn't vibe with this. Specifically in the performances, they just felt so character-ish and like they were playing characters, very much a capital A acting movie, and I just found myself so removed from the story

Name: Bridge of Spies
Review: Solid direction. Solid performances. Solid script. Solid. Quite literally what you expect from a movie like this

Name: The BFG
Review: In The BFG Spielberg treats Britain to the same sentimentality and nationalism he's given to America throughout his career...

Name: Toy Story
Review: Truly mental how this was our first foray into 3D animation and it already feels so well realised. Not only does the variation in the toys make for such unique animation, but how it introduces us to this world so quickly is genius. You get a sense for so many characters, which forms the basis of the entire series in an instant: the petty bitch that is Mr Potato Head, the snarky Ham, and the lovable Rex. These side characters are barely in the film, but they’re so important in colouring this film

Name: Toy Story 2
Review: I fear I might be part of the “this is the best in the series” gang. A ridiculously good genre film that climaxes in the best sequence in the series?
